item.title: Message Box Pro for Your Team
item.description: A quick note for anyone who works in politics or communications for a living: I’m rolling out group subscriptions for Message Box Pro — my consulting service for candidates, campaign staffers, and political operatives. If your team, office, or firm signs up together, everyone gets 30% off the…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
A quick note for anyone who works in politics or communications for a living:

I’m rolling out group subscriptions for Message Box Pro — my consulting service for candidates, campaign staffers, and political operatives.

If your team, office, or firm signs up together, everyone gets 30% off the individual rate. That means $45 per person, per month (with a two-seat minimum), versus $65/month for individual subscriptions.

Message Box Pro members get weekly strategy memos, polling analysis, messaging guidance, and access to a growing community of operatives, organizers, and candidates on Discord. Since launching a few months ago, it’s grown into a real professional network with congressional staff, statewide campaign teams, communications professionals, and grassroots organizers all in the mix. I’m on Discord every day, answering questions and sharing insights with the subscribers.

The group rate is designed specifically for:

  • Communications and consulting firms

  • Think tanks

  • Advocacy organizations

  • Campaign teams

I’m biased, of course, but I think Message Box Pro can be a huge asset for communications shops on Capitol Hill or in state capitols around the country.

If that’s you or your team, I hope you’ll consider giving it a try.

Questions? Reply to this email — I read every one.

Dan

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post.

item.title: Trump's Stunning Admission About CNN
item.description: It didn’t get very much attention, but on Sunday, Trump made a stunning admission. On Sunday morning, Trump reacted to the death of Lindsey Graham in a way that only Trump could or would. He called into the Sunday shows to talk about Graham. Trump heard about the death of a close political ally and…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
It didn’t get very much attention, but on Sunday, Trump made a stunning admission.

On Sunday morning, Trump reacted to the death of Lindsey Graham in a way that only Trump could or would. He called into the Sunday shows to talk about Graham. Trump heard about the death of a close political ally and frequent golfing partner and saw an opportunity to get more attention for himself. In some ways, it was a fitting tribute. Graham was a mainstay on the Sunday shows (despite being wrong about nearly every consequential foreign policy issue in the 21st century) and was preparing for what was supposed to be his 64th appearance on Meet the Press when he died.

Either the White House or Trump himself told the Sunday shows that Trump only wanted to talk about Graham and wouldn’t address other issues. To be clear, this is a request, not a command, and the hosts had no obligation to follow through. In fact, they did try to get Trump to talk about some other issues, which led to this stunning exchange with Jake Tapper.

TAPPER: I know you don't want to talk about any other issues out of respect for Lindsey Graham, but we would love to have you back sometime bc I do have a lot of other questions TRUMP: Sure. We're trying to have CNN go on a normal path T: I'm on a normal path right here, sir TRUMP: Good. You are.

Sun, 12 Jul 2026 14:22:09 GMT

Think about this for a minute. Trump says “WE are trying to get CNN to go down a normal path.” He is admitting on live television that the FCC’s approval of Paramount Skydance’s acquisition was part of a corrupt plan to make CNN more MAGA-friendly.

Now this isn’t shocking. David Ellison, the pro-Trump billionaire who owns Paramount, reportedly made promises to Trump to change CNN’s news coverage and on-air lineup. And this is exactly what Ellison did when he acquired CBS News. He installed Bari Weiss, a conservative opinion journalist with no TV news experience, to oversee the network. Weiss and Ellison immediately began gutting 60 Minutes, the news program that has been most at odds with Trump over the years.

It seems like CNN is the next media outlet in line for a billionaire-funded MAGA makeover.

Trump’s blatant admission shows just how aggressive the right is being to remake the media in their image. It’s a warning sign for democracy, and it’s a call to arms for Democrats to get their shit together and start fighting fire with fire.

Democrats tend to think politics is a contest of ideas, or a contest to see who can knock on the most doors by election day. That may have been true at one time, but today’s politics is information warfare, pure and simple.

To be fair, Democrats have gotten better at the tactics of the new politics. They are going on more podcasts, talking to more influencers, and spending more time on the platforms that matter, like YouTube and TikTok.

But let’s be honest: Democrats are massively outgunned.

Pro-Trump billionaires currently own Fox News, CBS News, CNN, the Wall Street Journal, Roku, the Washington Post, and a huge swath of local television and radio stations. More alarmingly, these MAGA billionaires control the platforms that distribute news, including Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Twitter, and TikTok.

I could write a book about this (I kind of did). The Democratic donor community and party leadership should stop putting all their money into TV, which reaches fewer people every day and is ignored by almost everyone under 40. Instead, they should invest in long-term media infrastructure that can deliver our message on our own terms. We’ve made some progress, and the pro-democracy side now has outlets like Crooked Media, Courier News, Meidas Touch, and others. But those examples are still too rare, and the number of donors willing to really invest in pro-democracy media keeps shrinking.

I don’t know how many billionaires or trillionaires (hi, Elon!) subscribe to Message Box, but if any of you do, I would be happy to sit down and talk through how and why we need to start building an arsenal to compete in the information wars.

But for every non-billionaire reading this today, I encourage you to support independent, pro-democracy media. Subscribe if you can, but if you can’t, your attention is an invaluable resource. Watch, read, share, follow. The more traffic the good guys get, the more the algorithms will share that content.

I have been arguing for Democrats to change their playbook for a decade. Six years ago, I put my money where my mouth was and launched Message Box to be a counter-balance to the both-sides, misinformed political analysis that has dominated the Trump era, and to provide people with real, actionable advice on how to fight back against MAGA. I’m not saying my newsletter is going to move mountains, but I do think it has value. I am so grateful to all of the paid subscribers who make Message Box possible. If you are not yet a paid subscriber, I hope you will consider supporting my efforts. If you sign up today, you get 20% off the subscription price for a year.

Thank you so much for reading and being part of this amazing community.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post.

item.title: How Democrats Can Still Beat Susan Collins
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. It was a pretty messy, dispiriting week in politics. Obviously, Graham Platner’s demise and the ensuing chaotic and crowded campaign to replace him on the ballot have dominated the political discussion and the questions submitted this week. In case you missed it, I…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. It was a pretty messy, dispiriting week in politics. Obviously, Graham Platner’s demise and the ensuing chaotic and crowded campaign to replace him on the ballot have dominated the political discussion and the questions submitted this week. In case you missed it, I wrote about why I was supportive of Platner and what I missed. I also had an extensive conversation with Alex Wagner about it on the most recent episode of Pod Save America. Today I’ll answer more questions about Maine and other stuff.

As a reminder, these mailbags are for paid subscribers. If you want to get your question answered — and read my answers to everyone else’s — please consider becoming a paid subscriber.

If you’re already a subscriber, leave your question for next week in the comments below.

What are the highest priorities for a candidate to defeat Susan Collins?

As I read about the Platner mess, I can’t help but feel like we got really lucky on this one. We are losing an incredibly problematic candidate at just the right time, and the old, establishment candidate dropped out. So the Collins campaign really has to scramble to figure out how to beat Platner’s replacement. If Maine Democrats go with someone who is progressive and vetted it seems like we have a really solid chance to beat Collins. Am I on the right track?

Lots of questions this week about whether Democrats can still win Maine or whether Graham Platner ruined our chances. This is a critical question because there is no plausible path to a Senate majority without a win in Maine. Susan Collins is very hard to beat. She is a unicorn, a Republican who regularly wins a Democratic state. In fact, she is the last Republican representing a tried-and-true blue state. Prior to the sexual assault allegation but after the reports of Platner sexting women outside of his marriage, I thought Platner was a slight favorite to beat Collins — if nothing else came out.

Well, something horrifying came out. Platner is out of the race, and Democrats have two weeks to find a new candidate and mount a campaign. It seems daunting, and it is, but I think this race is still very winnable.

Here’s why and how we win it.

The reason this race is winnable is pretty simple. Maine is a very blue state. Kamala Harris won it by about 9 points. In the recent NYT/Siena poll conducted right before the last Platner revelations, Democrats led the generic ballot in Maine by 11 points. Even with all of Platner’s baggage at the time, he still led Collins by two points. He was underperforming a generic Democrat by 9 points — and still winning. There was no secret sauce in his lead. It wasn’t because he was winning over a bunch of working-class white Trump voters; in fact, he was doing no better with that group than Kamala Harris did nationally in 2024. I also think it was psychologically easier for voters who hated Trump to pull the lever for Collins because they were simultaneously casting a vote against Trump. They won’t have that option this fall.

Are things a giant mess right now? Absolutely. But this race is quite winnable with the right candidate and the right strategy.

First, Graham Platner needs to shut up and go away. His video announcing the suspension of his campaign could not have been more counterproductive. Spooling out conspiracy theories about the political establishment and corporate media concocting sexual assault allegations to stop his progressive campaign is a surefire way to tamp down turnout for Platner’s eventual replacement. He can have no say, no role, and no influence on the process. The worst possible scenario is for the new nominee to be portrayed as Platner’s handpicked successor.

Second, the process to replace Platner must be smooth, transparent, and unifying. The Maine Democratic Party is planning a convention at the end of the month where 600 delegates chosen by the county parties in Maine’s 16 counties will select the nominee. This is essentially the brokered convention scenario that gets talked about every four years. There is no perfect solution or easy answer. There is no time for another primary. As messy as this sounds, it’s almost certainly the best option, and the Maine Democratic Party deserves some credit for going this route. Having the delegates vote openly, with candidates eliminated and endorsements made, should instill confidence in the process and some unity behind the eventual winner.

Third, everyone from outside of Maine needs to stay out. The narrative of the Platner campaign is that a group of out-of-state consultants came to Maine and recruited (and created) Platner, while some coastal podcasters (ahem) and media elites fanned the flames, creating the Platner phenomenon. On the other side, DC Democrats tried to clear the field for their chosen candidate, who didn’t want to run and couldn’t win if she did. Now, that is an oversimplified story, and Platner took off because the people of Maine found him compelling. There was real energy under his campaign, and he blew the two-term incumbent governor out of the water. The DSCC, the DSA, the DC consultants, and all of the other groups should sit this one out and let Maine figure it out.

Fourth, make this race all about Susan Collins. Because of all of the positive and negative attention garnered by Platner, Susan Collins has flown under the radar. She has been nothing more than background noise in her own reelection campaign. In fact, the race had become, in some senses, a referendum on Platner, not on the senator who cast the deciding vote to overturn Roe. Every word, ad, post, etc. needs to be about Collins. This is a race against Susan Collins, not a race for whoever ends up on the ballot. Honestly, the more generic the Democrat, the better. In the Times poll, 57% of Mainers think Collins will be too supportive of Trump; that’s the thrust of the argument.

Finally, a vote for Collins is a vote for a Republican Senate. The challenge of beating Susan Collins is that lots of Democrats and Independents, voters we need in 2026, have voted for her multiple times in the past. Democrats need to create a permission structure for them to do something different this time. One argument that the Platner campaign and the Democratic Super PACs have made is that Susan Collins has changed. I am quite skeptical that approach will work. Instead, I would advise them to nationalize the election and center the campaign on the idea that a vote for Collins is a vote for a Republican Senate and for Trump appointing two more Supreme Court justices. This photo of Collins yucking it up with Trump while holding a MAGA hat should be in every ad that runs between now and election day.

While Platner was only up 2 in the poll I mentioned earlier, 53% of Mainers wanted Democrats to control the Senate. That’s a pretty damn good reason for these Democrats, Independents, and Republicans unhappy with Trump to vote for the Democrat TK in Maine.

Dan, can you address the vetting process and what went wrong? Somebody missed something with Platner. And, to my mind, Mills was/is not a good choice. I feel like we got distracted by the shiny object and not the substance behind it. And Schumer hasn’t come to grips with the reality that Democrats are ready for... well... not him and his political worldview.

The “vetting” conversation about Platner has been a little confused and detached from the reality of actually vetting a candidate. Let me stipulate that the vetting process for Platner was piss-poor malpractice. What typically happens for a Senate race is a two-part process. The first part is that a team of researchers would pore over the candidates’ finances, public statements, legal filings, and everything they have ever said, written, or posted in public. This process should have found all of Platner’s Reddit posts, and it’s a massive fuckup that they didn’t. In a presidential race, the campaign might reach out to former coworkers, roommates, and ex-boyfriends and girlfriends. Had that happened, the campaign would have known about some of the allegations coming from women he dated, but to be fair, that level of vetting would be highly unusual in a Senate race.

The second part of the process is to sit down with the candidate and their spouse and ask them to disclose everything that could be used against them. This is where the tattoo should have come up. It’s where Platner’s wife disclosed the sexting. The campaign team needs a candidate who will tell them the truth. Platner did not do that, so even the best vetting team is doomed to fail with a candidate too narcissistic and short-sighted to disclose the truth.

His campaign team did do a poor job of vetting him, but ultimately Platner is really at fault.

Dan, with Platner now out, what happens to all the money he’s raised and — as importantly maybe — things like TV time he’s booked. How can the next nominee use what the prior campaign now can’t?

A candidate for federal office can give their unspent campaign funds to a party committee or to charity. They can roll it over into a PAC for themselves, or give it out in $2,000 increments to other candidates. From the reporting, it seems like Platner had very little money left. One report said he had about $100,000 on hand, so this might be a little bit of a moot point.

The TV money is likely refunded to the campaign if the ads don’t run.

Dan, I appreciate your analytic approach to polls and politics- it’s very helpful to see actual data. I’m wondering if your assessment of the possibility of Democrats taking the majority in the Senate (which you have admitted is unlikely but still possible) takes into account the possible party switching of John Fetterman. He will not be re-elected either as a Republican or Democrat in Pennsylvania in 2028, but could do a lot of damage over 2 years if he switches parties. Thank you!

My analysis that the Senate is tough but possible for Democrats is based on Democrats getting to the 51 votes they need for the majority, not the 52 votes they would need to maintain the majority if Fetterman were to switch parties. Fetterman swears he won’t switch parties, for whatever that is worth.

I noticed that the Trump administration recently removed protections for a large number of endangered species. Politically, that struck me as an odd move. I don’t imagine there’s a significant “extinction lobby” within the Republican electorate.

Is this really less about endangered species than about signaling a broader anti-regulatory philosophy? If so, is the political calculation aimed more at satisfying organized interests—such as construction, energy, manufacturing, and other industries affected by environmental regulations—and attracting their financial and political support than at winning votes from individual citizens?

More broadly, how should we think about policy decisions like this that appear to have relatively little appeal to the average voter but are pursued nonetheless? Is this an example of how organized interests can matter more than broad public opinion, or is there another political dynamic I’m missing?

This is all about doing a favor for donors and political allies. The organized interests you mentioned hired lobbyists; those lobbyists donate money to the right campaigns, and then work their contacts in the administration to get these rule changes. The people within the administration are probably sympathetic anyway, so it’s not that hard to get done.

Dan, thank you for your voluminous, entertaining and informative work in the content mines both here and on all the Crooked podcasts. Stopping data centers and Flock cameras are two issues that seem to resonate with the public and cut across party lines. What can Democrats do to be champions of these issues, pulling together a winning coalition without letting Republicans seize the credit for it in the minds of the voters? Some Republican elected officials, no matter their other odious views, seem to be on the right side of the fights against data centers and mass surveillance via Flock camera.

I could not agree more on data centers (haven’t seen very much research on Flock cameras). Over on Message Box Pro, my subscription service for people who work in politics, I have been advising candidates to focus on stopping data centers, particularly at the local level. It’s a bipartisan issue that is populist and elicits a passionate response. It’s insane to me that more Democrats are not doing this.

It’s a layup.

Don’t forget to leave your questions for next week below.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Can a Democrat Really Win Texas?
item.description: I hope everyone had a great Fourth of July holiday and celebrated our country despite Trump’s best efforts to destroy it. These mailbags typically come on Saturdays, and since Saturday was the Fourth of July, I thought sending it out that day would hurt engagement. Now that I think about it,…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
I hope everyone had a great Fourth of July holiday and celebrated our country despite Trump’s best efforts to destroy it.

These mailbags typically come on Saturdays, and since Saturday was the Fourth of July, I thought sending it out that day would hurt engagement. Now that I think about it, sending it out on the Monday after a holiday weekend probably isn’t much better, but I made a promise (without really thinking it through), so here we are.

As a reminder, these mailbags are for paid subscribers. If you want to get your question answered — and read my answers to everyone else’s — please consider becoming a paid subscriber.

If you’re already a subscriber, leave your question for next week in the comments below.

In your view, what is similar (Gov, Senate, House, State leg) and different from the last time it broke our hearts? What races is Vote Save America focusing on and why?

When i think about how consequential Texas is - this November and particularly beyond - I feel like I should be directing effort there, but like driving across it, it’s just so damned big and overwhelming.

So many Democrats I talk to love James Talarico but are afraid to get their hopes up for a Democrat in Texas. I very much get this sentiment. Texas has been Lucy with the football for Democrats for a decade now. The 2018 election, in particular, crushed our souls. Beto O’Rourke was such an inspiring candidate and Ted Cruz was so viscerally annoying that we came to believe a Democrat could finally win Texas.

Look, this is a tough race. Texas is a tough state. The GOP is going to drop about half a billion bucks in negative ads right on Talarico’s head. According to the New York Times poll of Texas released last week, Talarico and Abbott are tied 47-47.

But I think Democrats have a better chance in this election than we did in 2018, for three reasons.

First, it’s somewhat challenging to use the 2018 exit polls as a benchmark, because there’s been so much shifting in political coalitions since then, and Texas — with its large and growing Latino population and an influx of voters post-COVID — is ground zero for those changes. In 2024, Trump won Latino voters by 10 points. Talarico is winning them by 32.

Second, in 2018, Trump’s approval rating in Texas hovered around even. In the NYT poll, today it’s 44-53. Midterms are referendums on the incumbent, and a less popular Trump is good news for Talarico.

Finally — and I don’t say this lightly — Ken Paxton is a much worse candidate than Ted Cruz. In September 2018, Quinnipiac found Cruz with a 52/43 favorability rating. The NYT poll has Paxton eleven points underwater, and 30 points underwater with independents. Cruz was a popular(ish) incumbent. Paxton is an unpopular nominee with a divorce on biblical grounds, a federal bribery investigation, an impeachment, and a securities fraud indictment in his rearview mirror.

There’s a long way to go. Talarico faces an uphill battle, but he has a very real shot.

Hi Dan. I've heard a lot of news about establishment Dems or members of their ecosystem saying that leftists aren't real Democrats, mainly around the recent New York primary. Do you agree with this sentiment? And if so, where should supporters of the party's left flank put their votes and resources if they are not welcome in the Democratic party? It was my understanding that if you, as a citizen, want to see a particular agenda set by our representatives, you should vote accordingly and persuade your fellow voters. Is this not what has been happening with the uptick in left leaning candidates and victories?

This is a very timely question. Since it was submitted last week, Mirat Kiros, a DSA member, defeated Colorado Congresswoman Diana DeGette, a 30-year incumbent, by more than 10 points. A DSA candidate might win the Wisconsin gubernatorial nomination. Much of the Democratic Party establishment is, of course, freaking out. It’s worth noting that the story of the anti-establishment revolt within the party is much bigger than the so-called invasion of Democratic Socialists. Graham Platner is not a Democratic Socialist. Abdul El-Sayed, who is leading in the polls in Michigan, is not a Democratic Socialist. Manny Rutinel, a progressive who won the Democratic nomination in Colorado’s 8th district, is not a Democratic Socialist. There’s something bigger and generational happening here too.

But is that change good? Bad? Or ultimately destructive?

With all that said, the anger of the party establishment has been focused mainly on the DSA-aligned candidates. Some have argued these candidates aren’t really Democrats and want to destroy the party from within. Here’s Jonathan Chait making that point recently in The Atlantic.

The DSA, in fact, seems to despise the Democratic Party. Darializa Avila Chevalier has called Joe Biden a “rapist” and posted a vulgar attack on Kamala Harris on social media. She was nonetheless nominated for a House seat in New York last week by Democratic voters who presumably don’t all share those sentiments. The DSA now includes a growing caucus in Congress, has mayoral candidates well positioned to win in several big cities, and plans to throw its weight behind a yet-to-be-determined presidential candidate in 2028.

Many Democratic leaders have treated these DSA candidates as interlopers and suggested there’s something inappropriate or illegitimate about their wins — not that there was election fraud, but that someone who wasn’t “really” a Democrat won a Democratic seat.

Here’s the thing: no one in Washington, DC or at the DNC gets to decide who is a Democrat or what positions someone must hold to be one. There’s no official list of things you can or can’t say, no rulebook on who you have to be nice to.

Democratic voters pick their standard-bearers. It’s their choice. If the voters of New York City prefer Democratic Socialists to establishment Democrats, that’s their choice. Several states allow independents to vote in Democratic primaries — I know a lot of Democrats hate this, since it lets people who don’t officially align with the party pick our nominee (I disagree) — but New York has a closed primary, and only Democrats voted here.

This is how democracy works. The voters pick. Democrats in New York will pick different people than Democrats in West Virginia.

The polling and the electoral results are as clear as day: Democrats are unhappy with the status quo within the party, and they’re voting accordingly.

If you disagree with the direction the party is going, stop complaining, stop whining, and start offering a more compelling message about why your direction is better.

Regarding Mary Najarian’s question: An alternative that doesn’t take a Constitutional amendment would be for the congress to return to the original way we counted votes in the electoral college: by congressional district. That would mean each state would cast its votes based on how each of its congressional districts voted, and two based on how the state voted as a whole. That would eliminate the winner-take-all voting that we have now. Wouldn’t that make the election somewhat more democratic?

This approach, which Maine and Nebraska currently employ, can be changed at the state level and doesn’t require a constitutional amendment. Moving away from winner-take-all means candidates campaign in more places — Biden and Harris ran ads in Omaha because Nebraska’s Second District is competitive at the presidential level.

There are a couple of challenges to awarding electoral votes by congressional district. First, gerrymandering gives state governments tremendous influence over the outcome — Texas and Florida, for example, effectively took about 10 electoral votes away from Democrats through partisan redistricting. Second, for such a system to be equitable, everyone would need to adopt it. California and Texas would have no incentive to do so, since it would clearly advantage the other party.

Hi Dan. I’m frustrated by how little Dems have to say about climate change. Do you think our next presidential candidate can make it part of a winning message? Do you think a Dem trifecta can make more lasting moves toward a green economy than Biden did?

I'd separate these two questions. First, it's important to acknowledge that climate change is the top policy priority for highly educated Democrats, but much less so for the rest of the electorate. One of the problems for Democrats is that voters think we're focused on the wrong things.

This is all race- and context-dependent, but I wouldn’t counsel a Democrat to make climate change a central campaign message. I know that’s an unsatisfying — maybe infuriating — answer, but to do anything about climate change, we need to win first.

Second, Democrats can and should use the power they hopefully win to pass something significant on climate. There may be a political price to pay, but it would be worth it.

It’s 2032, and Democrats have just won a governor’s or Senate race in one of the old Confederate states other than Texas or Florida. Before you look at the results, which state do you assume it was?

The cop-out answer is North Carolina. It’s a state Obama won in 2008, came close(ish) to winning in 2012, and was competitive in 2016. It has a Democratic governor and attorney general, and could elect a Democratic senator this year for the first time since 2008 — the demographic profile of a state Democrats should win: a large Black population, large and growing cities and suburbs full of college-educated voters, and a growing Latino population.

If Democrats can win Georgia, we should be able to win North Carolina.

The bolder pick is South Carolina, but that’s probably further away than 2032.

Given the news about NBC, do you anticipate any meaningful changes in the quality, breadth, or depth of its news coverage? Is there reason to think a stand-alone NBC News will have greater editorial ambition, or are the same economic pressures that have been shrinking newsrooms still likely to dominate?

A related question: there is a vocal segment of online activists that seems to equate anything short of a full-throated charge toward the barricades with a lack of journalistic courage or sincerity. How do news organizations persuade those audiences that careful, evidence-driven reporting is often more effective—and ultimately more credible—than overt advocacy? Or is that simply an audience that cannot be mollified?

Finally, purely for fun but with a serious point behind it: do you see any plausible future in which a relatively small, independent media organization—prrhaps built around podcasts, newsletters, and Substack—grows rapidly enough to pull off the media equivalent of a moonshot and acquire a legacy network like NBC? Or has the era of that kind of surprise leveraged buyout effectively passed? And what role would you play?

A few thoughts here:

I’ve long worried about corporate media, where the outlet is just a line item in a larger business that intersects with government in countless ways. Trump has leveraged that dynamic, and billionaire media barons like Jeff Bezos and David Ellison have willingly — even joyously — complied. NBC spinning out from Comcast alleviates some, but not all, of those concerns.

The economics of linear television keep getting worse, so unless NBC can find a way to profitably pivot to streaming or digital, it’s likely to have fewer resources to invest in journalism over time.

People understandably feel frustrated that too much political journalism defaults to a “pox on both your houses” frame, understating the true danger Trump poses. Too often, Democrats see the media as allies in the fight for democracy. The media very much does not see it that way.

In this hypothetical, I would not invest in a linear television network. I think it’s probably a bad financial bet, and would have far less political impact than people assume. The voters we need to reach mostly don’t watch broadcast news.

Okay, that’s it for this week. Don’t forget to leave your questions for next week below.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Thoughts on Graham Platner's Imploding Campaign
item.description: I was originally planning to send the mailbag on Monday, but then we got some major news in the Michigan Senate race. So I planned to send it Tuesday, but then we got major news in the Maine Senate race. The mailbag will come on Wednesday, unless another Democratic campaign implodes. For weeks now,…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
I was originally planning to send the mailbag on Monday, but then we got some major news in the Michigan Senate race. So I planned to send it Tuesday, but then we got major news in the Maine Senate race. The mailbag will come on Wednesday, unless another Democratic campaign implodes.

For weeks now, Democrats have been holding their breath, afraid that another shoe would drop about Graham Platner.

Yesterday, Jenny Racicot, a woman whom Platner had dated, accused him of sexual assault. Politico broke the story, and here is what they reported:

Racicot said she had an on-and-off relationship with Platner, who is now the Democratic Senate nominee in Maine, for more than two years before he entered her rural Maine home uninvited one night in late 2021, deeply intoxicated, and forced himself on her while she repeatedly told him to stop. She said she cut off contact with him after telling him the encounter was not consensual.

“I remember him grabbing my pelvis and being really forceful of me,” she said. “I remember the specific moment where I thought to myself, like, ‘This is no longer my choice.’”

The allegations are disturbing, well documented, and highly credible. There is no question Platner should drop out of the race as soon as possible. If he does so soon, Democrats can replace him on the ballot with another candidate to run against Susan Collins.

I have been supportive of Platner and open to his candidacy, despite concerns about his behavior and judgment. There are many people online today saying “I told you so” and pointing out that I and others were very wrong to back Platner. Obviously, supporting him turned out to be a mistake. I owe it to you to lay out why I did so, what I learned, and where we go from here.

The reason I supported Platner is pretty simple. I believed he had a better chance of beating Susan Collins. Long before I knew much about Platner, I was concerned about Janet Mills as the anointed choice to take on Susan Collins. I thought the DSCC trying to clear the field for her was a politically disastrous move. This is what I wrote back in October of last year:

Given all of this, I have to question the political judgment of anyone who looks at the current landscape and concludes that the right answer is a 77-year-old establishment politician. At the end of her first Senate term, Mills would be in her mid-80s — several years older than Joe Biden was when he dropped out of the presidential race. According to Politico, the DSCC has never even reached out to Platner.

In 2020, Democrats ran an establishment politician against Collins and got their asses kicked. Mills would likely run a better campaign than Sara Gideon, but at a time when voters hate politicians, are down on Democrats, and are yearning for younger leaders, is a 77-year-old, two-term incumbent governor really the best choice?

As the race progressed, I was impressed by Platner as a messenger and campaigner. He built an impressive movement with real grassroots support and a record number of volunteers.

I stood by Platner after the revelations about his online posts and his tattoo; I believed that Maine voters would forgive mistakes made by a man dealing with PTSD after serving their country. And until yesterday, I continued to believe he was a better candidate than Mills. In the NYT poll of Maine released last week, after the sexting allegations but before the most recent one about sexual assault, Platner was still more popular than Mills.

Regardless of my opinion or the opinion of podcasters and national pundits, Plantner was the overwhelming choice of Mainers despite the flags. Platner was so dominant in the race that Mills dropped out before the election.

This is the question that has been stuck in my head since the Politico story dropped. I want to take them one by one.

Today, several people said to me (or tweeted at me), shouldn’t the Nazi tattoo have been enough?

My take then and now is that I believed Platner’s story that he got the tattoo when he was drunk on leave with a bunch of fellow Marines. I was always skeptical of this idea that Platner was a secret Nazi, in part because he had never said or done anything that suggested Nazi sympathies, and it’s not like Platner was shy about sharing some really bad opinions online during that phase of his life. The connection that so many people are making between this allegation and the tattoo is tenuous at best.

The question of Platner’s past online posts is more complicated. He told a very compelling redemption story about a dark period in his life when he was dealing with PTSD and spending too much time online. He claimed that past was behind him, disavowed the comments, and presented himself as a changed man. I was sympathetic to that story, in part because if we want younger candidates, we are inevitably going to get some candidates with bad posts from their youth. If posting dumb or even offensive stuff online is a non-starter, we are going to run out of candidates quickly.

The question isn’t what you said in the past. It’s whether you are willing to disavow those comments. People grow and change, and we should be open to candidates who do so.

However, my one regret is that I wish I had paid more attention to his posts minimizing sexual assault allegations in the military and he should have been pushed harder on that.

The more recent allegations of Platner sexting with women who were not his wife were very concerning and I found them infuriating. The recency and the lack of judgment were worrisome, but the bigger concern was that they raised real questions about his redemption story. When pressed by the senators who had endorsed him about what else may be out there, Platner reportedly assured them there was nothing else.

He was lying to them, and he was lying to all of us.

Everyone is looking for broader lessons to take from this debacle. One lesson is that we need to do more vetting of our candidates. I was shocked to read the recent reports that his campaign had done almost no vetting of Platner and had been caught unaware of much of what has come to light.

I worry that some will use Platner to argue that first-time and outsider candidates are too risky. That is a defense mechanism by establishment types who want to keep power for themselves. Some outsider candidates will blow up in our faces with scandals, but that is not a condition limited to outsiders. Earlier this year, Eric Swalwell’s campaign imploded over sexual assault allegations. Swalwell was no outsider; he was an insider who had served in Congress for years and run for president.

I fully expect Platner to drop out. Continuing is not an option. Were he not to do so, it would be a zombie campaign marching on to certain defeat with no support and no resources.

Platner is reportedly trying to use the threat of staying in the race to ensure that an ideologically similar candidate gets nominated. I’m sorry, but Platner has no real leverage. He forfeited that long ago. If he really cares about the issues he ran on and really believes in the urgency of beating Susan Collins, he should just step aside right now.

The Democratic Party of Maine will decide who will replace him, and I hope they pick the right person, because the right Democrat running the right race can definitely beat Collins.

I’m pissed at Platner, the people who ran his campaign, and at myself, and I am sad for the people who put their faith in Graham Platner because they thought he could bring the change they so desperately need.

They deserve much better than Graham Platner.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Michigan and the Fight for the Future of the Party
item.description: I was originally planning to send the mailbag out today, but then we got some major news in the Michigan Senate race. Now (barring other earth-shattering news), the mailbag will come out tomorrow. It’s been an exciting and tumultuous primary season for Democrats. Several incumbent members of…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
I was originally planning to send the mailbag out today, but then we got some major news in the Michigan Senate race. Now (barring other earth-shattering news), the mailbag will come out tomorrow.

It’s been an exciting and tumultuous primary season for Democrats. Several incumbent members of Congress have lost their primaries, in many cases to first-time candidates. But most of those races have taken place in some of the bluest districts in the country, where the primary winner is effectively the general election winner.

Despite all that has happened, there is probably no primary more interesting and consequential than the one happening in Michigan. There is no path to a Senate majority without a win in Michigan. It’s the one Democratically held seat that might be in danger. Senator Gary Peters is retiring, and Mike Rogers, who lost to Elissa Slotkin by less than 20,000 votes, is running unopposed for the Republican nomination.

The Democrats had been in the middle of a pretty contentious and very expensive three-way primary featuring Abdul El-Sayed, State Senator Mallory McMorrow, and Congresswoman Haley Stevens. This primary has been a proxy for so many of the bigger divisions roiling the party — anger at the establishment, ideology, and Israel and Gaza.

Yesterday, this three-way primary became a two-way primary. McMorrow suspended her campaign. She had fallen into third place in the polling, and there were questions about how she could overtake El-Sayed and Stevens before the August 4th primary.

Today, I'm announcing that I am suspending my campaign for United States Senate.

Sun, 05 Jul 2026 17:42:40 GMT

As some of you know, I was supporting McMorrow in this race. I rarely, if ever, take a position in a Democratic primary, but I have long been impressed by Mallory and have gotten to know her a little bit over the years. I think she is extremely talented, a good person, and someone who is in politics for the right reasons.

Great candidates sometimes lose races. Barack Obama famously got clobbered in a race for Congress. I’d encourage you to watch the video above. Mallory McMorrow has a very bright future, and I’ll be watching to see what she does next.

Her decision reshapes the most important Democratic primary in the country. Here’s what we need to be watching.

As always happens when a campaign fails, there is a lot of Monday morning quarterbacking. For the McMorrow campaign, much of that analysis has centered on how McMorrow responded when Hasan Piker came to campaign for El-Sayed. McMorrow condemned El-Sayed for campaigning with Piker.

The move seemed to backfire; the progressive world turned its fire on McMorrow instead of Stevens, who is backed by AIPAC, and McMorrow started to drop in the public polling. I don’t think McMorrow handled that moment particularly well; it definitely damaged her, but I think its importance may be overstated. I’m just not sure how many actual voters tracked an online dispute over a streamer whom, according to one poll, 80% of Michigan Dem primary voters didn’t know.

Either way, the Piker incident is indicative of the broader challenge that might have doomed McMorrow’s candidacy. She was stuck in the middle: El-Sayed had consolidated the progressive base and had the backing of Bernie Sanders and several prominent unions, while Stevens was backed by Chuck Schumer, the DSCC, and AIPAC, which was spending millions on her behalf. El-Sayed had the progressive lane, Stevens had the moderate lane, and McMorrow’s only path was as the Goldilocks candidate who could appeal to people in both camps.

That was always going to be hard, but it became impossible once El-Sayed had momentum and AIPAC started spending on pro-Stevens ads.

The battle for McMorrow’s supporters is underway, with both candidates responding to her announcement by trying to make common cause. A Data for Progress poll from April found that Stevens was the second choice for 55% of McMorrow supporters, while El-Sayed was the second choice for 25%. This poll is making the rounds today, but I am skeptical of it for two reasons. First, it’s from April; a lot has happened since then, and some of those voters have already moved to other candidates. Second, these sorts of theoretical questions have limited utility. In California, the polls were clear that Tom Steyer and Katie Porter were the second choice of Eric Swalwell’s supporters, but when Swalwell dropped out, his voters flocked to Xavier Becerra.

(I should note that McMorrow’s name will still be on the ballot, since ballots were already printed before her announcement.)

The RealClearPolitics polling average has the race at El-Sayed 35.0%, Stevens 29.7%, McMorrow 7.7%. Looking at these numbers, what’s more important is what happens with the 28% of voters who are still undecided. Who are they, and what issues do they prioritize?

One of the challenges in analyzing this race is that there has been a lot of polling, but very little of it comes from well-regarded pollsters or has a sample size large enough to really dig into the crosstabs. This race will ultimately come down to four questions.

First, can El-Sayed do well enough with women to win? Using the 2020 Democratic presidential primary as a benchmark, 54% of the electorate were women. The pro-Stevens side is already trying to weaken El-Sayed with women through an AIPAC-funded ad that went up last week claiming he has a history of disrespecting women.

Second, can either candidate build a real lead with Black voters? In the 2020 primary, one in five primary voters was Black. The candidate who wins Black voters will most likely be the nominee.

Finally, do voters think El-Sayed can win? The not-too-subtle argument from the establishment is that everyone needs to get behind Stevens because El-Sayed can’t win. I expect Stevens and her allies to keep arguing that El-Sayed is simply too risky a bet with the Senate on the line. While they’ll frame the risk as ideological, it’s mostly going to be about his name and his background.

The argument is a little bizarre, because El-Sayed is, without a doubt, a superior communicator and campaigner and has a much tighter message than Stevens. He fares about as well as she does in hypothetical matchups with Rogers.

There’s clearly risk with El-Sayed: he’s never won an election, the Right is pretty skilled at demonizing progressive candidates, and Rogers is a very tough opponent who codes more moderate than he actually is. But anyone who tells you Stevens is a sure bet is full of it. She’s a sitting member of Congress in an anti-incumbent environment, and according to Split Ticket’s wins-above-replacement model, Stevens performed about as well as a generic Democrat in 2024. I also have real concerns about whether a candidate bankrolled by AIPAC can generate the base turnout we need to win in November.

Michigan is a testing ground for the post-2024 Democratic Party. What happens in that primary will tell us a lot about where the party is headed and who’s going to control the Senate.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: The Supreme Court Just Handed Republicans Another Midterm Advantage
item.description: The Supreme Court has been on quite a run recently. A few months ago, they greenlit racial gerrymandering, allowing Republicans to literally draw Black members of Congress out of office. Last week, they allowed Trump to potentially deport more than a million immigrants in this country who currently…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
The Supreme Court has been on quite a run recently. A few months ago, they greenlit racial gerrymandering, allowing Republicans to literally draw Black members of Congress out of office. Last week, they allowed Trump to potentially deport more than a million immigrants in this country who currently have legal status and essentially shut down the entire asylum system. But their decision today in a case that has mostly flown under the radar is a mortal blow to what remains of our campaign finance system. It will advantage the Republican Party (of course) and dramatically increase the influence of billionaires on our politics.

In a 6-3 decision, the court ruled for the plaintiffs in a case called NRSC v. FEC. Here’s how Politico described the ruling:

The Supreme Court struck down limits on coordinated spending between candidates and political parties on Tuesday, a win for Republicans that will fundamentally change how tens of millions of dollars are spent in congressional elections.

One quaint little fact about the case is that J.D. Vance was one of the original plaintiffs who brought it back in 2022.

Campaign finance is incredibly esoteric and seems almost designed to obscure the legalized bribery taking place behind closed doors and under the table. But let me be very clear: this is the most consequential campaign finance decision since Citizens United, and it will have a significant and immediate impact on the midterm elections.

Here’s why:

There is a legal limit on what people can contribute to campaigns. Whether it’s you and me or Elon Musk, we can only give about $3,500 to a primary campaign and another $3,500 in the general election. People like Elon, of course, can give unlimited amounts to super PACs. And while there is plenty of grey area in the law, candidates cannot directly solicit or receive donations to super PACs or other outside groups. The contribution limits are designed to check the influence of wealthy people by capping what they can give directly to a candidate’s campaign at a relatively small amount.

People can give more to political parties and party committees, such as the RNC, the DNC, and the congressional and senatorial campaign committees. This is all very confusing because these committees have different accounts with different limits, but the short version is that a wealthy couple could give more than $6 million per cycle to all the various party committees combined.

Up until today, the FEC addressed this loophole by limiting the amount of money party committees could spend in direct coordination with candidates. In a congressional district, the party can only spend about $65,000 per cycle in coordinated funds, which is less than a week of ads — in some markets, not even a single ad. In Senate races, they can spend between $130,000 and $4 million, depending on the state’s size. Not nothing, but not much more than a drop in the bucket in today’s environment. Roughly $400 million was spent on the Pennsylvania Senate race in 2024.

Like so much else in our campaign finance system, those limits were imperfect, but useful.

And thanks to today’s decision, they’re gone.

It’s best to think of this coordinated spending as a direct cash contribution to the campaign. The party committees will spend the money on whatever the candidates want. Heck, the candidates could theoretically write the TV ads that the party’s money would pay for.

Here’s how it will work now:

A single donor writes one very large check to a party — not $3,500, but north of $3 million ($6 million for a couple) when you stack every party account across a two-year cycle. That was already legal.

The party committees move that money among themselves to concentrate it where it will have maximum impact. That was already legal too.

Finally, the party turns around and spends an unlimited amount of it coordinating with one specific candidate. That’s the part that was illegal until today.

Prior to this decision, an individual could really only put about $7,000 in direct contributions into a candidate’s campaign. Thanks to the Supreme Court, that number is now effectively in the millions, further shifting power and influence toward the wealthiest Americans.

While it may seem minor and technical, this decision will have a big impact — and there’s no reason to believe the court is done gutting the campaign finance system.

In 2008, the last presidential election before Citizens United, outside groups spent about $340 million on federal elections. By 2024, that figure had exploded past $4 billion — money raised and spent in unlimited amounts by super PACs that didn’t legally exist before the ruling.

Four years later, in McCutcheon v. FEC, the Court struck down the overall cap on how much one donor could give across all candidates and party committees combined. The predictable result was the explosion of “joint fundraising committees,” which let a single donor write one giant check split among dozens of party accounts.

At some point, the court could invalidate the individual contribution limits altogether, allowing Elon Musk to write a billion-dollar check to the candidate of his choosing.

Technically, this ruling affects both parties the same way. Democrats can now spend in the same unlimited fashion as Republicans. But practically, it will help the Republicans for two reasons. First, Democrats are better at raising small-dollar donations and Republicans are better at raising large ones — so anything that hands more power to the people who can write million-dollar checks is an advantage for the GOP. The second problem is hopefully short-term and resolvable. The RNC has a massive financial advantage that they will now be able to deploy to maximum effect

Since the 2024 election, the DNC has really struggled to raise money, so it’s hard to imagine a flood of people suddenly writing seven-figure checks to the organization.

Four years ago, a party committee filed a lawsuit, and today the rules the rest of us have to live by were rewritten by the only branch of government no one elected. In fight after fight, this Court has sided with the people with the most money and the most to gain from buying our elections outright.

Yet another decision from the Roberts Court that’s good for the Republicans and bad for America.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Some Thoughts on the Maine Poll Panic
item.description: Democratic anxiety about the Maine Senate race has been at a fever pitch ever since the last of the seemingly endless revelations about Graham Platner came out a few weeks ago. The New York Times did not put anyone at ease with the brand-new poll it released in partnership with the Portland…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Democratic anxiety about the Maine Senate race has been at a fever pitch ever since the last of the seemingly endless revelations about Graham Platner came out a few weeks ago. The New York Times did not put anyone at ease with the brand-new poll it released in partnership with the Portland Press-Herald on Monday morning. The poll found Graham Platner leading Susan Collins 48-46.

Now, on one hand, it’s very on brand for online Democrats to have a doomer take on a poll that shows a Democrat leading Collins, someone we have tried and failed to defeat for decades. Last I checked, being ahead is much better than being behind.

However, given Maine’s Democratic lean and the overall political environment, it’s fair to expect a Democrat to be doing better. And as always, it’s important to point out that there is absolutely no path to a Senate majority without Maine.

The Platner discourse has become so polarizing within the Democratic Party’s mostly online activist base that the poll is being used as a cudgel by folks on both sides to reinforce their priors. There are even some prominent Democrats using the poll to call for Platner to drop out of the race before it’s too late to replace him on the ballot.

It also won’t shock you to learn that some of the hot takes circulating online about the poll are not exactly correct and are certainly lacking context.

While I was very critical of the DSCC for backing Mills and excited by Platner’s potential when he launched his campaign, I have significant concerns about what we’ve learned about Platner since then.

With all that in mind, here’s a (hopefully) more nuanced take on what the poll does (and does not) say:

The initial take on the poll is that Platner’s online posts, his tattoo, and the more recent and concerning revelations about Platner’s sexting with women who are not his wife are weighing down his candidacy. There is a fair amount of evidence for that contention in the poll.

First, 88% of likely voters have heard about the controversies surrounding Platner. Second, Collins has massive advantages on the questions of character and morals.

Finally, and perhaps most convincingly, Democrats have a 12-point advantage on the generic ballot in the poll, and Platner leads by only two points. A significant underperformance.

Susan Collins is not a generic Republican like Ashley Hinson in Iowa. She is not a deeply flawed MAGA zealot like Ken Paxton in Texas or Mike Collins in Georgia who says and does dumb things all of the time. Susan Collins has a long history of winning crossover votes in her state. In 2020, she won reelection by nearly nine points while Joe Biden was winning her state by nine points, meaning she overperformed by a stunning 17 points.

There are a lot of Democrats and Democratic-leaning independents who have been voting for Collins for decades. She is very hard to beat, which was one of the original arguments for Platner. He’s a candidate who theoretically had the capacity to reach out to working-class voters who had abandoned the Democratic Party. A progressive with working-class credentials.

In this poll, at least, Platner is very much failing to fulfill that potential. He has the support of only 36% of white voters who didn’t go to college, which is exactly the same percentage of white non-college voters who chose Kamala Harris in 2024, according to Catalist data. Unfortunately, we don’t have Maine exit poll data from 2024, but we do have it from 2020. And Platner is performing one point better with white non-college voters than Democrat Sara Gideon did in that race. So, despite all of the hype,

In an ironic twist, Platner is performing 13 points better than Gideon with college-educated white voters. One area where Platner is delivering on the promise of his candidacy is with young voters. Platner is doing much better with voters under 45, and particularly those under 30, than Gideon.

I would have loved to see Platner winning by much more. He could definitely lose this race. I don’t want to sugarcoat the situation, and I am most definitely not telling you not to wet the bed.

But I also think those who look at these numbers and see doom are wrong. They are either (understandably) conditioned to see the worst possible outcome, or they are pro-Mills or anti-Platner people who cannot stop fighting the last war.

First, for all of Platner’s problems and the intense focus on his online posts, his tattoo, and his behavior, his favorable rating is just three points worse than Collins.

Second, there is no question that Collins has an advantage in character, morals, and in being the more extreme candidate.

The way I look at these numbers, there are lots of Mainers who think Susan Collins is a moral person but are voting against her anyway, and there are a small number of people who don’t think that about Platner but are holding their nose and voting for him. Also, if I were the Platner campaign, I would be more concerned about the idea that Platner is extreme than the questions of character and morality.

Third, Collins has her own vulnerabilities. A majority of Maine voters and 57% of independents said they worry Collins would be too supportive of Trump in the Senate, and 55% believe she is too old to be an effective senator.

Fourth, not sure if this says more about Platner or the overall political environment, but Platner has a significant enthusiasm advantage — 71% of Platner supporters are very enthusiastic about voting, and only 52% of Collins supporters are very enthusiastic.

Finally, midterms are typically a referendum on the incumbent president, and that is bad news for Collins. Trump’s approval rating in Maine is under 40. More importantly, 54% of Mainers want Democrats to control the Senate, which means Collins needs a bunch of people who want a Democratic majority to vote for the person who could guarantee a Republican one.

One last thing. Platner is FAR from a perfect candidate, and he could definitely lose, but the poll suggests that Democrats would be slightly worse off with Janet Mills. She has a higher unfavorable rating than Platner, and 85% of likely voters rate the Maine economy poorly, which is quite a burden for a two-term incumbent governor to overcome. It’s pretty hard to make the economic case against Trump when people think the economy in your state is so bad.

The big question is what — if anything — else comes out about Platner between now and Election Day. I am not sure how many more bricks the load can sustain, but this poll shows that absent that, he can definitely win and is probably a slight favorite to do so.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Can We Trust the Polls in 2026?
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. Lots of great questions and comments this week, so let’s get right into it. As a reminder, these mailbags run every Saturday for paid subscribers. If you want to get your question answered (and read my answers to all of the other questions), please consider becoming…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. Lots of great questions and comments this week, so let’s get right into it.

As a reminder, these mailbags run every Saturday for paid subscribers. If you want to get your question answered (and read my answers to all of the other questions), please consider becoming a paid subscriber.

If you are already a subscriber, you can leave your question for next week in the comments below.

In the last presidential election the polls did not reflect accurately what the final outcome was. Why should we believe them now and are they really true indicators of what and how voters feel?

This question goes to the heart of this newsletter and frankly, much of what I do. I like to believe that the value proposition of Message Box (and Message Box Pro) is that my takes and insights are grounded in data. I really try not to fire off from the hip, because that’s what 95% of all political commentators do.

So asking whether the polling data that undergirds my analysis is accurate is a tough but very fair question. Beyond my work, so many Democrats want to know if they can trust the polls that show Trump sinking, Democrats winning on the generic ballot, and candidates like James Talarico and Mary Peltola in the hunt in deeply red states like Texas and Alaska.

And after the giant poll misses in recent elections, it’s fair to ask — can we trust the polls?

First, I want to begin with the simple proposition that polls are not supposed to be predictive. They aren’t designed to tell us what is going to happen. They are snapshots of a moment in time. The polls that show Talarico beating Ken Paxton right now tell us next to nothing about what will happen in November. They just tell us where the race is in early June.

Second, polls are imperfect. They have a margin of error, usually about three points for a good poll. So if a poll says a race is tied and one of the candidates wins by two points, that poll is not inaccurate.

Third, in 2016, the polls were off by an average of about 4.7 points at the state level, with the misses concentrated in exactly the wrong places, producing a result that blindsided almost everyone. In 2020, state-level error was just as bad, again around 4.7 points, with the polls once more underestimating Trump. In 2024, that error fell to roughly 2.2 points, the lowest in at least 25 years, and while the polls still leaned slightly toward the Democrats as they had in the two prior cycles, the outcome landed comfortably within the margin of error. The difference wasn’t that pollsters finally stopped underestimating Trump, but that the size of the miss shrank to something close to the historical norm.

Finally, both in 2022 and 2024, people thought the polls were wrong because the pundits were wrong. In 2022, most pundits predicted a Red Wave, and in 2024, many pundits predicted a Kamala Harris win. But in both cases, the problem wasn’t the polls; it was the analysis of the data.

I would note that the issue with the polls has been getting enough Trump voters into the sample. Voters with low institutional trust are less likely to participate in polls, and over the last decade, these voters have disproportionately been Trump voters. That’s why the polls in the 2018 and 2022 midterms were more accurate — because the voters who don’t participate in polls are also less likely to participate in the election.

I can’t promise you the polls are right, but there are a number of reasons to believe they are credible, particularly because this is a midterm without Trump on the ballot.

Let’s check back in again in 2028.

I’ve been a big proponent of getting rid of the electoral college. I might be wrong but if candidates had to appeal to the entire country rather than a few swing states maybe we’d get more centrist candidates. And voter participation might rise. In my state of Illinois I don’t know why a republican would even bother to vote! I’m seeing more discussion around this from people like Pete B. And read something recently about how it might not even need a constitutional amendment to happen. What do you think? Any chance?

Technically, eliminating the Electoral College and reverting to a popular vote to pick our president requires amending the Constitution, something that is functionally impossible in our current political system. The workaround you’re referring to is the National Popular Vote Interstate Compact (NPVIC).

It’s an agreement among participating states to award all their electoral votes to whichever presidential candidate wins the national popular vote. It only takes effect once states totaling 270 electoral votes (a majority) have joined. The point is to effectively make the popular vote decide the presidency without amending the Constitution. It works through states’ existing constitutional power to allocate their own electors however they choose.

In the past, it was assumed that most Republican states would never join the NPVIC because the Electoral College had such a significant Republican bias. However, that bias was significantly reduced in 2024 as Trump made gains with voters of color.

Now, to the second part of your question. The Electoral College was a dumb idea, and the U.S. should have gotten rid of it long ago. However, I don’t think eliminating it will necessarily have the effect you hope for. It’s the parties’ nomination processes that pick the nominees. So the order of the first primary contests and how delegates are awarded has much more influence on the candidates’ ideology.

Putting that aside for a second, the Electoral College gives disproportionate value to small rural states. For example, Wyoming has about 580,000 people and 3 electoral votes, which means one elector per 193,000 people. California has 39 million people and 54 electoral votes, or about one elector per 722,000 people. Smaller rural and Midwestern swing states tend to be more conservative than big liberal states like New York and California. In a popular vote scenario, the big four states of California, New York, Texas, and Florida become an even bigger part of the electoral calculus. This is notable because New York and California are much more Democratic than Texas and Florida are Republican. A Democrat might be incentivized to gin up turnout in NYC, San Francisco, and Los Angeles more than to persuade swing voters in Waukesha, Wisconsin.

Similarly, a Republican might be incentivized to appeal more to moderate voters in California and New York. In a popular vote contest, losing those states by less would be much more valuable than running up your margins in smaller states.

Dan, suppose you wake up on November 4, 2026 and learn that Democrats have won a 51–49 Senate majority. Before looking at the state-by-state results, which Democratic Senate candidates do you assume won, and which do you assume lost?

This is my favorite genre of question — the kind that lets me recklessly speculate about things that have not yet happened.

So if we get to 51 seats, it means Democrats will have won in Michigan, Maine, and North Carolina, and then won two of the four redder states in play (Ohio, Iowa, Alaska, and Texas).

If I had to guess (and you’re making me guess), I’d say Democrats won Alaska and Ohio. I pick those because Mary Peltola and Sherrod Brown have both won statewide before and are running against unpopular incumbent Republicans. But I could also see scenarios where Democrats win Texas and/or Iowa.

If by some miracle Democrats win enough Senate seats this November for a +2 majority, what can they do if come late November Trump finds a way to force one or both of Thomas & Alito to retire so he can replace them with young right wingers?

Maybe the couple of Rs who Trump has turned on will refuse to seat a new Supreme Court Justice but I certainly wouldn’t count on it.

Not to be defeatist here, but unfortunately there is very little Democrats can do to stop Senate Republicans from pushing through new Justices. We saw this in 2020 when they rushed Amy Coney Barrett through to replace Ruth Bader Ginsburg. Democrats can — and must — do everything they can to gum up the works, raise holy hell, and delay the process, but the minority has basically zero say in the confirmation process.

Is the Obama prez library as cool as it looks??

I am, of course, very biased on this matter, but I thought the whole Obama Presidential Center was such a cool experience. The museum is great and interactive, and the whole campus is beautiful and inviting. I'm very much looking forward to going back and bringing my whole family.

Okay, that’s it for this week. Next week’s mailbag will come on Monday due to the holiday weekend.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Making Sense of the Shocking Elections in New York
item.description: Last night was primary night in New York and, as is befitting the Big Apple, the results sent shockwaves through the body politic and shivers up the spine of Democratic leaders. Here’s how the New York Times described the results: Mayor Zohran Mamdani and his allies swept a series of congressional…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Last night was primary night in New York and, as is befitting the Big Apple, the results sent shockwaves through the body politic and shivers up the spine of Democratic leaders. Here’s how the New York Times described the results:

Mayor Zohran Mamdani and his allies swept a series of congressional primaries in New York City on Tuesday in a remarkable show of strength for the insurgent left that sent shock waves through the Democratic Party.

Mr. Mamdani’s candidates toppled a pair of incumbents backed by the city’s political establishment, including major labor unions and the House Democratic leader. Another candidate backed by the mayor won an open House seat, and a handful of democratic socialist challengers he supported were winning down the ballot.

In a relatively bold move, Mamdani went all in. He bet real political capital to elect Brad Lander, Darializa Avila Chevalier, and Claire Valdez. He even appeared in this ad during the New York Knicks NBA Finals.

Sometimes these primary results, particularly when they happen in the city with the most reporters and pundits per capita, can be wildly over-interpreted. However, the Chair of the Congressional Hispanic Caucus losing to a first-time candidate with no political experience who supports prison abolition and has a colorful history of attacking the Democratic Party’s most popular politicians is something certainly worth analyzing.

What does the “socialist earthquake” say about the state of the Democratic Party and the rise of socialism, and should Democratic leaders start panicking?

1. Should the Establishment be Panicking?

Definitely

Two incumbents losing primary challenges on the same night is a huge warning. Incumbents almost always win renomination. In fact, since 1946, more than 98% of all incumbents have won renomination. I will also note that Dan Goldman and Adriano Espaillat are two of four Democratic incumbents to lose a primary this year, but the other two are victims of redistricting. Al Green lost to another incumbent Democrat, and Julie Johnson moved into a new district and lost to former Congressman Colin Allred.

While there may not be a spate of incumbents getting tossed out of office, there is plenty of evidence that dissatisfaction with the Democratic establishment is a major factor in races across the country.

That anger is fueled by a combination of factors, including a sense that the Party has failed to stand up to Trump, that it’s captured by corporate interests, that it’s not focused enough on the working class and cost of living, and that it has been too weak in its response to the genocide in Gaza.

The most notable example is Maine, where Janet Mills, the two-term incumbent governor with the support of Senator Chuck Schumer, was losing so badly to Graham Platner that she dropped out of the race before the first vote was cast. Progressive insurgents have also won races against establishment favorites in New Jersey, California, and Maine’s Second Congressional District. In Michigan, Abdul El-Sayed, a Bernie Sanders-backed progressive, has jumped out to a lead in the polls for the primary in early August.

In Senate primaries, even successful candidates have been twisted in knots, having to explain whether they would vote for Schumer as leader.

There’s no question that the wins in New York City will embolden Justice Democrats, the Democratic Socialists of America, and others who are recruiting and funding these primary challengers.

Perhaps the person who should be most afraid is Chuck Schumer. The Senate Leader is up for reelection in 2028. His favorability rating with Democrats is particularly poor, and he represents New York, the cradle of this anti-establishment revolution. At least half of the Democratic primary vote in 2028 will come from New York City, the place represented by a Democratic socialist mayor and a growing number of DSA-backed members of Congress.

Finally, Tuesday should be a wake-up call to the Democratic Party and particularly the groups trying to push the party to the middle. You are getting out-organized, out-fundraised, and out-maneuvered by the groups of the Left. Much of the party infrastructure has been hollowed out in recent years, and the vacuum is being filled by highly motivated and creative upstart groups. Most of them are on the Left. These progressive groups are giving voters and their candidates something to be for, while most—but not all—of the groups in the center spend their time scolding the left rather than advocating for a different vision.

And they wonder why they keep losing.

2. Is this the Sign of a Leftward Shift in the Party?

Yes and No.

It is an incontrovertible fact that the party has moved left in recent decades, and particularly since Trump won in 2016. As Milan Singh wrote in The Argument:

In 1994, self-identified liberals made up just a quarter of Democrats. But sometime between 2018 and 2022, liberals became a majority of the Democratic Party.

In The Argument’s polling, we see the same thing. Across all of our 2026 surveys, 61% of Democrats identify as liberal or very liberal, rising to 67% when you drop Democratic-leaning independents. On social issues, 69% of Democrats call themselves liberal; on economic issues, it’s 61%.

There are a variety of factors driving this movement, including and primarily polarization. As the Republicans move right, Democrats move Left. The other factor is that since 2016, the party has become much whiter, wealthier, and more college-educated, which has made Democrats more liberal, particularly on social issues, and moved them away from economic issues as a central priority.

Therefore, it’s not surprising that more liberal candidates are winning more often than they are losing. And it’s not particularly surprising that it’s happening in New York City, where Kamala Harris beat Trump by 38 points in a subpar Democratic year.

But it’s not uniform across the board. Democrats have nominated, without challenge, moderates in red states like North Carolina, Alaska, and Iowa. In Ohio, Sherrod Brown is an economic populist, but he is far from a Democratic Socialist.

Moderate Democrats won the governorships in New Jersey and Virginia last year. On the same day that the Mamdani candidates won, Democrats nominated more moderate veterans in key congressional races elsewhere in New York state. As the New York Times poll from last month pointed out, most Democrats want the party to move to the center on issues like crime and immigration. This is particularly true of Black, Latino, and Asian Democrats.

3. Are Democrats Really Embracing Socialism?

Well, it’s complicated.

The idea of socialism is growing in popularity with Democrats. A 2025 Gallup poll found that two-thirds of Democrats had a positive view of socialism.

Similarly, the New York Times/Siena poll found that 49% of Democrats and Democratic-leaning independents had a favorable view of socialism.
