A plurality of every age cohort of Democrats, including Dems over 65, viewed socialism favorably. Notably, it’s Democrats age 30 to 44, not 18 to 30, who are most favorable to socialism. The difference between the NYT and Gallup numbers is due in part to the inclusion of independents.

Now, I do not believe this is because a bunch of voters have studied the tenets of socialism and have started embracing the collective ownership of the means of production. Instead, I believe it is more an indictment of a capitalist economic system that is failing them than an embrace of socialism. Notably, that 2025 Gallup poll found that a majority of Democrats had a negative view of capitalism.

My main takeaway and advice for Democratic candidates around the country is not to embrace socialism in an attempt to quell an intra-party rebellion. But if you want to survive the rebellion, you have to do three things.

First, find a way to distance or distinguish yourself from the establishment that the base has soured on. Second, speak to the very real economic concerns that are fueling the embrace of socialism. Finally, you need to speak with the same authenticity and conviction that the DSA and other progressive candidates are bringing to the campaign trail. If you are being risk-averse, worrying about angering various constituencies or donors, or reading words written by a pollster, you are going to lose—and you are going to have no one to blame but yourself.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Why the Iran War Will Still Cost Trump in November
item.description: On a daily basis, it’s hard to know what’s going on with the Iran War. Some days, Iran and the US are on the cusp of striking a mediocre peace agreement. On other days, Trump is threatening to blow the shit out of Iran. When the war started, many (like me) made dire predictions that it would cost…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
On a daily basis, it’s hard to know what’s going on with the Iran War. Some days, Iran and the US are on the cusp of striking a mediocre peace agreement. On other days, Trump is threatening to blow the shit out of Iran.

When the war started, many (like me) made dire predictions that it would cost the Republicans the election. It was being covered and discussed with white-hot ferocity. Once-erstwhile MAGA fans like Tucker Carlson, Megyn Kelly, and Joe Rogan were savaging Trump for breaking his pseudo-promise not to start wars.

Four months later, the war has become background news.

True news junkies (Worldos in particular) are still following every twist and turn. They are tracking the on-again, off-again negotiations. They are well aware that even under the best-case scenario, Trump ends up signing a deal much weaker than the one Obama negotiated more than a decade ago.

But for most of America, the war has become background noise. It no longer drives conversation. Data from Magnitude Media shows that in recent days, more Americans are posting about the reality show Love Island than the Iran War.

Trump’s numbers have stopped sinking and stabilized at a historic low. The generic ballot has been steady for a couple of months.

This raises the question of what impact the Iran War will have on the midterms, which are still several months away. Some are already speculating that the Iran War will be yet another massive Trump blunder that gets memory-holed before people start voting.

I disagree.

Trump is already paying the price for starting and losing the dumbest war in American history. Even if a deal is struck in the coming days and weeks, the shooting stops, and the Strait opens, Trump will not escape the political pain from the Iran War.

Here’s why:

Everything in American politics over the last five years has revolved around prices. It’s what took down Biden’s presidency. High prices are why Trump was elected, and his failure to lower them is why Republicans are staring down the barrel of a brutal midterm.

That actually understates the case. It’s not just that Trump didn’t lower prices. It’s that he raised them by starting a war no one wanted against a country no one thought was an imminent threat.

Gas prices are up nearly 40% since the U.S. started the war, with five states near $5 and California near $6. According to an estimate from Brown University, Americans have spent an additional $33 billion on gasoline, which translates to anywhere from less than $20 to more than $300 a month for a typical driver.

And it’s not just gas. Diesel prices are way up, too, which raises the cost of everything because diesel powers freight, farm equipment, and shipping. So the prices of food and goods are up because of Trump’s dumb war.

Even if Trump and Iran cut a deal tomorrow (which seems highly unlikely), it would take months, if not years, for traffic to reach pre-war levels in the Strait of Hormuz. Patrick De Haan, head of petroleum analysis at GasBuddy, says we may not see pre-war gas prices until 2027.

Long after the midterms are over.

Even if prices do come down from their post-war high, Trump has suffered other, more permanent damage.

Were it not for his massive bestselling book “The Art of the Deal,” Trump almost certainly would never have been president. The 1987 book spent roughly a year on the New York Times bestseller list, sold more than a million copies, turned a New York developer into a household name, and minted the brand he’s been selling ever since: Trump, the dealmaker.

That imprimatur of business success has given Trump so much — largely undeserved — credibility with voters on the economy and in his dealings with foreign leaders.

Well, that brand has taken a massive hit during the Iran War. A brand-new CBS News poll finds just 22% of Americans said the deal with Iran is better for the United States, while 37% said it’s better for Iran. The dealmaker is supposed to win the deal; a plurality thinks he lost. What makes it even worse for Trump: only about 4 in 10 Republicans think he negotiated a win.

It’s impossible to come back when a core part of your political identity collapses. Bush’s image as someone who could protect the homeland never recovered from mismanaging Katrina. Biden’s identity as a steady hand never came back after the chaotic withdrawal from Afghanistan and a perceived unsteady management of the pandemic.

And I am very skeptical that Trump can recover from being seen as so weak and feckless against Iran.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: The Media Merger that Should Really Scare Democrats
item.description: Last week, the Trump Administration approved Paramount’s acquisition of Warner Brothers/Discovery. This is the second major acquisition for David Ellison, a pro-Trump billionaire who has become even more slavishly pro-Trump while seeking regulatory approval. Ellison and Bari Weiss, his ideological…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Last week, the Trump Administration approved Paramount’s acquisition of Warner Brothers/Discovery. This is the second major acquisition for David Ellison, a pro-Trump billionaire who has become even more slavishly pro-Trump while seeking regulatory approval. Ellison and Bari Weiss, his ideological henchwoman, will now control CBS and CNN.

Within a few short months, Ellison and Weiss have tried to functionally destroy 60 Minutes, and they probably have their eyes set on the CNN journalists who have tried to hold Trump accountable over the last few decades.

Paramount buying Warner Brothers is bad. Full stop.

But it’s not the media merger that scares me the most.

On Monday, Fox Corporation agreed to acquire Roku for $22 billion — the largest deal in the company’s history. Most of the coverage framed it as the Murdochs finally getting into the streaming business, where they have long lagged behind their competition. That’s true, but it buries the part that should worry anyone who cares about democracy.

Fox buying Roku is a very big deal, and in terms of political impact, it might end up being much larger than pro-Trump billionaires owning declining traditional media outlets.

Here’s why.

When most people think about Roku — to the extent that they think about Roku at all — they picture a hardware device like an Apple TV or an Amazon Fire Stick that plugs into your TV to let you access streaming content like Netflix, YouTube, and Disney+.

But Roku isn’t really a hardware company. It’s an operating system.

When you turn on a Roku TV — or a TV from any of the dozens of brands that run Roku’s software — the first thing you see isn’t a show. It’s Roku’s home screen. The tiles. The recommendations. The big promotional box in the corner. That screen decides what tens of millions of Americans see before they’ve made a single choice of their own.

That home screen is massively powerful, and it has a real influence on what people choose to watch. If this deal goes through, Fox and the Murdoch family will control the gateway to television for tens of millions of Americans.

Most of the conversation around streaming focuses on platforms like Netflix, Apple TV, and HBO Max, because they make the prestige programs viewed by the wealthier, college-educated populace that drives pop culture and business conversations.

But the bigger story is happening somewhere else, and Nielsen — the gold standard of TV measurement — tracks Roku two different ways. Both should alarm you.

The first is everything Americans watch on a Roku-powered device, whether it’s Netflix, YouTube, Disney+, or anything else. In December 2025, 21.2% of all TV viewing in America took place on the Roku platform. One in five minutes of all American television now flows through a single company’s operating system.

The second is the Roku Channel, Roku’s own free streaming service, which has quietly become a destination in its own right: it hit an all-time high 3.0% share of total TV in December 2025, something only YouTube, Netflix, and Disney have ever achieved.

We don’t talk enough about Free Ad-Supported Television (FAST) channels, but they are an increasingly influential part of the media landscape. As more Americans cut the cord and subscription services like Netflix keep raising their prices, more and more people are watching the free channels built into their smart TVs.

Fox already owns Tubi, one of the two biggest FAST services in the country. This deal hands it the Roku Channel, too, meaning one partisan company would control the two largest free-streaming services in America, plus the operating system through which one in five of the nation’s TV-watching minutes flows.

Pro-MAGA billionaires controlling media and content is bad. Pro-MAGA billionaires controlling the means of distributing that content is much worse.

The paradox of the modern media system is that we are awash in endless amounts of content, but it’s very hard for creators to get that content in front of people — and very hard for consumers to discover it. That makes the most valuable media properties not the content itself, but the pipes that provide access to it.

Those who control the pipes control access. And the Murdochs are about to control some of the most important distribution mechanisms in America.

They can use the Roku home screen to promote their products, their politics, and their ideology — or to bury progressive content where no one will find it. What icons they place on the home screen, what they feature in the advertising and preview tabs, what they push and what they hide: all of it will be enormously influential in shaping what tens of millions of Americans see.

We have spent decades yelling about the dangers of the Fox News channel. The Murdochs are no longer content to own one megaphone in the room. They’re buying the room. And they’re doing it while everyone’s attention is fixed on the last war. Don’t let the size of the Paramount headline fool you. The Roku deal is the one that ends with someone else’s hand on your remote.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Is "It's the Economy, Stupid" Still the Right Strategy?
item.description: Good morning, and greetings from the Chicago airport. I'm headed home after an incredible couple of days at the opening of the Obama Presidential Center. The whole experience was deeply meaningful and hopeful. I hope you got to see some of the speeches, and that you get a chance to visit one day.…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Good morning, and greetings from the Chicago airport. I'm headed home after an incredible couple of days at the opening of the Obama Presidential Center. The whole experience was deeply meaningful and hopeful. I hope you got to see some of the speeches, and that you get a chance to visit one day. Some really interesting questions this week, so let's get right into it, but first, some housekeeping.

If you are already a subscriber, you can leave your question for next week in the comments below.

Given the plethora of regime outrages, incompetence, and festering issues, is it still, “It’s the economy, stupid”, as priority emphasis for congressional candidates this November?

You have nailed the fundamental question at the heart of this election and one of the core strategic tensions since Trump stormed onto the scene.

As many of you know, “It’s the economy, stupid” is a motto coined by James Carville during Bill Clinton’s 1992 campaign to remind himself and the team that the economy was the most important issue in the race and that everything Clinton said and did should come back to it.

“It’s the economy, stupid” was a central principle in almost every Democratic campaign from ‘92 to 2016. But then Trump scrambled the equation. His statements, his behavior, his corruption, and his indecency were so shocking that they became the central focus of Democratic messaging.

As bad as Trump was in 2016 and 2020, he is so much worse now. The rhetoric and conduct are more dangerous. The corruption is more brazen. The impetuous and idiotic decisions—like starting a war with Iran—are more obvious.

Given all of that, should Democrats run a traditional campaign centered on the economy, or should we focus on the outrages, incompetence, and festering issues?

As Democrats, we have a natural tendency to overcomplicate the simple. The polling is crystal clear. Voters rank the economy and cost of living as their top issues; they are very unhappy with the economy, and they believe Trump’s policies have raised prices and made things worse.

The voters who put Trump back in the White House, Latinos, young voters, and others, have abandoned him primarily because of the economy. Trump ran on lowering prices, and instead, he raised them. Voters are pissed, and they are looking to take it out on Republicans.

So the economy and cost of living are the best issues for Democrats. But making the campaign all about the economy, stupid, is not as simple as it sounds.

Politics has changed a lot since Carville wrote those words on a whiteboard in Little Rock. Back then, campaigns had far more control of the message. There were two ways to reach voters at scale: paid television ads and press coverage. Ads were more important and more valuable than press coverage, because everyone watched the same TV, and you could reach the masses efficiently with the exact message of your choosing. If you wanted the campaign to be about the economy, you ran ads. And while you couldn’t control your press coverage, driving a message through earned media was a very viable strategy.

Those days are long over. The media has disaggregated and the old models have disintegrated. Much of the public rarely watches linear television, spending their time instead on streaming services and social media apps that don’t allow political ads. News is now delivered through algorithms that optimize for outrage and engagement rather than importance.

It’s simply not viable to ignore everything and repeat the word “affordability” ad nauseam. Trump’s corruption, his assaults on democracy, his relentless focus on the ballroom and the reflecting pool, and the rest of the outrages and absurdities drive the political conversation. To get attention, you have to be part of that conversation—and being part of it requires talking about the things everyone else is talking about.

Here’s the key thing to understand: the outrages, incompetence, and festering issues aren’t a separate lane from the economy. The most effective framing fuses them. The strongest version of the Democratic argument isn’t “look at this scandal,” running parallel to “prices are high.” It’s the chaos that's why your life is harder. Corruption means they’re working for themselves, not you. Incompetence means they can’t or won’t fix the things costing you money. This is the corruption, costs, and chaos structure—the outrages become the explanation for the economic pain rather than a competing topic that asks voters to care about process for its own sake.

I will admit that most of my questions come from places where I have disagreed with you. I really appreciate you answering them.

Another area where I have differed with you and most of your followers was on how Democrats should approach appropriations bills. I was glad when Senate Democrats avoided a shut down in March ‘25 and hated seeing Chuck Schumer walk into the debacle of the October ‘25 shutdown strongly suspecting that the end result would be the absolutely furious base that resulted. However, I did think that refusing to give the votes on the DHS bill this spring when DHS got stripped off on its own was the right choice. Politically, I still think that. Practically, I’m not so sure.

Now that the GOP has funded DHS through reconciliation through the end of Trump’s presidency, where do you think Congress stands on funding the government in the future? Will there ever be bipartisan appropriations bills when one party has a trifecta? If we only hold one chamber in the next Congress, will Hakeem Jeffries be allowed to cut a bi partisan deal and politically survive? Any reflections on shutdown politics from the last year?

There is a real desire among most legislators, particularly in the Senate, to return to some form of bipartisan appropriations deals. The main thing preventing them is Mike Johnson’s inability to control his right flank. But even under those circumstances, Congress passed all of the appropriations bills other than DHS—and would have passed that one too, if the killings in Minnesota had not happened.

The real question is what happens if Democrats take the House or Senate. My strong suspicion is that Jeffries will be able to get Democrats on board for a fair deal, but Trump is the wildcard. His absurd intransigence could be the thing that blocks an agreement, as it was in 2019 when he shut down his own government to get funding for the wall.

As for the shutdown politics of the last year, I still believe they were a necessary evil. Democrats needed to find a place to take a stand and use their limited leverage to extract some concessions. They did not win on the substance. The Obamacare tax credits were not extended. ICE is still funded. But they won on the politics. Sometimes fighting and losing is better than not fighting at all. This is one of those issues where people can have a good-faith disagreement about the merits, because both options—shutdown or rubber-stamping—were suboptimal.

What are the actual economic incentives for independent pollsters? Like what creates new ones and what leads outlets like gallup to stop their polls?

This is a great question, because pollster bias can affect results. When someone uses the term “independent pollsters,” they’re referring to polls conducted by entities other than campaigns, political parties, or others trying explicitly to influence an election. There are a few different types.

First, there are media outlets that conduct polls, such as the New York Times, CNN, and the Washington Post. Polls are expensive, but they generate content and clicks. These outlets want people to read the articles or watch the segments about the polls. So to the extent they have a bias, it’s toward making the poll newsworthy. That’s why they ask about the 2028 primary, even though polls this early tell you nothing.

Second, there are nonprofit entities, mostly foundations like Pew and universities like Quinnipiac. Their incentives are, in theory, about conducting research consistent with their mission.

Finally, there’s Gallup, an example of the third category. It’s a for-profit corporation that makes money doing research, analysis, and consulting for corporations and nonprofits. Its political polling, which isn’t in service of any particular client, promotes the larger brand and generates more business. I have no reason to think this affects their results—there’s a long track record of quality work. While they haven’t said so explicitly, I assume they’re backing away from presidential polling for business reasons. Polling is getting more expensive as it gets harder to convince people to respond.

Hi Dan, I love reading the Message Box and so appreciate your perspectives on the myriad of issues we face. My questions is one of hypotheticals, but to play the game of “what if” for a moment, I’ve been thinking about what things might look like now if Trump had actually won in 2020. He would not have had four years to regroup and restrategize, cultivating deeper sycophantic relationships with loyalists. Project 2025 might not have been developed in that time, or at least not as quickly. Perhaps some of his cabinet might have remained more “normie” and it would have been him trying to bail us out of the COVID economy. It feels like that four years off only gave him time to develop plans to pervert the government to serve himself and his family. I know it’s only hypothesis, but might we be in a better position today since he would have been out in 2024?

This is a hard and kind of fun question to answer, because it lets me recklessly speculate with no accountability. I can’t be proven wrong, which is great.

Here’s my take. If Trump had won reelection in 2020, I think he would have “governed” much as he did in his first term. He’d have been surrounded by the same people, and the right would not have been radicalized by the 2020 loss, January 6th, and the subsequent prosecutions. He would simply have been less effective at wreaking havoc. The GOP used the Biden interregnum to plot a vicious assault on the federal government and democracy. In this scenario, Trump becomes a lame duck early, does less damage, and Democrats win the White House in 2024.

There’s one glaring problem with that, though. Trump would have been in charge during the last couple of years of the pandemic and the onset of inflation. There is no doubt in my mind that he mismanages it—extending the pandemic, exacerbating inflation, causing more economic damage, and costing many more lives.

I’m not sure which is worse: that scenario or the one we’re living in. Neither is great.

I am from California and I leftist and it is not acceptable how long it takes for them to count votes and certify the election. They should hire more people and spend more money to just get it done faster. It should not take two or three weeks to call a congressional election or a mayoral election.

I couldn’t agree more. The process is a mess. Some have called for California to change its mail deadline to fix it: if ballots had to be received by Election Day, the votes would be tabulated faster. But I’m hesitant to do anything that means more legitimate votes getting tossed simply because the mail didn’t deliver them on time.

The better answer is to find ways to count faster—through more personnel, better technology, or both.

Okay, that’s it for this week. Please leave your questions for next week in the comments.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: What Today's Dems Can Still Learn from Obama
item.description: Today, I am in Chicago for the opening of the Obama Presidential Center. This is an emotional homecoming of sorts. I had worked on a number of campaigns (mostly losing) before, but when I packed up my life and moved to Chicago to work for an upstart long-shot candidate, it changed everything. My…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Today, I am in Chicago for the opening of the Obama Presidential Center. This is an emotional homecoming of sorts. I had worked on a number of campaigns (mostly losing) before, but when I packed up my life and moved to Chicago to work for an upstart long-shot candidate, it changed everything. My near-decade working for Barack Obama is the signature professional experience of my life.

It’s where I learned the most important and hardest lessons in politics. I met my wife (and my podcast co-hosts) there. And I made some of the most important friendships that endure to this day.

As you can imagine, I have spent a lot of time in recent days thinking about those years working for Obama. It’s not just nostalgia, although there’s plenty of that too. I have been thinking about how his style of politics and communication fits the current political moment. So much has changed since Obama stood in Grant Park on that surprisingly — but fittingly — warm November night in 2008. Some of the rhetoric about hope, change, and bipartisanship may sound naive now that Trump and the GOP are trying to seize power through every anti-democratic means possible.

But Obama is the most successful Democrat of his generation and remains the most popular political figure in America — including with voters too young to really remember his presidency.

So there is much for today’s Democrats to learn from him. That’s why I wanted to reshare this piece I wrote a few years ago about how Barack Obama communicates.

The closing weeks of the Midterm campaign have felt dreary at times. Exciting, qualified candidates are clinging to narrow leads against the worst characters in the MAGAverse as the political bottom threatens to fall out. Inspiration and excitement are in short supply.

Enter Barack Obama.

This past weekend, the former President hit the trail and blew everyone’s proverbial socks off with a combination of hope, inspiration, humor, and brutally effective political attacks delivered with a winning smile. The clips of Obama’s rally went immediately viral. A slew of adulatory press coverage quickly followed. Representative Ro Khanna put it best in an interview with the New York Times:

‘If he were running in every state, we’d win every Senate race, but he’s a once-of-a-generation talent,” said Representative Ro Khanna, Democrat of California, adding that watching the former president’s remarks should be required “homework” for the party.

As a former Obama staffer who still gets irrationally annoyed at the slightest criticism of my former boss, I have very much enjoyed this focus on 44’s immense political skills. Even those against Obama’s policies or politics cannot argue with his communications chops. He is clearly one of the best orators in American political history. I mean — he went from State Senator to the White House in four years in large part because of a singular speech that captured the nation’s attention. Obama has a powerful stage presence, a persuasive speaking style, and a gift for comedic timing. It’s easy to watch these clips and simply be in awe of his preternatural talent:

For other Democrats, “be more like Obama” is unhelpful advice. The political equivalent of “Be Like Mike.” This advice misses the point. It’s worthwhile to separate the orator from the oratory. There are lessons to learn from Obama’s speechmaking success. Compelling political communications is equal parts message and messenger. From the perspective of someone who spent more than a decade watching Obama communicate up close, I would like to share some lessons that Democrats up and down the ballot can replicate next week and into the future.

Barack Obama is a policy wonk, Nobel Prize winner, award-winning author, student of history, and constitutional scholar. There are few rooms he has entered where he did not immediately become the smartest person in that room. As he readily admits, he has a fondness for details and a penchant for being professorial. The defining characteristic of his speechmaking is simple. He talks like a human trying to communicate with other humans. It sounds easy, but few politicians in either party do it. Too many use political jargon, rely on cheesy lines written by mediocre speechwriters, and do poor impressions of past presidents, real (JFK) and fictional (Bartlett).

For example, watch Obama lay out the consequences of electing a historic dunderhead like Herschel Walker in the simplest, clearest, most devastating way:

People hate politicians. Talking like one is a surefire way to lose support. This isn’t an argument for oversimplification or avoiding nuance. You have to talk like a human, but you shouldn’t talk down to your people. Treat the voters with respect by making a sophisticated argument. Just do it conversationally.

Barack Obama is good at telling jokes. In fact, he likes telling jokes so much that he actually enjoyed attending the White House Correspondents’ Dinner — an event that required him to spend a Saturday night mixing and mingling with reporters and taking selfies with their celebrity crushes. On paper, that dinner would be slightly less preferable to a root canal without anesthesia. But comedy isn’t just a pastime; it’s a political weapon for the former President.

Over the years, Obama often asked us to find jokes or funny ways to make a point about one of his opponents. He understood that laughter leavened the political attack. A smile achieves more than an angry grimace. You can see this strategy in action during Obama’s speech in Michigan over the weekend:

The former President made waves on a recent appearance on Pod Save America when he said that Democrats could be “buzzkills.” He was making a broader point about the party, but he also believes that the best politics are fun politics. People don’t want to join an angry, self-loathing movement. Taking back our country should be a righteous and joyous fight, so let’s have a little fun along the way.

The following paradox often flummoxes Democrats: voters like our economic policies — often by very wide margins — but they trust Republicans more on the economy. Obama always understood that economic debates are really about advocacy. Who are you going to fight for and against to give people better lives? Voters are understandably cynical about politicians’ ability to deliver real progress — particularly on an economy that hasn’t worked for the vast majority of Americans for a very long time. But given a choice — voters will choose the candidate they think will fight for them. Obama was that candidate in 2008 and 2012; Trump was that candidate in 2016; and 2020 is complicated.

Democrats usually close their campaigns with attacks on Republican proposals to cut Social Security and Medicare. I think this is a good strategy. I have argued for it in multiple newsletters, but how the argument is made matters. Watch this clip from Obama’s rally in Wisconsin:

Obama uses Republican Ron Johnson’s support for cutting Social Security to demonstrate Johnson’s values. It’s evidence that Johnson cares more about rich people like himself than hardworking Wisconsinites. Using policy to make an argument about values tells you not just what Johnson stands for but who he stands with. And values-based arguments are always more powerful.

At its core, our politics is a battle over the story America tells itself. Who are we? What do we stand for? And what does it mean to be an American?

Nobody understands these questions better than Barack Hussein Obama, the son of an absent Kenyan father from Hawaii via Indonesia. From suggesting that his sympathies lay with foreign terrorists to falsely claiming he wasn’t born here, Republicans spent more than a decade calling him un-American. Still, Obama refused to concede the issue of patriotism to Republicans. I would argue that no politician has talked more about American exceptionalism. This line from President Obama’s speech to the 2004 Democratic National Convention is the Rosetta Stone to understand all of the speeches that would follow:

I stand here knowing that my story is part of the larger American story, that I owe a debt to all of those who came before me, and that, in no other country on earth, is my story even possible. Tonight, we gather to affirm the greatness of our nation, not because of the height of our skyscrapers, or the power of our military, or the size of our economy. Our pride is based on a very simple premise, summed up in a declaration made over two hundred years ago, “We hold these truths to be self-evident, that all men are created equal. That they are endowed by their Creator with certain inalienable rights. That among these are life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness.”

He introduced himself to the nation by centering his unique story as a proof point of America’s greatness. In Wisconsin, Obama did the same for Senate candidate Mandela Barnes who has faced attacks very similar to ones Obama faced in his career:

I suspect I will have more to say on this issue after the election, but the party that wants to destroy democracy, overturn elections, do favors for the rich, and take away people’s retirement doesn’t get to own patriotism. But they will if we let them.

Obama will be back on the trail this week in Nevada, Pennsylvania, and elsewhere. It will be fun to watch, but let’s also make it a blueprint for our messaging going forward.

If you found this post interesting, I hope you’ll consider sharing it with others who might enjoy it — and upgrading to a paid subscription. This is a reader-supported newsletter, and every paid subscription makes the work possible.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post.

item.title: The Four Forces That Will Decide the Midterms
item.description: Most political analysis, mine included, often focuses on the micro: the skills of the candidates, the efficacy of their messages, the quality of their ads, the virality of their videos, and the podcasts they do and don’t go on. That’s the stuff that fills the diet of the true political junkies…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Most political analysis, mine included, often focuses on the micro: the skills of the candidates, the efficacy of their messages, the quality of their ads, the virality of their videos, and the podcasts they do and don’t go on.

That’s the stuff that fills the diet of the true political junkies (like all of us), but in the end, the stuff that decides elections isn’t the small-bore strategy and tactics; it’s the larger forces that matter.

Kamala Harris didn’t lose because she skipped Joe Rogan’s podcast, or because of the infamous trans ad, or because she campaigned too much (or not enough) with Liz Cheney. None of those things helped, but Harris lost because she was the Vice President to a deeply unpopular President during a time of surging inflation and concern about the border. This is why I believed Trump was the favorite even in the middle of Brat Summer and Harris’s surge in the polls.

Similarly, the midterms won’t be decided by the messages and the ads, although that stuff will matter on the margins in very close races. If you want to know what’s going to happen in November, you need to understand the four forces shaping the political battlefield in 2026.

Midterms are always referendums on the party in power, and presidential approval correlates to electoral success. This is why most Presidents lose seats in the midterms, especially the second midterm. The only two modern examples of Presidents bucking that trend were Bill Clinton in 1998 and George W. Bush in 2002. In both cases, the incumbent Presidents had abnormally high approval ratings — Clinton because the Republicans made the politically insane choice to impeach him, and Bush because of 9/11 (this was before he invaded the wrong country).

I know the narrative is that Joe Biden had a surprisingly good midterm in 2022. And Democrats did hold the Senate, thanks in part to the Supreme Court and some terrible Trump-endorsed candidates. But we still lost the House majority.

This is all very bad news for the Republicans, because Trump is very, very unpopular. According to Nate Silver’s model, Trump’s approval is about 38%.

That’s a point lower than Biden’s at the same point in 2022, and four points lower than Trump’s own approval in 2018, when Republicans lost 40 seats.

Trump is a drag on his party. His approval is underwater basically everywhere. He’s bleeding support from Latinos, young people, and even white working-class voters, the core of the MAGA base.

This is why Democrats can compete in states and districts that Trump won by double digits only two years ago.

Trump ran on the promise of lowering prices for groceries, gas, housing, and just about everything else.

He has failed miserably.

The May Consumer Price Index numbers show prices rose 0.5% over the last month and 4.2% from a year ago, the highest annual figure since April 2023. For the first time in three years, wages are no longer outpacing inflation.

The American people are pissed, and they blame Trump. A recent CNN poll found that 77% of Americans — and a majority of Republicans — believe Trump’s policies have increased the cost of living in their community. It’s not just that Trump failed to lower prices; it’s that he did two high-profile things that raised them: enacting tariffs and starting the Iran War.

Inflation destroyed Biden’s presidency, and it’s doing the same to Trump’s. Whether Republicans like it or not, affordability will be the driving force in the midterms — and once again, that’s very bad news for the GOP.

The American people are not happy with the state of the country, our politics, our politicians, or our institutions. A recent Pew poll found that only 29% of Americans are satisfied with the way things are going in the country, and a majority believe the country’s best days are behind us.

An NBC poll found that just 33% say they are “extremely proud” to be an American, with another 23% “very proud” — down from 82% extremely-or-very proud at the start of Trump’s first term in 2017. That’s a record low for national pride. The same poll found that, for the first time in its history, a majority of Americans say they have “very little” confidence in the federal government or “none at all.” In 2016, that figure was 34% — an 18-point collapse in roughly a decade.

This is not a new problem. It’s been building for more than twenty years, and it helps explain why nearly every election since 2004 has been a change election.

This is a particular problem for Trump, because voters blame him for the negative state of affairs.

So far, this has all been good news for Democrats and very bad news for Republicans. I do think this is shaping up to be a very positive midterm for us. The political winds are blowing fiercely in our favor — but there are some concerns.

The biggest is that while voters see Trump and the Republicans as the problem, they remain skeptical that Democrats are the solution.

The Democratic brand remains somewhere near the toilet. Typically, party favorability works like a see-saw — one side goes down and the other goes up. But despite all of the Republicans’ woes, Democrats remain about 15 to 17 points underwater in most polling. During Trump’s first term, Democrats were quite popular. Our numbers took a nosedive during Biden’s presidency and have yet to recover. The numbers are particularly bad with independents.

Our brand problem puts a ceiling on how many House seats Democrats can win in a midterm that largely plays out in places Trump won in 2024.

Add it all up, and the forces of political gravity are pulling hard toward the Democrats: an unpopular President, rising prices, and a sour electorate that blames the party in power. The wild card isn't the environment — it's us. Whether 2026 becomes a wave or a missed opportunity depends on whether Democrats can convince voters they're not just the alternative to Trump, but the answer to what's making them so miserable in the first place.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Should Democrats Worry About Another Fetterman?
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. This is a bonus mailbag of sorts. I am on a family trip and was originally planning to take this week off, but I have a long train ride, and my kids are pretty zonked out right now, so here goes nothing. As a reminder, these mailbags run every Saturday for paid…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. This is a bonus mailbag of sorts. I am on a family trip and was originally planning to take this week off, but I have a long train ride, and my kids are pretty zonked out right now, so here goes nothing.

As a reminder, these mailbags run every Saturday for paid subscribers. If you want to get your question answered (and read my answers to all of the other questions), please consider becoming a paid subscriber.

If you are already a subscriber, you can leave your question for next week in the comments below.

What lessons can we learn from Fetterman? Were there signs? What do the Pennsylvania voters think?

John Fetterman seems to relish angering the party faithful. He votes with the Republicans on issues like the Iran War, and he’s a frequent guest on Fox News, where he echoes the network’s worst caricatures of the Democratic Party.

In some ways, it is a head-spinning turn. Fetterman had been a rising star in the party for a long time. In 2022, his campaign was praised (especially by me) as a model for modern communication.

As many wonder what happened to Fetterman, others have used him as an example to raise concerns about Democrats who could also end up betraying the party, in particular Graham Platner. Democrats who have opposed Platner have repeatedly raised the concern that he could be another Fetterman.

So what lessons can we learn from Fetterman, and should we be worried that Platner could pull a similar switcheroo?

As you know, I was highly critical of the DSCC for backing Mills and pretty sympathetic to Platner’s apologies and explanations for his tattoo and online posts. I find the latest revelations particularly disturbing and concerning.

That said, Platner is our nominee. Many people want to blame the Left, consultants, and podcasters (like me) for this situation, but Maine voters overwhelmingly chose him, and we need him to win. His support in Maine was so strong that the sitting Governor — with the full backing of the Democratic establishment — abandoned her campaign a month before the first vote was cast. The latest revelations came out late in the process, but Platner won his primary with 74% of the vote, and the last poll before they came out had him at 76%.

I know that is a very frustrating outcome for many (me included), but no matter how much you don’t like it, I promise you will like a Republican-controlled Senate less. Sometimes there are only bad and worse options.

Having said all of that, I want to unpack the idea that Platner is another Fetterman.

The argument is that because Platner is so new on the scene and expressed some very toxic views in the past, he could win office and then do an ideological about-face once in the Senate.

But Fetterman was not some out-of-nowhere outsider who rode a viral video to office. He was a prominent mayor for a long time. He was lieutenant governor for eight years. He was a prominent backer of Obama in 2008 and Bernie Sanders in 2016. He was also a leading advocate for legalizing marijuana and criminal justice reform. His record was well known and long established.

I am always hesitant to make assertions about someone’s mental or physical health, but it’s hard to separate Fetterman’s recent behavior from the stroke he suffered and his subsequent mental health challenges. This is not to say that someone cannot change their positions absent some trauma, but Fetterman’s change has been accompanied by erratic behavior that has concerned his aides and acquaintances.

All of this is to say that I don’t think there are a lot of lessons to learn from Fetterman. His situation is sui generis. I don’t know what will happen with Platner, but Fetterman tells us very little about him. The anti-Platner folks also note that Platner and Fetterman share the same consultants. You know who else used that consulting team? Zohran Mamdani.

For what it’s worth, Fetterman is in deep political trouble. He actually has high overall approval ratings, but those are driven entirely by Republicans. Two-thirds of Democrats disapprove of Fetterman, which means he has no chance in a Democratic primary when he is up in 2028.

Theoretically, Fetterman could change parties and run as a Republican in 2028, although he has repeatedly made it clear that he has no intention of doing so. And even for all of his recent conservative votes, Fetterman would be a very odd ideological fit in the Republican Party. And even if he did switch parties, it’s really hard to see how he would win a general election. Party switchers tend to earn the enmity of their old party and no enthusiasm from their new one.

My #1 question about the Maine U.S. Senate race is “Why were Platner and Mills the only potential candidates?” It suggests to me that the Maine Dems don’t have a very deep bench, or much of a bench at all.

This is an interesting and under-discussed question. Maine is a very Democratic state with a relatively deep bench of statewide and local officials. Platner came out of nowhere. He was recruited to run by progressive activists and consultants, but initially, most thought he was a long shot at best. Chuck Schumer's recruitment of Mills and her lengthy decision-making process froze the field. Because they assumed (correctly) that Mills would eventually run, most of the other Democrats opted to run for Governor or the second district seat once Jared Golden announced his retirement.

The absence of other, potentially better candidates is the fault of Schumer and Mills more than anyone else.

The breathless “election fraud” lies from the regime are already happening and being fake investigated in my home state, with the media covering it as if it’s no big deal since fraud is rare and the investigations will fizzle like they usually do, instead of explicitly stating it’s the administration’s way of interfering with (Dem) state-run elections.

What happens when these “investigations” come up with false/fake fraud? Or when the DOJ opens a sham federal investigation into Becerra or Bass, or both, for some made-up reason in October? I’m sure this kind of stuff will happen in other States as well.

You are correct to be concerned that Trump and his minions will use California’s very legal, very democratic voting processes to pursue bogus claims of electoral fraud. We also know that the Department of Justice is stocked with enough hacks that they will try to investigate and prosecute the claims, no matter how absurd.

For what it's worth, what's happening in California is totally normal. In California, any legal vote postmarked by Election Day is counted, so ballots often arrive days, and sometimes more than a week, later. Democrats tend to vote by mail while Republicans tend to vote on Election Day, so the late-arriving ballots skew heavily Democratic. That's especially true this year, because so many Democrats voted later than usual while they were undecided on the governor's race.

I don’t want to be sanguine about it, because as we know from 2020, these bogus fraud claims can lead to violence. But one lesson of the last 18 months is that judges and juries are pretty good bulwarks against trumped-up charges with no evidence. The margins in California will be such that it’s of little concern. My anxiety rises in races that could be much, much closer, where the fraud claims could be used as a pretext for the Republican House to refuse to seat a Democrat.

There is no way to prevent the GOP from making these claims or weaponizing the government to pursue them. The best solution is to win by a margin that is “too big to rig.”

Dan, I know I asked a question last week, but I’m really concerned about this issue... the OBBSBA arranged it so that the worst of the Medicaid and SNAP cuts would occur in 2027, so that if Dems won the House (and Senate), they would be blamed for those actions. When and how are we going to start making noise so the electorate is made very well aware of those facts?

The Republicans did delay the cuts until 2027, but I don’t think that was to pin the blame on Democrats. It was to get on the other side of the midterms. They couldn’t wait any longer for budgetary reasons.

Members of Congress — and particularly Republican members of Congress — are naturally short-term thinkers. Pass the bill now, delay the pain until after the election, and figure it out later.

Even if Democrats take the House and the Senate and are in charge when the cuts take place, Trump will almost certainly take all of the blame. The buck will stop with him, and Republicans will pay a price in 2028.

But hey, at least they made it through 2026 (or did they?).

Hi Dan - I hope you have a great vacation. I want to know if someone, somewhere is keeping track of the grift that Trump and his people are perpetrating - like in a spreadsheet with receipts. I think that the details of that would be important to use as folks compete in general elections for the midterms and in the 2028 elections. I think a clear accounting of how MAGA candidates are benefiting from his boondoggle would be good fodder for Dems and Independents who are challenging MAGA.

Yes, several Democratic organizations have taken on this task. Democratic Attorneys General and Congressional committees are also tracking all of Trump’s corruption. The big caveat is that they are really only aware of what has come out publicly through the press. That should change if and when Democrats take the majority and can exercise real oversight authority.

Dan, I have a general polling question. Somebody has to pay to get good polls done, and aside from the campaigns themselves, that’s traditionally included a lot of media outlets. Those outlets are under considerable economic pressures these days. From your perspective, is this affecting the number of polls we are seeing for the midterms?

The polling paradox of our time is that we have never had more polls and never had fewer high-quality ones. You are exactly right: media organizations are cutting back — not in the number of polls, but in the quality and sample size. Gallup has also stopped polling presidential approval, which is a loss because they have been doing it for decades and have very important historical data.

The campaigns will have access to all of the high-quality polling they need, but the public will be a little bit blinder than in the past.

Hope you enjoyed this mailbag. Leave your questions from next week in the comments below.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: What Democratic Voters Actually Want
item.description: The 2026 Democratic primary season is in full swing, and it’s mostly being interpreted through an ideological prism — do Democratic voters want the party to move further to the left or tack back to the center? This is a core question in Michigan, Iowa, and several House primaries taking place…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
The 2026 Democratic primary season is in full swing, and it’s mostly being interpreted through an ideological prism — do Democratic voters want the party to move further to the left or tack back to the center? This is a core question in Michigan, Iowa, and several House primaries taking place across the country. It was also the central question in the California race between Tom Steyer and Xavier Becerra. Prospective 2028 candidates and their aides are watching these results carefully, trying to divine clues for what Democrats will want in two years.

The electoral results are interesting and important, but each race is different and shaped by its own circumstances. Did Platner prevail over Mills because of ideology, or was it about age, or something else? In Iowa, the more moderate candidate seems to be leading in the polls — but is he leading because of his moderation, or because he’s been the beneficiary of nearly $10 million in Super PAC ads?

I promise you we’ll all debate these questions based on our priors until the end of time. But the recent New York Times poll has some really interesting data that sheds light on them.

And guess what? It’s more complicated than the pundits and partisans are willing to admit.

What you’re about to read is an excerpt from a detailed strategy memo I wrote for Message Box Pro, my subscription product for people who work in politics or communications at any level.

I’m offering Message Box subscribers a special deal: sign up and your first month is free. After that, you’ll be charged $65/month for full access — but only if you stay. Cancel anytime, no questions asked.

There has been so much discussion about how bad the Democratic Party’s approval rating has been since Trump won in 2024. We’ve definitely suffered among independents, but the thing driving down Democratic approval is that Democrats themselves hate the party.

In this poll, 53% of Democrats and Dem-leaning independents are unsatisfied with the Democratic Party. The dissatisfaction is greatest among the most important parts of our coalition:

  • Black voters: 59% dissatisfied

  • Voters under 30: 68% dissatisfied

  • Dem-leaning independents: 65% dissatisfied

Even a majority of college-educated white voters are dissatisfied with the Democratic Party. Relatedly, Chuck Schumer is more disliked than liked.

Many political observers have interpreted Democrats’ dissatisfaction with their own party through an ideological frame — that the party is too far left, or not left enough. This poll contradicts that interpretation.

Only 20% say the party is too far to the left, and only 17% say it’s too far to the right. Fifty-five percent say the party is neither too left nor too right.

Based on focus groups, anecdotal data, and common sense, it seems like people are pissed at the party for losing the 2024 election and not doing a better job of standing up to Trump. This is why Democrats are simultaneously telling pollsters that their party sucks and then voting for that same party at every opportunity like their lives depend on it (and they may).

That said, 47% of Democrats and Dem-leaning independents would like to see the party move to the center, while 28% would like to see the party move left and 19% don’t want it to move either way.

And in case you think this result is driven by the inclusion of Dem-leaning independents in the sample, every cohort other than voters under 30 thinks the party should move to the center — and even that group is only a 7-point margin in the other direction. Black and Latino voters want the party to move to the center by very large margins.

The poll also asked voters whether they personally wanted to see the party move left or to the center on a battery of issues. By large margins, Democrats and Dem-leaning independents want the party to moderate on crime and immigration.

The poll also asked Democrats and Dem-leaning independents about what the party needs to do to win. I usually hate these sorts of poll questions — I’m much more interested in what voters actually believe than in questions that turn them into pundits and make-believe strategists. But given how heavily Democrats are factoring electability into their choices, this data is pretty instructive.

First, the pollsters asked whether moving to the center, the left, or neither would give Democrats the best chance to win the next presidential election. Consistent with the other results, 52% said moving to the center would help a Democrat win, while only 25% said moving to the left would.

A similar dynamic exists across all issues except health care.

The number of Democrats who think the party should move to the center is slightly smaller than the number who think doing so would help it win. In other words, there’s a swath of voters willing to swallow their own policy preferences for what they believe will help the party win.

Make no mistake — Democrats wanting the party to move to the center is not the same thing as Democrats wanting mealy-mouthed, sand-down-the-edges corporate centrism. Far from it.

Democrats, like the rest of the electorate, think the system is corrupt, broken, and unfair, and they’re looking for people with the willingness and the strength to overturn it. Nearly nine in ten respondents think the country’s economic system is unfair to most Americans. More than eight in ten think the political and economic system either needs to be torn down completely or undergo major changes.

The party also prefers a candidate “who promises to lower prices by going after corporate monopolies and price gouging” over one who promises to lower prices by making it easier to build housing and expand energy production — by 37 points. You can also see the desire for populism in the fact that 50% of the sample wants Democrats to move to the left on health care.

I know these results are frustrating and confusing. But voters can be frustrating and confusing. They look at the world differently than political professionals do, and they tend to reject ideological silos.

Perhaps the most important finding from this poll is that there’s an assumption baked into much of Democratic strategy: that there’s an inherent tension between appealing to the base and appealing to swing voters — that appeals to one group necessarily alienate the other. This poll, very helpfully, shows that’s not really the case.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post.

item.title: How J.D. Vance Fumbled the GOP Nomination
item.description: At the beginning of 2026, J.D. Vance seemed like a lock to be the Republican nominee in two years. He was the sitting Vice President, and sitting Vice Presidents never lose. He was leading hypothetical 2028 primary polls by large margins, and he had the tacit support of Donald Trump and the MAGA…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
At the beginning of 2026, J.D. Vance seemed like a lock to be the Republican nominee in two years. He was the sitting Vice President, and sitting Vice Presidents never lose. He was leading hypothetical 2028 primary polls by large margins, and he had the tacit support of Donald Trump and the MAGA movement.

Well, all of that is gone now.

A recent Emerson poll showed Vance and Marco Rubio in a dead heat. Vance had 36% support and Rubio 35%. Back in August of 2025, Vance led Rubio 52% to 9%.

Vance’s problems got even worse with a recent blockbuster New York Times story with this brutal lede:

In recent conversations with aides and allies, President Trump often interjects with a question about his vice president: Does JD Vance have what it takes to go all the way?

He usually answers his own question: He’s not so sure.

This is a stunning fall for Vance. Even if the Emerson poll is an outlier (and it’s very possible that it is; their Democratic numbers in that poll were also unusual), other polling makes it clear that Vance’s numbers are coming down rapidly and Rubio’s are rising.

As you can imagine, I find this very enjoyable. I have always found Vance to be a dangerously shameless politician who will do or say anything for the sake of fulfilling his bottomless well of ambition. I can’t help the schadenfreude (and you probably can’t either). Vance is repellent.

Vance’s star is falling more rapidly than any presumed presidential frontrunner in recent memory, and what’s particularly notable is that there was no precipitating event. The more people saw Vance, the less they liked him, and then, as criticism and concerns about his abilities permeated the public conversation, his support seemed to collapse.

I know we have midterms coming up in about five months, and it may seem irresponsible to dabble in speculation about 2028, but what’s happening to Vance offers object lessons about the state of politics.

With that in mind, here are three reasons Vance is losing the GOP nomination before it even starts.

Authenticity — or at least the perception of authenticity — matters more than anything else in politics. Observers often confuse honesty with authenticity. Trump lies more before breakfast than most presidents do in an entire term. He is a liar, but he is authentic.

Trump is a narcissistic asshole, but that is his authentic self. You know what Trump is feeling at any given moment. You know which things he says that he really believes, and when he says things that he doesn’t believe, you know that too.

Vance is the opposite of authentic. Ten years ago, he was comparing Trump to Hitler, and he didn’t come on board until the moment he needed Trump’s support to run for Senate. To be clear, Vance doesn’t secretly still believe Trump is bad but is swallowing it for ambition. He believes nothing. Vance called Trump Hitler back in 2016 because he thought that was the best way to advance his career. When the circumstances changed, Vance changed with them.

As far as anyone can tell, Vance stands for nothing other than his own ambition, and that is patently obvious to everyone.

When Trump chose him as his vice-presidential nominee in 2024, Vance was heralded as the future of the MAGA movement. Reportedly, Trump was looking for someone who could shepherd his political coalition after him.

Vance was thought to be more ideologically aligned with the MAGA base than other contenders like Rubio, Doug Burgum, or Tim Scott.

In hindsight, this was an absurd proposition, because it’s based on a fundamental misunderstanding of the movement itself. Most pundits think of MAGA as a coalition of voters who are anti-immigration, anti-trade, anti-government, and isolationist, but more economically populist than traditional Republicans.

MAGA is not an ideological movement. It’s a cult of personality.

The evidence for this reality is all around us, but the most glaring is that this supposedly isolationist movement that rejected the Forever Wars of George W. Bush and U.S. support of Ukraine under Biden immediately became the biggest supporters of Trump’s Iran War.

To lead a cult of personality, you need a charismatic leader who can entertain the masses to paper over the rifts within any coalition. I find Trump’s personality to be odious, but he has charisma and the ability to grab and hold people’s attention. His rallies are giant parties for his supporters, and they find him endlessly entertaining.

The problem for Vance is that he has the charisma of a gym sock. He’s not funny, he’s not charming. He doesn’t fill the room or the TV screen.

You can’t build a cult of personality around someone with the personality of the most annoying member of the high school debate team.

If Vance really does blow the GOP nomination, we will look back to Monday, April 14th, 2025, as the moment it all fell apart.

That’s the day J.D. Vance was presiding over the White House event for the national champion Ohio State football team. When he went to pick up the trophy, it fell apart, and he dropped it on the floor. The video rocketed across the Internet.

Even Trump was concerned about Vance’s trophy fumble. According to that New York Times story:

Mr. Trump, always keenly attuned to the optics of the presidency, has zeroed in on moments when Mr. Vance might not look the part. He has repeatedly brought up a moment from last spring, when Mr. Vance fumbled Ohio State’s national football championship trophy on the White House South Lawn.

That was the moment Vance became a meme and was introduced to the public as a bumbling dork. That image followed him when he campaigned with Viktor Orbán in Hungary right before Orbán got clobbered in the election, and then went to Pakistan and failed to get a deal to end the Iran War.

I know it seems ridiculous, but memes matter in American politics, and once they set in, they are almost impossible to shed. What’s happening to Vance is similar to what happened to two other Vice Presidents who wanted to be promoted one day. Dan Quayle was meme-ified as dumb when he kinda, sorta misspelled potato in front of a classroom of kids. Al Gore, once known as one of the most ethical and honest people in politics, was portrayed as a liar after he supposedly claimed he invented the Internet.

None of this is guaranteed. It’s so so early, but Vance’s problem isn’t a bad week or a bad poll — it’s that the more America sees of him, the less it wants. You can fix a message. You can’t fix being J.D. Vance.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Why "Return to Normalcy" Is a Trap for Democrats
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. I wrote this earlier in the week than usual due to some travel. The dominant question, though, was about Graham Platner and the recent revelations about sexually explicit texts with women who weren’t his wife. Not an easy or fun topic, but let’s get right into it.…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. I wrote this earlier in the week than usual due to some travel. The dominant question, though, was about Graham Platner and the recent revelations about sexually explicit texts with women who weren’t his wife.

Not an easy or fun topic, but let’s get right into it. First, some housekeeping.

These mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

And don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments.

I’d like you to elaborate on the question Reid asked at the end of poller coaster and you cut him off. Are Osoff and Talarico “return to normalcy, nice young men” candidate or are they “uniquely qualified”? What are your definitions and distinctions here?

The context here for non-Pollercoaster listeners is whether the excitement around James Talarico, Jon Ossoff, and Pete Buttigieg, who all code as “nice young men,” represents a return to normalcy in the post-Trump era.

I reacted aggressively to the notion of Democrats embracing a “return to normalcy.” I believe I said I would light myself on fire if that was the approach.

Aggressive? Maybe. But let me explain.

The fundamental mistake of Democrats in the Biden era is that we interpreted the 2020 victory as the American people voting for a return to normalcy after Trump. Because of that analytical error, Biden et al. began touting a return to normalcy and defending the status quo. That’s how we ended up with a message focused on defending democracy rather than reforming it.

As we sit here today, Democrats have a good chance of winning the presidency in 2028. If things stay on this trajectory, the 2028 election looks a lot like 2008, and Donald Trump looks like George W. Bush: a toxic incumbent who will drag down his party.

But it would be a huge error to treat that win as evidence that voters are yearning for some pre-Trump normalcy. Four of the last five presidential elections have been change elections where voters threw out the party in power. The only exception was Obama’s reelection in 2012.

“Nice young man energy” (or nice young woman energy), a term I believe Sarah Longwell of The Bulwark has used to describe these candidates, might prevail in 2028. But it wouldn’t be because voters want the old status quo. David Axelrod once said:

Voters rarely seek the replica of what they have. They almost always seek the remedy, the candidate who has the personal qualities the public finds lacking in the departing executive.

This is why Obama replaced Bush, Trump replaced Obama, Biden replaced Trump, and so on.

If we win, it will be because voters want the change Trump failed to deliver. We must embrace it, embody it, and then enact it. Our job is not to simply rebuild what Trump tore down. It’s to build something new and more responsive to the American people.

How is recruiting one septuagenarian (Janet Mills) with a history of winning in a state that has proven difficult for Democrats to win an example of the “fiasco” of Chuck Schumer’s leadership and recruiting another septuagenarian (Sherrod Brown) with a history of winning in a state that has proven difficult for Democrats to win statewide an example of recruiting a “great candidate?”

Listen, I accept that Graham Platner won this primary decisively before any one even had to cast a vote and he’s going to be nominee. I don’t like it and I think every Democrat would say that Platner’s baggage (a generous euphemism) would be disqualifying if it were a Republican’s, but I accept that he’s the nominee and hope he wins. I am up front about my bias here. But, I have found the manner in which everyone reacted to recruiting Mills as if it was so outrageous and indicative of some rot in the “establishment” as beyond bizarre and pretty disingenuous. It seems to me a line of attack that isn’t based on any principle, but is always just situation dependent based on personal political preferences (see also, Bernie running in his 80s, Markey/Moulton).

I appreciate the aggressiveness of the response and the willingness to engage in good-faith debate.

Let me make a few points.

First, I’m not sure I have ever used the term “rot” to describe what’s going on with Schumer. The argument I’ve tried to make is that the establishment more broadly, and Schumer in particular, have lost touch with the Democratic electorate and the rules of modern politics, and have failed to learn some of the lessons of 2024.

Second, there is a difference between someone who is 73 and someone who will be 80 before they’re sworn in to their first term and is forced to commit to serving only one term. Every person is different. I interviewed Mills, and she seemed quite sharp. But the echoes of Biden were clearly a bridge too far for Maine voters.

Third, if you go back and read my writing from last year about the Maine race, my argument is not just about Mills’ age. My complaint was that the DSCC and Schumer were so certain that a 79-year-old card-carrying member of the establishment was the best choice. You can be a member of the establishment or a member of the gerontocracy, but you can’t be both.

Fourth, this is Maine. There are lots of Democrats who could have run. Schumer didn’t need to clear the field for Mills. I’ve made similar complaints about the DSCC putting its thumb on the scale in Michigan (disclosure: I support McMorrow in that race) and Iowa.

Finally, maybe Platner is the right candidate, maybe he isn’t, but ultimately he’s the nominee because the people of Maine overwhelmingly preferred him to Mills.

I am once again asking about Colorado’s 8th district. It’s supposed to be the most competitive district in the country but I have not heard much about it

Not sure if you’re based in Colorado or this district. If so, I’m surprised (and maybe a bit concerned) that you haven’t heard anything about it. I can’t say it’s THE most competitive district in the country, but it’s a huge pickup opportunity for Democrats. Trump won the district by only 1.8% in 2024, making incumbent Republican Gabe Evans, who beat Democrat Yadira Caraveo, one of the most vulnerable incumbents in the party.

Perhaps one reason you haven’t heard much is that Democrats have a primary that won’t be decided for a few weeks.

Dan, if Democrats win the House and Senate in 2026 but not the presidency, we’ll have leverage without real governing power—able to investigate and block, but not pass major legislation.

Politically, that feels like a trap: if we use that power aggressively, do we reinforce the “Washington is broken” narrative and get blamed for dysfunction? How do we reframe “we’re blocking them” into “we’re preventing damage”?

At the same time, how do we manage expectations among our own voters, who want results, when big wins are unlikely until 2028?

In that scenario, what does “success” actually look like for Democrats over those two years?

I’m not overly worried about managing expectations, but given the very real limits of our power, it’s important that Democratic leaders be as transparent as possible about their strategy, goals, and the limits of what can be accomplished. This will mean having some tough conversations with our base, which is something the leadership has tried to avoid in the past.

Success to me looks like the following:

  • Using investigative and oversight authorities to expose the corruption and incompetence within the Trump Administration.

  • Stopping Trump from filling key judicial vacancies. This is a place where Democrats cannot go weak for the sake of norms, procedure, and Senatorial etiquette.

  • In the House, passing an agenda that addresses higher costs and Trumpian corruption, which the Senate should force Republicans to vote down.

  • Using their newfound leverage to increase funding for Democratic priorities and force real reforms on ICE and other programs.

IF [and it’s a big IF] we take Senate and House, can we expand SCOTUS add term limits, and enforceable ethics?

Speaking of the limits to our power, we can’t do that until we have the Senate, House, and White House, hopefully in 2029.

Why isn’t there more anger and blame directed at Republicans? I get that Democrats need to find their spines and grow a (few) pairs. But we seem to have lost sight of the fact that things are shitty because of Republicans, NOT Democrats.

I suspect this question (and others like it) refers to the anger that I and many others have shown toward the DNC, the Bidens, and other failures of the Democratic leadership.

I agree we need to keep things in perspective and recognize the true threat. This is why we all need to come together, regardless of who wins the divisive primaries in Michigan, Iowa, and elsewhere.

But I also think we want to hold our side accountable and use our leverage to ensure they’re doing what needs to be done to address that threat.

For the voters, at least, that anger is very clearly being directed at Republicans. Hence, Trump’s abysmal approval rating and Democratic performance in nearly every election since 2024.

Should a big emphasis for 2026 be to persuade likely Republican voters to stay home and not vote? Persuading to vote Dem is hard, but there seems to be a despair among many Republicans that could be encouraged/strengthened.

Emphasize the truly grotesque aspects of the Trump Administration to draw the link that voting for a Republican candidate means that you are self-identifying with the worst aspects of a Trump-dominated party.

I always like to think about these things in terms of raw math. A Republican deciding to stay home is a net gain of one vote for our side. Persuading them to also vote for a Democrat is a net gain of two, so twice as impactful.

But even if we focused on getting Republicans to stay home, I’m not sure adopting that approach would change our strategy much. I view persuasion as a two-step process. First, you persuade them to leave their side, and then you persuade them to join ours. You have to do step one to accomplish step two.

That said, there should be some discrete efforts to divide and sow discontent among the MAGA base, by focusing on Trump’s broken promises on the Iran War and the Epstein files. Democrats should also use clips of MAGA personalities like Tucker Carlson and Megyn Kelly criticizing Trump in ads targeted at the GOP base.

Dan - question about Mallory McMorrow, what is driving your support for her? I’ve been following the Michigan Senate race and I don’t find her impressive. She consistently flip flops, she looks uncomfortable when speaking unscripted. Her plan seems to be try and triangulate between Stevens and Al Sayed and it looks forced. She doesn’t seem authentic.

Do you find her to be the most electable person in that race?

Are you working with / for her? That’s fine if that’s the answer!

Are you friends with her?

Genuinely curious what you see in her as a candidate.

My general approach is to be officially neutral in Democratic primaries, but to be clear about my views on the candidates’ strengths and weaknesses. Michigan is the exception this cycle, where I am explicitly supporting Mallory McMorrow and hosted a fundraiser for her. I keep mentioning that because I want to be transparent with you.

As you can imagine, I have a very different impression of her and have been a big fan, both as a person and as a political talent, for a very long time. But I don’t want to use this newsletter to convince you to support her over the other candidates. My support of her is in my personal capacity, if you will.

I do want to clear something up. I am not working for her (or any candidate other than the downballot candidates who have subscribed to Message Box Pro) and would certainly never have a financial relationship with a candidate or organization without disclosing it to my readers.

Writing For My Life

Dan, is Crooked Media still hosting a debate between the MI Senate candidates? Do you know? And do you know if it has been scheduled yet?

Answer

Yes. It was scheduled for a couple of weeks ago but had to be rescheduled because of votes in the House. I’m not intimately involved in the details, but my understanding is we’re working on a new date with the campaigns.

Okay, that’s it for this week. Leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments below.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: The Democratic Trust Deficit
item.description: It’s like they never left. The Bidens are back in the news. Jill Biden has a book out this week where she tries to set the record straight about the debate, President Biden’s health, and his decision to run again. In an interview with CBS News, Jill Biden said the following about her reaction to…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
It’s like they never left.

The Bidens are back in the news. Jill Biden has a book out this week where she tries to set the record straight about the debate, President Biden’s health, and his decision to run again.

In an interview with CBS News, Jill Biden said the following about her reaction to the debate.

I don’t know what happened. As I watched it, I thought, ‘Oh, my God, he’s having a stroke.’ And it scared me to death.
