When Sara Gideon lost to Susan Collins last time I recall you saying that bashing Collins wasn’t enough. You had to convince long time Collins voters that it was OK to leave her. You called this creating a “permission structure” and I wondered if you see Democratic campaigns doing this any better this time around?

I’m frankly impressed you remember that from 6/1000 years ago. The idea of a permission structure is about understanding what’s preventing voters from coming aboard for a candidate they otherwise like, and then crafting strategies and messages that factor those hesitations in. This was a big part of the Obama 2008 campaign. When we started, our data showed that Democratic primary voters universally loved Obama, but many had hesitations about supporting someone so young and lacking a traditional resume. For Black voters in particular, there were electability concerns. Would America really elect a Black man President of the United States?

So on that campaign, we thought about what we could do every day to give these voters permission to do what their hearts wanted and support Obama. One example was often highlighting the support Obama had from generals and other experienced military leaders. We also knew that the best way to win Black voters in South Carolina was to win the lily-white Iowa caucuses. That win served as proof that Obama could win over a sufficient number of white voters.

This cycle, I see a lot of candidates running in red states and districts doing a good job giving permission to disenchanted Republicans to vote for a Democrat. James Talarico and Mary Peltola are two very good examples.

Hi Dan! Trying to figure out best strategy to ensure we don’t get two Republicans on the ballot in November in the CA gubernatorial race. Wait until the last couple days, see which Dem is ahead in the polls, and vote for that candidate? Or am I irrationally worried about this possibility in the first place?

As a California resident, I get this question from people all day every day. It comes up at any gathering — kids’ soccer games, barbeques, school pick-up. Fairly or unfairly, people are generally unsatisfied with their options and worried about the nightmare of California stumbling ass-backward into a Republican governor.

The odds of a lockout have come way down with Xavier Becerra’s surge and Steve Hilton getting more of the Republican vote thanks to Trump’s truly bizarre decision to endorse Hilton. But the possibility still exists.

In the most recent Emerson poll, the two Republican candidates are getting 28% of the vote. Republicans tend to get more than 30% in a jungle primary, which implies that a decent amount of the undecided vote is Republicans who will go to either Hilton or Bianco.

Since becoming the frontrunner, Becerra has come under withering attack. If he starts to drop and his support allocates evenly between Steyer, Porter, and Mahan — or goes to someone else — that would increase the chance of a lockout.

I generally like to mail in my ballot as soon as possible just to ensure it gets counted, and if something bizarre happens, I can fill out a provisional ballot. This time I’m going to wait a week or two to see how things shake out.

I’m 24 and want to engage with politics and form my own opinions but the sheer volume of news and daily chaos makes it feel impossible to form a nuanced opinion about anything. How do you stay informed without being overwhelmed and develop your own opinions? What approach would you take to following the news if it wasn’t your job?

Answer

This is a really tough question because I haven’t been a civilian news consumer since the advent of the internet. For much of my professional life, there have been people whose job it was to read the news and send around the highlights and key stories. And now that I’m at Crooked Media, there are a team of very smart producers who help flag stories for me. I’m also in several group chats filled with political operatives, junkies, and fellow media people who are constantly sending around stories of interest.

I also know that following the news has never been harder, because even with all of that support, I sometimes struggle to keep up with everything.

In general, here would be my advice.

First, sign up for several newsletters like Politico Playbook, Axios AM, and of course Crooked Media’s What a Day. These publications read the news for you and help sort the wheat from the chaff. Each of them brings their biases to the curation process, but it’s at least a starting point to get a handle on what’s most important. Plus they’re free.

Second, despite all of their flaws, I still think the New York Times is the best journalism in the world. If you can afford a subscription, it’s a great way to understand what’s happening in the U.S. and the world.

Finally, have a diverse media diet. Whether in writing, on social, or on YouTube, don’t be afraid to listen to voices with whom you disagree. You have to actively seek them out, because the algorithms want to send you only things you already agree with.

It’s not easy, so good luck!

Okay, that’s it for this week. Have a great weekend, and leave your questions for next week in the comments.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Trump's Brand Was Strength. It's Gone.
item.description: Most of what Donald Trump does and says is pure demented idiocy born of narcissism, a historically incurious mind, and a media diet rich in conspiracy theories and hagiography. But for all of Trump’s stupidity, he does have one core political insight that has fueled his rise to power: strength is…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Most of what Donald Trump does and says is pure demented idiocy born of narcissism, a historically incurious mind, and a media diet rich in conspiracy theories and hagiography.

But for all of Trump’s stupidity, he does have one core political insight that has fueled his rise to power: strength is the currency of American politics.

Voters want strong leaders who can protect them from threats, domestic and foreign, and stand up to what they increasingly see as a corrupt and broken political system.

As Bill Clinton famously said:

When people feel uncertain, they’d rather have somebody who’s strong and wrong than somebody who’s weak and right.

Trump embodies strong and wrong to so many voters. It’s an image he cultivates. I think Trump truly wants to be a dictator. He is genuinely envious of dictators like Xi, Putin, and Erdogan. But Trump also acts like a strongman because it communicates strength to voters. And because it baits Democrats into attacking him as a dangerous strongman, which also communicates strength to voters.

Trump loves to call his opponents weak. “Low energy Jeb.” “Sleepy Joe.” “Lil Marco.” He even called the Pope “weak on crime.”

Trump rode his strongman image to two terms in the White House using the classic authoritarian playbook. He is often described as a master brand marketer, and Trump’s political brand was strength.

He began his second term astride the global stage. More popular than he had ever been. Reams of political capital.

Well, that’s all gone now.

Trump is no longer seen as strong and decisive, and that has big implications for American politics.

This is Trump’s emperor-has-no-clothes moment. And it’s the end of the Trump era as we have known it.

Strength matters to voters because it’s the character trait they most closely associate with success. It’s not just being tough enough to take on dangerous enemies abroad and protect Americans from threats at home — although that’s part of it. Voters especially value presidential strength at times of uncertainty and chaos.

But it’s bigger than that.

Americans (for good reason) think the political system is awash in money and powerful special interests who will fight like hell to protect the status quo and their own power. If you aren’t strong, you can’t pass health care reform, raise the minimum wage, or do anything else on their agenda.

Kamala Harris lost because she was the Vice President to an unpopular president at a time of high inflation. Voters thought Trump would do a better job of lowering costs, in part because they believed he was stronger. Of course, strength in politics plays into all sorts of misogynistic tropes, and that certainly played a role in both of Trump’s presidential wins.

But Trump’s brand as a strong leader also explains why his loss to Biden was so narrow despite the raging pandemic — a pandemic Trump worsened by failing to prepare and proving incapable of managing. In the 2020 exit polls, “strong leader” was the most important quality for 33% of voters, more than any other trait. Trump won those voters 50-44.

The candidate seen as stronger usually wins. It took a once-in-a-century pandemic to prevent it.

In 2024, strength once again powered Trump’s victory. The exit polls gave voters four candidate qualities and asked which was the most important to their vote. Among those who chose “has the ability to lead,” Trump won 66-33. Among those who chose “can bring needed change,” Trump won 72-28.

On Inauguration Day 2025, at the peak of his political popularity, 64% of Americans in the Economist/YouGov poll called Trump a strong leader.

Those days are long gone.

Nothing sums up Trump’s political problems like a recent TIPP poll: only 38% of voters — and 30% of independents — describe his leadership as strong. A plurality, 45%, now call it weak. Among independents, it’s 48%.

The candidate whose brand was strength is now seen as weak.

A lot of armchair pundits attribute this shift to the TACO phenomenon — the idea that Trump Always Chickens Out and backs down from a fight despite all the bullying tweets and bravado. That’s part of it, for sure. And making the United States look impotent in the war with Iran doesn’t help. By a 2-to-1 ratio, Americans say the military action has been unsuccessful rather than successful. 61% say it was a mistake. The war Trump promised would be quick and decisive is now as unpopular as Iraq in 2006 and Vietnam in the early 1970s.

If TACO and Iran were the whole story, Trump could repair his political situation by stopping serving tacos and putting the war behind him.

But something bigger is going on. A problem Trump can’t really solve.

He looks small and weak on the world stage. He seems incapable of reining in the chaos and, in fact, is constantly creating more of it. And perhaps most damningly, Trump didn’t take on the broken system like he promised. He became part of it.

Failure evinces weakness. And Trump’s second term has been one constant failure after another.

This is a dramatic shift. For a decade, we have been arguing with voters about whether Trump is dangerous, whether he is dishonest, and whether he is unfit. We were right on all of it, and we lost anyway. Because voters thought he was strong, and too often, strong beats right.

But strength is now up for grabs. The Trump era is over. Democrats have an opportunity and an obligation to redefine strength in a way that is consistent with our values — and a rebuke to the tinpot dictator who has driven America into the ground.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: A quick note about Message Box Pro
item.description: A few weeks ago, I launched Message Box Pro — a members-only consulting service for people working in politics and communications at any level. Subscribers get weekly strategy memos, data-driven messaging insights, polling analysis, guidance on communicating in this chaotic media environment, and…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
A few weeks ago, I launched Message Box Pro — a members-only consulting service for people working in politics and communications at any level.

Subscribers get weekly strategy memos, data-driven messaging insights, polling analysis, guidance on communicating in this chaotic media environment, and regular office hours where I answer your questions.

I wanted to reach out because we’re down to the last handful of founding memberships — and I don’t want you to miss out if this is something you’ve been thinking about.

A quick reminder of what that means: founding members lock in $49.99/month forever. Once we reach 250 members, the price increases to $65/month for everyone who joins after that.

If you’ve been on the fence, here’s where things stand a few weeks in:

  • How to develop a campaign message

  • A comprehensive midterm narrative built around costs, chaos, and corruption

  • Where swing voters are actually getting their information

  • Why Democrats now have their biggest opening on the economy in 16 years

  • How to beat the GOP’s strategy for the midterms

I built MBP for exactly the people who are in the fight — candidates, staffers, organizers, and anyone who wants to sharpen how they communicate in this chaotic media environment.

The founding memberships are going quickly, and I want to make sure you have a chance to sign up before they’re gone.

If you have any questions, reply to this email and I'll get back to you right away. And if you know anyone who might find Message Box Pro valuable, I'd really appreciate it if you could pass this along.

Thank you, Dan

item.title: What's More Accurate, Prediction Markets or Polls?
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. Lots of great questions this week, so lets get right into it. A quick note: these mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags. And don’t forget to leave your questions…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. Lots of great questions this week, so lets get right into it.

A quick note: these mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

And don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments.

What’s your take on the accuracy and value of prediction markets vs. traditional polling? And should we be wary of prediction markets affecting outcomes, either by driving campaign strategies or influencing voter behavior?

I get some version of this question all the time (including from Bethany on multiple occasions!). To date, I have resisted putting it in a mailbag because of the topic’s complexity and importance.

But there is no time like the present (and as I have said many times before, the subscribers are always right).

There has been an inherent and understandable skepticism about polls for the last decade, particularly among Democrats. The polling misled us to think that Trump couldn’t win in 2016 and that Biden would romp in 2020. In both cases, the polls underestimated Trump’s support.

So it’s understandable that people are looking for a more accurate alternative. But are prediction markets like Kalshi and Polymarket the answer? Are they more accurate or even as accurate as polls?

The polls vs. prediction markets question is based on something of a false premise. Polls and prediction markets measure different things and serve different purposes. Polls are an inherently imperfect measure of public opinion at a snapshot in time. They are not supposed to be predictive. They are not designed to tell us what is going to happen. A poll on October 1st that says Trump is winning by 3 points is not a prediction that Trump is going to win by 3 points when the votes are counted a month later.

Prediction markets, as their name suggests, are supposed to lay out the odds of a binary outcome. Once again, they are not telling you what is going to happen — they are telling you what the markets think the odds are that something will happen. In other words, how likely or unlikely it is to happen. The market is weighted by money and shaped by all the collective wisdom and data that leads people to place wagers. Importantly, polling is one of the most important pieces of data that shape the prediction markets for political outcomes. Polymarket and Kalshi odds can shift dramatically based on newly released polling. So moving to prediction markets as a way of looking at possible political outcomes does not solve the problem of bad polls. In fact, it may exacerbate it, because the expert analysis and punditry that also shape the odds are themselves based, in part, on that same polling.

The argument for prediction markets is based on the Wisdom of the Crowds theory, which argues that the aggregated judgments of a large, diverse, and independent group will, on average, be more accurate than the judgment of any individual expert within it. The problem with prediction markets is that one trader can move the market significantly with a single massive bet. This happened in 2024, when a single French trader placed roughly $30 million in pro-Trump bets, which shifted the odds significantly toward Trump. Even James Surowiecki, who came up with the wisdom of the crowds theory, has been skeptical that prediction markets as currently constructed truly reflect the wisdom of the crowds.

The track record is more mixed than the post-2024 narrative suggests. Markets badly missed Brexit in 2016 and had Hillary Clinton at 70-85% to win that same year. In 2024, Polymarket had Trump around 60% while polling averages showed a coin flip. Trump won, but by only 1.5 points. In other words, the market got the winner right but overstated the certainty.

Prediction markets are all the rage. You can’t open up a social media app without seeing ads for them. Kalshi seems to have spent a ton of money on influencer campaigns to make it seem like everyone is betting on them all the time. In addition to the obvious risk of corruption and insider trading, I worry about prediction markets having an undue effect on the political process. I don’t mean to dismiss the corruption — it’s a massive problem that needs immediate regulation, but that’s a different discussion than this one.

Just like the polls, the shifting odds can influence the behavior of various stakeholders, including the media, donors, and voters. Now to be fair, the polls have a similar effect. The once highly touted Des Moines Register poll used to be a seismic event in the Iowa caucuses. The candidate who was leading or beat expectations would get a massive influx of momentum, while candidates who did worse than expected could be pronounced DOA on the spot.

In the end, I don’t think this is an either/or proposition. Look at polls and prediction markets. Understand their differing purposes and methodologies, and then draw your own conclusions.

Dan, I know it is somewhat early to be thinking about 2028, but, with the reality (which you carefully explained in Message Box Pro) that the voters we need to persuade get their news from alternative sources (YouTube, etc.), how can we think of Kamala Harris as a serious candidate when last time she did not appear on the biggest alternative sources of “news”? Can we overlook that as a lack of insight as to the dynamics of contemporary media (I don’t buy that - - PSA advocated for the “everywhere all at once” strategy), or, is it more of a personal discomfort with settings that do not have the guardrails of a formal debate or courtroom? If it is the latter, that is a bigger issue. Interested your thoughts, please.

Looking back on 2024, it’s clear that Trump won the media battle against Harris, and he did so quite handily. His strategy was more aggressive, more innovative, and much more effective. Trump was both omnipresent and made sure his media was targeted to the voters he needed most (young, less engaged, mostly men).

If we are being fair, Kamala Harris did do some smart things, including interviews with Call Her Daddy, Brené Brown, the All the Smoke Podcast, and Shannon Sharpe. And she did make a real attempt to get on Joe Rogan.

However, there’s no question that Harris was overly risk-averse and spent too much time and energy on traditional media outlets that didn’t reach the voters she needed to reach. I am sympathetic to the argument that she was under massive time constraints because of the compressed nature of her campaign. Subsequent reporting has suggested that Joe Biden made it very difficult for her to separate from him, which made doing any interviews more fraught. Hence the disaster on The View that haunted the campaign.

Regarding 2028, did she learn from her experience, and is she willing to do things differently? Can she take more risks and adopt an everywhere, all at once media strategy?

If the answers to those questions are yes, she would be a formidable primary candidate. If not... well, you know the answer.

Do you recall Dem Senate primary candidates being asked about support for the Senate Leader in past cycles? Could this mean that disapproval of Schumer (in his own right and as an avatar for the Dem establishment & gerontocracy problem) has spread beyond Dem political junkies and become a concern among non-activist Dem voters? And if so, are the DSCC and DCCC taking notice?

In the past, I have seen some moderate candidates running in redder districts pledge to vote against Pelosi as speaker, and I have seen conservative GOPers promise to vote against various GOP speakers like Boehner and Ryan. However, I have never seen it on the Senate side, nor have I seen it to this degree.

Thus far, an endorsement from Schumer has been a burden rather than an asset. It was a problem for Janet Mills in Maine. In both Michigan and Iowa, the candidates endorsed by Schumer or his allies have struggled to answer questions about whether they would support him as leader. The Schumer question came up in the Iowa Senate debate earlier this week.

There are some specific bones to pick with Schumer. Some of the complaints are fair, others are unfair, but there’s no question that for many, Schumer has come to embody everyone’s frustration with the Democratic Party establishment and the gerontocracy.

The DCCC just endorsed some candidates in tough primaries. The non-endorsed candidates are trying to use the snub as validation of their anti-establishment credentials. I will be watching to see if this is a Democratic Party leadership problem or just a Schumer issue.

Hey Dan - curious about your thoughts on Adam Hamilton announcing he’s running as a democrat for Roger Marshall’s Senate seat in Kansas. He is the senior pastor of the largest Methodist church in the country and is very well-known in the Kansas City area. Kansas has a democratic governor (who got re-elected) and voted almost 60-40 to protect abortion access. I think he may have a real shot here.

I need to look more carefully at the race. Adam Hamilton is an interesting candidate. Trump won Kansas by 16 points, so it would take a massive swing for Hamilton to prevail. His profile is one of a candidate who could pull some crossover votes from previous Trump supporters. The question is whether he can pull enough.

I will keep an eye on it.

Is there any recent polling in the Florida races for senate and governor? Is there any chance Vindman and Jolly can win here? We can use some good news here given the latest Desantis shenanigans

Emerson did a Florida poll at the end of March, and here are the results.

While the Republicans lead, they are underperforming. On paper, this is a state where Democrats could have a shot. In 2024, Trump did about the same in Florida as he did in Ohio, which is very much in play this cycle. His 2024 Florida win was powered by historic gains with Latinos, who have turned on Trump with ferocity since the election. But the Democratic brand in Florida is in tatters, so it's going to be an uphill climb.

Hi, Dan - Are there *any* efforts being made to put guard rails in place so that, post-Trump, no other President will be able to take actions like withholding appropriated funds to government agencies, destroying Presidential records, refusing to release their taxes, put their businesses in a blind trust...the list goes on. Or are we just gonna continue to be naive?

I believe this will be part of three different efforts — the legislative agenda of the Democratic majority in 2027, the policy agendas of several presidential candidates in 2028, and the think tanks working on Project 2025-like efforts.

The question will be whether Democrats will summon the political will to enact them if and when they have power. Political capital is not unlimited, and they will have to make some tough decisions.

In a nod to WWHL—Dan Pfeiffer, as an expert in messaging, what is the best way Amanda and West can move forward?

If you are unsure what this question is about, please feel free to skip it. It’s a reference to THE SCANDAL in the reality TV world. Like many Summer House fans, I am anxiously awaiting the reunion. The best advice I could give both of them is to apologize to the people they hurt, say they never planned for this to happen, but that they have genuine feelings for each other.

Take accountability and fall on your swords. Don’t be defensive, don’t be combative. You have already lost the PR war (for good reason), so please acknowledge that and accept it with grace.

The text message that West sent KJ, which was read live on WWHL this week, is a very good approach.

Okay, that's it for this week. Hope you have a great weekend and a happy Mother's Day. Leave your questions for next week in the comments below.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: The Virginia Ruling Was a Gut Punch. Democrats Can Still Win the House
item.description: Like me, you were probably going about your Friday morning feeling bad about the world but good about Democratic prospects this fall when the Virginia Supreme Court dropped a giant flaming bag of poop into your lap (apologies for that vivid metaphor). This morning, the Virginia Supreme Court struck…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Like me, you were probably going about your Friday morning feeling bad about the world but good about Democratic prospects this fall when the Virginia Supreme Court dropped a giant flaming bag of poop into your lap (apologies for that vivid metaphor).

This morning, the Virginia Supreme Court struck down the referendum that passed two weeks ago, which would have allowed the state to redraw the congressional map to give Democrats four new seats. After it passed, there was much crowing by Democrats about how the GOP’s efforts to rig the midterm map had failed, and that Democrats had successfully fought back. We seemed to be on a glide path to winning the House with relative ease.

Well, since that moment, it’s been all bad news.

First, Florida redrew its map in an attempt to add four Republican seats. Then, the Supreme Court gutted the Voting Rights Act, essentially legalizing racial gerrymandering and giving permission to Republicans to eliminate Democratic districts drawn to be majority-minority. And now, the Virginia decision has made a bad situation worse.

After all of this insanity, where do things stand in the battle for the House? Are Democrats still favored, or have Republicans successfully rigged the midterms despite a brutal political environment and a woefully unpopular President?

Prior to the U.S. and Virginia Supreme Court decisions, the redistricting battles were essentially a draw.

Now that Democrats have lost the four new seats in Virginia and Republicans are drawing new districts across the South, things look very different.

Tennessee passed a new map yesterday that nets Republicans a seat, and Louisiana is on track to do the same in the coming days. South Carolina, Alabama, and Mississippi might also redraw their maps.

As this chart from the Cook Political Report makes clear, the best-case scenario for Republicans is that they net 11 seats from redistricting.

Prior to all of these shenanigans, Democrats only needed to pick up three seats to flip the House. In this scenario, we would need to pick up 15.

A much higher hurdle, for sure.

I’m not going to try to tell you that any of this is good news. It’s not. It sucks, and it contributes to the idea that the system is rigged against anyone trying to take on Trump and his rich and powerful friends.

But it may not be as bad as it looks.

First, it’s not clear that all of the Southern states will be able to redraw. There are caveats and complications. Alabama is under a federal court order that prevents mid-decade redistricting. Mississippi has already held its primaries. And South Carolina is already so gerrymandered that there is some risk of a redraw blowing up in Republicans’ faces.

Second, it’s not clear that Republicans will really net four seats from Florida. The new seats are not all safe Republican seats — they’re the kind of seats that could go Democratic with the right candidate in this political environment.

Finally, even under the old maps, Democrats will pick up one and maybe two seats in Virginia. Democrats are favored to win the second district, which Trump carried by just two-tenths of a point in 2024, and the first district might also be in play.

Look, this is a big blow, and I’m not going to sugarcoat it, but Democrats can still win the House and might even be slight favorites. We currently have a 6-point advantage in the generic ballot.

Democrats have been outperforming Kamala Harris’s 2024 numbers by about 13 points in special elections and maintain a sizable enthusiasm advantage going forward. Democrats are fired up. Republicans are divided and depressed. These court rulings don’t change the basic reality that the American people think Trump and the Republicans are doing a terrible job, are focused on all of the wrong things, and want a change in Washington.

The hurdle is higher, but Democrats can absolutely still get over it. This sucks, but all is not lost.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: How Worried Should You Be About the DNC?
item.description: In my overly long political career, the Democratic National Committee has always been a bit of a mess. Running an umbrella organization with too many responsibilities and not enough power is never easy. Its leadership serves at the pleasure of the more than 400 DNC members, including state and…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
In my overly long political career, the Democratic National Committee has always been a bit of a mess. Running an umbrella organization with too many responsibilities and not enough power is never easy. Its leadership serves at the pleasure of the more than 400 DNC members, including state and local chairs, activists, and the people in big hats and buttons you see dancing at the convention every four years.

There is always some level of worry about whether the DNC is up to the task, but I’ve never seen it as bad as it is right now.

The concern about Ken Martin’s stewardship went from bubbling under the surface among activists, operatives, and electeds to a roiling online conversation after Martin’s viral interview with my Pod Save America cohost Jon Favreau.

Jon and Martin went back and forth over two major sources of concern. The first is the DNC’s refusal to release the autopsy of the 2024 election. When Martin ran for chair, he promised to conduct and publicly release a report on what happened in 2024. He even criticized the previous DNC leadership for not releasing an autopsy after 2016. At some point last year, after the report was reportedly completed, Martin changed his mind and decided not to release it. You can listen to his explanation in the interview to see if it’s compelling. Many others and I did not.

The second issue is the DNC’s fundraising. As CNN reported last month:

An already massive gap between Republican National Committee and Democratic National Committee finances widened in February, according to new reports filed with the Federal Election Commission.

The RNC out-raised the DNC in February, $18.5 million to $10.3 million. And entering March, the RNC reported nearly seven times as much cash on hand, $109 million to $15.9 million.

The DNC has more outstanding debt, $17.4 million, than cash on hand, keyed to a $15 million loan the committee took out last October.

The DNC has done pretty well with grassroots fundraising, which is great. But many of the major donors who typically fund the DNC have refused to give. Some have specific complaints about Martin; many others simply don’t trust the DNC after the 2024 election. Either way, Martin has struggled to match the Republican fundraising machine. He has also spent a lot of money early, hence the low cash on hand. I generally agree that early investments are better than hoarding money to the end, but a 7-to-1 cash disadvantage is suboptimal to say the least.

Concerns with Martin’s leadership have reached the point that, according to The Bulwark’s Lauren Egan:

Some DNC members have privately discussed trying to force Martin out of the job, according to three people familiar with these conversations. The idea was put on hold after members failed to identify an alternative candidate willing to step into the role.

I have heard from a shocking number of people about the DNC. I initially resisted writing about this because I thought Jon’s interview spoke for itself, but with the midterms less than six months away, people are quite concerned that a poorly run DNC low on cash could cost the party in November.

Here’s the thing, though: the midterms aren’t actually what you should be worried about. The DNC plays almost no role there. The real stakes are 2028 — and on that front, the situation is worse than people realize.

Let me explain.

People love to yell at the DNC for everything that is wrong with the party. When the message sucks, they yell at the DNC. When the wrong candidates run, and the right ones don’t, it’s the DNC’s fault. They hold the DNC accountable for bills that don’t pass, bills that aren’t considered, and unpopular compromises on Capitol Hill.

The DNC does none of those things. They don’t recruit candidates. They play almost no role in the party’s message and have no influence on legislative strategy.

The DNC is an organizing body that works with state parties. It’s also the hub for a lot of the technological and data assets that campaigns at all levels rely on. It plays a bigger role when a Democratic President is in the White House, because it houses the President’s political operation.

But it plays almost no role in the midterms. They don’t recruit candidates, run campaigns, advise on message, or air ads — not when compared to the DCCC, DSCC, their associated Super PACs, and the campaigns themselves.

A superbly run, well-funded DNC would at best be helpful on the margins in 2026, and a poorly run, cash-poor DNC will only hurt on the margins.

One of the responses to Jon’s interview from Martin defenders has been something along the lines of “Sure, the DNC should have released the autopsy, but who really cares. It’s not like it was going to be the Rosetta Stone.”

There’s some truth to that. There is a lot of speculation that the real reason Martin didn’t release the autopsy is that the work product sucked. He tasked a crony to do it, and many of the key people who would have a lot to offer say they were never interviewed. I don’t know if that’s true, but it does explain why Martin is being so stubborn about an issue that was core to his campaign for chair.

The reason Martin breaking his promise matters is that we really need Democratic voters to trust the DNC.

We are about to embark on a highly consequential, and likely contentious, presidential primary, in which the DNC will play a major role. They set the primary calendar, decide how delegates are awarded, sponsor the debates, and put on the convention. That task is even more complicated this year, because the primary calendar is completely up in the air. After multiple campaigns where the order of the contests was Iowa, New Hampshire, Nevada, and South Carolina, the DNC is tasked with coming up with a whole new plan. After the Iowa Caucuses were a debacle in 2020, the DNC, under Biden’s direction, moved South Carolina to the front of the calendar as a show of gratitude for the role the state played in Biden’s nomination and to ward off potential primary challenges. When the DNC made that change, it made it for one cycle only.

The order of the first four contests will play a major role in dictating the nominee. How and when the debates are scheduled, and which candidates get on stage, will also be significant questions.

If no one trusts the DNC, no one will trust the process set up by the DNC. And that will make it harder to unite the party around the eventual nominee. We got a preview of this in 2016, when leaked emails from DNC leadership showed a clear preference for Hillary Clinton over Bernie Sanders. To this day, Sanders supporters still complain that the process was rigged.

Martin’s refusal to offer a coherent explanation about the autopsy and his defaulting to tired talking points exacerbate that trust problem.

Ultimately, that’s why all of this matters.

Share

item.title: How Trump Turned His Ballroom into a Midterm Gift for Democrats
item.description: How leaders respond in moments of crisis tells you everything about their values and priorities. After the shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, President Trump went to the White House briefing room to address the press and the nation. In that moment, Trump could have called for…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
How leaders respond in moments of crisis tells you everything about their values and priorities.

After the shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, President Trump went to the White House briefing room to address the press and the nation.

In that moment, Trump could have called for national unity or decried the disturbing rise in political violence. He could have talked about the need for everyone to ratchet down the political rhetoric. I know this is crazy, but he could have even called for changes to our gun laws.

He did none of those things.

Instead, Trump used the moment when he had the nation’s attention — and the sympathy and concern of many people who disagree with him — to push for the construction of the White House ballroom.

After the press conference, Trump posted the following on Truth Social:

What happened last night is exactly the reason that our great Military, Secret Service, Law Enforcement and, for different reasons, every President for the last 150 years, have been DEMANDING that a large, safe, and secure Ballroom be built ON THE GROUNDS OF THE WHITE HOUSE. This event would never have happened with the Militarily Top Secret Ballroom currently under construction at the White House. It cannot be built fast enough! While beautiful, it has every highest level security feature there is plus, there are no rooms sitting on top for unsecured people to pour in, and is inside the gates of the most secure building in the World, The White House. The ridiculous Ballroom lawsuit, brought by a woman walking her dog, who has absolutely No Standing to bring such a suit, must be dropped, immediately. Nothing should be allowed to interfere with its construction, which is on budget and substantially ahead of schedule!!! Thank you for your attention to this matter. President DONALD J. TRUMP

This wasn’t just a rattled, addled President shooting from the hip. This was a concerted White House PR campaign to use the shooting to argue for Trump’s ballroom construction project, which is currently tied up in court. The White House maintains group chats with MAGA influencers and tells them what to post about (this is not unusual; Democratic politicians do the same thing). At the exact same moment on Saturday night, all of these MAGA accounts started posting the same message calling for the construction of the ballroom.

Trump’s obsession with his ballroom is bizarre. Given all the problems in the world and Trump’s political challenges at home, taking every opportunity possible to wax about a home renovation project is an odd choice, to say the least.

Trump’s ballroom fixation also helps explain why his approval is at an all-time low and continuing to sink.

Seriously.

It’s emblematic of all of his political problems.

Here’s why.

This is not the most important point, but Trump’s ballroom is not a solution to the problem of political violence. It’s one of those arguments that seems like it makes sense at first but can’t withstand three nanoseconds of scrutiny.

First, I’ll defer to others on the specific security setup at the dinner. It’s been many years since I’ve been there, but the setup seems similar to what I remember. The ballroom is downstairs, and the main security perimeter is well before people get close to it — not at the main lobby of the hotel. It’s a massive hotel, with guests in town for the dinner but for other reasons too. The main level hosts a series of receptions that include people who don’t have a ticket to the dinner itself. The gunman didn’t penetrate the security perimeter or get close to the dignitaries. Every event has a security perimeter, and someone can always bring a gun to that perimeter. That’s true at the White House too, where someone could have a gun outside the gates and start shooting. Building a ballroom doesn’t fix that problem.

Second, let’s say it did fix the problem. Are Trump and his allies arguing that he will never leave the White House? Will all of his events be on campus? Is Trump just going to hole up in the White House and never leave? (Maybe?) The dinner is not a White House event. It’s hosted by the White House Correspondents’ Association, the group that represents the reporters who cover the White House. Even if the ballroom were built, the dinner wouldn’t have been there.

It just makes no sense. It’s painfully stupid.

Sorry, I had to get that off my chest.

Trump’s political problems are myriad and growing. Everything that could be going wrong seems to be going wrong for Trump and Republicans. But if I had to boil it all down, there is a pretty simple explanation.

You’ve heard me say this a million times, but it’s still the most important factor in American politics: Trump ran on the promise to cut people’s costs, and he didn’t do it. In fact, he seems focused on everything other than lowering prices — ICE raids, wars in the Middle East, deposing Venezuelan dictators, lining his own pockets, and cutting people’s healthcare and closing rural hospitals.

Trump’s obsession with the ballroom fits in this category, but it also stands out because it’s so dumb and pointless. The last thing people care about is whether Presidents have sufficient space to wine and dine other elites.

A Washington Post poll released last week showed just how unpopular Trump’s ballroom is. Barely a quarter of Americans support it, and 56% oppose it.

Notably, only 18% of independents and 34% of non-MAGA Republicans support it.

Sometimes in politics, you need an evocative example to illustrate a larger point. In the 1980s, a commission sponsored by Ronald Reagan was tasked with looking for waste in military spending. It found that the Pentagon was paying $435 for a hammer, $600 for a toilet seat, and $7,000 for an aircraft coffee maker. Those examples made it easy to understand a wasteful and dysfunctional federal bureaucracy.

Trump’s ballroom can symbolize his focus on himself at the expense of everyone else. Building a massive, corporate-funded ballroom to host fancy parties — while Americans are paying $4 a gallon for gas, and while groceries, healthcare, and housing prices keep rising — is some real fiddling while Rome burns.

The ballroom is helping push down Trump’s approval rating, which is inherently bad for Republicans since presidential approval is at least somewhat correlated with midterm performance. But this was a Trump-specific problem that didn’t really bleed into the midterm campaign.

Then Republicans decided the political environment wasn’t bad enough.

Last week, Congressional Republicans made the problem worse by proposing that we spend $400 million in taxpayer money to build the ballroom. Rural hospitals are closing. Gas is over $4 a gallon. People are getting kicked off their healthcare — and Trump and the Republicans want to spend nearly half a billion dollars on a ballroom?

Democrats will be able to run ads hitting Republicans for spending taxpayer money on the ballroom instead of lowering prices. It fits the broader narrative of misplaced Republican priorities — tax cuts for the rich, forever wars in the Middle East, and ballrooms for political and moneyed elites.

This is a layup for Democrats, because we don’t even have to work that hard to highlight the ballroom.

Trump can’t shut up about it.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post.

item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box

item.title: Why the Press Is Harder on Democrats Than Republican
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. I have mostly recovered from my time in the swamp for the Crooked Media party during White House Correspondents Weekend. I was never planning to go to the dinner (haven’t been in more than a decade and doubt I ever will again), so I was nowhere near the legitimately…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. I have mostly recovered from my time in the swamp for the Crooked Media party during White House Correspondents Weekend. I was never planning to go to the dinner (haven’t been in more than a decade and doubt I ever will again), so I was nowhere near the legitimately scary situation that happened there.

Lots of great questions, so let’s get right into it.

A quick note: these mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

And don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments.

Considering the insane amount of ink spilled on Hunter Biden, why has the media basically accepted the fact that the Trump spawn are making obscene amounts of money off the government or that someone as inept, corrupt and compromised as Kushner is in charge of some pretty important ’peace’ talks?

Basically everyone just shrugs at the blatant corruption ‘cus Trump’ but when it comes to Kushner, how can anyone be sure he’s actually working in the interest of the US and not his personal funders?

Once again, elected reps, the media, voters all hold the two parties to extremely different standards.

This is a really hard and important question, because it gets at what has seemed so deeply unfair about the Trump era. There is undoubtedly a double standard, and the reasons why are helpful in understanding how the political media works.

There are three reasons the press holds Democrats to a much higher standard than Trump and the Republicans.

First, Trump has worn down the media. He is so corrupt in so many ways that the story has become old news. The folks at Popular Information looked at the coverage of Jared Kushner’s involvement in the Iran negotiations and found that only a fraction of the reports even mentioned his obvious conflicts of interest.

For the press, the story of Trump and his family being corrupt is old news. The New York Times and others still do deep investigative pieces uncovering the corruption, but those stories rarely make it into the daily coverage of the Trump administration. Trump, Karoline Leavitt, and other Trump surrogates are rarely pushed to answer tough questions about it, and when they are, they just feign outrage and never engage with the substance.

The second reason is that the press holds Democrats to a higher standard. This has always been sort of true, but it’s been particularly true in the Trump era. Reporters think Democratic voters care about whether their leaders are corrupt and Republican voters don’t. Therefore, a Democratic scandal could have bigger political implications, while a Republican scandal dies on the vine.

Third, most of the media is VERY sensitive to accusations that they are biased against Republicans. This is less true than it used to be, but most reporters are personally liberal on issues like abortion, guns, and climate. Some end up overcompensating by being tougher on Democrats.

Finally, Republicans do a better job of hammering these issues than Democrats. Plenty of Democrats have raised concerns about Don Jr. and Jared Kushner’s blatant corruption, but as a whole, we don’t do it often enough, loud enough, or in ways that break through. If and when Democrats have control of Congress, we will have additional tools to highlight the corruption for the world to see.

Saw Jon’s interview with Ken Martin. Isn’t he a recipe for continued disaster? Was cushioned for more of the same but worse even than expected.

Got a shocking number of questions about Jon Favreau’s interview with DNC chair Ken Martin. The interview was contentious to say the least and centered on two main issues: Martin’s refusal to release the autopsy report on the 2024 election that he promised to release when he ran for chair, and the DNC’s fundraising.

You should all watch the interview.

The interview speaks for itself, and Jon did an excellent job trying to pin Martin down, so I don’t need to pile on. Needless to say, I didn’t find Martin’s answers on the autopsy particularly compelling, and he seems unwilling to just answer the questions without defaulting to specious talking points. Ken screwed up. He promised to release the report and then didn’t, and instead of taking responsibility for that decision, he seems aghast that anyone would call him on it.

At the end of the day, the DNC is not as important as most think, but life would be easier if it were better run.

My question: with Trump’s polling numbers spiraling down, what is it going to take to get GOP members of Congress to break with him on the insanity, e.g. his nominees? My suspicion is fear of a primary, but it all seems suicidal to me.

Hey Dan, Hope you haven’t covered this previously but, WTF gives with Trump’s cabinet? They all have to know at this point that the loyalty is a one-way street. They’re all sure to be unceremoniously fired, having sold their souls, wrecked their reputations, and suffered abject disgrace in front of the world. For what? Joining the mile-high club on a luxury jet for half a year? Boozing it up in a locker room with the US men’s Olympic hockey team? Moving into military housing for 3 months? Point being: all of this graft is fleeting, so where do they go with their lives that made all this humiliation worth it (at least in their minds)?

-Loyal PSA subscriber, proud owner of an “It’s not great, Dan” t-shirt, and fellow Gen-Xer. Thanks for all you do!

I wanted to answer these questions together because Inherent in both is the assumption that most Republican members of Congress and most of Trump’s cabinet disagree with Trump substantively, dislike his style, or are uncomfortable with the blatant corruption.

That may have been true in his first term, but since 2016, the party has become much more MAGA. Most of them are perfectly comfortable with Trump doing what Trump does and stick by him out of loyalty and fear. Sure, there are exceptions, particularly in the Senate.

For those folks, it’s more than just a primary. The filing deadline has passed in almost every state, so Trump can’t recruit an opponent to any dissenters in this election. Instead, they are driven by a few factors. First, they hate Democrats more than they dislike or worry about Trump. For them, a crazy, corrupt Republican is always preferable to even the sanest and most ethical Democrat. Second, they don’t want to get excommunicated from the larger party infrastructure. After their time in office, these GOP members want to work at think tanks, trade associations, and lobbying firms. If they become persona non grata in the party, those opportunities disappear.

The Cabinet is slightly different because they aren’t in danger of getting voted out, but they are making the choice to leave lucrative careers or give up seats in Congress to serve a capricious boss known for firing people. Ambitious, thirsty people want to be close to power and are always willing to take real risks to do that.

Also, many of the people in Trump’s cabinet are dumb and kinda crazy, so we shouldn’t be surprised when they do dumb and crazy things.

Hi Dan! Question: In light of an 80 year old Dem. congressmen from Georgia passing away recently, when will the elephant in the room be (publicly and loudly) addressed regarding members of Congress who run for reelection when they’re approaching 70 and/or have a life threatening medical condition? Thanks.

The gerontocracy within the Democratic Party remains a major problem. People who run for office in that situation are incredibly narcissistic, selfish, and disrespectful of the voters they are supposed to serve.

Maybe I am too far into the bubble, but I feel like people are talking about this and have been since the 2024 election. Some older politicians like Dick Durbin and Jeanne Shaheen have stepped aside. All across the country, there are younger Democrats challenging older Democrats like Doris Matsui.

But folks like Jim Clyburn are running for reelection at the age of 85. We need a more public campaign in the next Congress to put pressure on these older members to retire.

The whole thing is shameful.

Fetterman has announced he’d vote against Iran War Powers Resolutions and support funding the ballroom, again the only Democrat to side with Republicans. If Democrats retake the Senate by exactly one seat, how real is the risk he becomes a spoiler? Could he caucus with the GOP, force a power-sharing deal, or otherwise hand Thune back the majority? Should the real goal be 52 seats to take back the Senate?

Unfortunately, you are correct. If the Dems are lucky enough to take the Senate, Fetterman is going to be a gigantic pain in the ass, much like Joe Manchin and Kyrsten Sinema were in the Biden years.

Obviously, if we can get to 52 seats we should, but that is easier said than done.

What is Hakeem Jeffries thinking saying impeachment won’t be on the agenda?

I have addressed this a couple of times in recent months, but the problem with impeachment is that it is a purely symbolic act. There is no path to actually removing Trump from office, because there is no way two-thirds of the Senate will vote to do so.

Impeachment probably helps Trump in the short term. His approval rating went up during the 2019 impeachment effort because negatively polarized Republicans snapped back into his column.

I understand it’s frustrating, but I agree with Jeffries that another failed impeachment is not the best use of Democrats’ time come 2027.

Dan, in the GOP presidential sweepstakes, aren’t you overlooking MTG? She not only seems to got out of thrall to Trump soon enough to innoculate herself, she’s also reengaged with the economic populism that underlies true MAGA. What do you think her chances might be?

I could be wrong. It’s happened before and will happen again. But my assumption is that Marjorie Taylor Greene’s supposed betrayal was too much for most GOP primary voters. Trump’s approval among Republicans remains in the low 80s and is probably higher among the partisans who actually vote in a GOP primary.

I struggle to see a path for someone Trump hates and someone who has been feted by Democrats for attacking Trump. MTG is the MAGA version of Liz Cheney.

Okay, that’s it for this week. Please leave your questions for next week in the comment section below.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Why Janet Mills Really Dropped Out
item.description: This morning, Maine Governor Janet Mills announced that she was suspending her campaign for Senate. Suspend is a legal term of art in campaign finance law; she is dropping out of the race. As Mills said in the statement she released Thursday morning: While I have the drive and passion, commitment…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
This morning, Maine Governor Janet Mills announced that she was suspending her campaign for Senate. Suspend is a legal term of art in campaign finance law; she is dropping out of the race.

As Mills said in the statement she released Thursday morning:

While I have the drive and passion, commitment and experience, and above all else — the fight — to continue on, I very simply do not have the one thing that political campaigns unfortunately require today: the financial resources. That is why today I have made the incredibly difficult decision to suspend my campaign for the United States Senate.

Oysterman and veteran Graham Platner is the de facto nominee for Senate against Susan Collins. There is no path to a Senate majority without a win in Maine.

This is a stunning turn of events for a two-term governor who was running with the backing of the Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee and Chuck Schumer. Candidates with this level of institutional support almost never lose their primaries. The fact that she dropped out a month before the primary makes it even more stunning.

The Mills campaign was clearly running out of money. After launching a series of negative ads against Platner, they pulled those ads a few weeks ago and even stopped running the relatively affordable digital ads that campaigns use to raise money.

But the lack of money was not the fundamental problem. The fact that Mills — again, a two-term governor with access to the biggest donors in Democratic politics — was unable to raise money tells a broader story about her candidacy. According to some polls, Mills was trailing Platner by as many as 30 points. She was unable to grab attention or demonstrate any momentum, despite weeks of terrible press for Platner after revelations about his offensive online comments from years ago and a tattoo with Nazi symbolism.

The collapse of the Mills campaign is bigger than Maine. It tells an important story about the state of American politics and what Democrats are looking for in candidates post-2024.

On paper, Janet Mills is a perfect Senate candidate. She is a two-term Democratic governor in a state that Kamala Harris won handily and has a long history of winning statewide.

However, Janet Mills is 78 years old. Her age was an immediate problem for her campaign. It wasn’t a question of her capacity. I interviewed her for Pod Save America; this was not a Joe Biden on the debate stage situation. She frankly seems much younger than Biden did at the same age.

But after the debacle of 2024, the party establishment lining up behind a candidate who would be 85 years old at the end of her first term in the Senate was a slap in the face.

The fact that Schumer and the DSCC failed to see this glaringly obvious problem is stunning.

Chuck Schumer did Mills no favors by endorsing her so publicly.

Schumer is particularly unpopular with the party base and has taken the brunt of the frustration with the Democratic Party’s pushback against Trump. In a recent CNN poll, Schumer had a net negative favorability rating among Democrats — the only one of the four congressional leaders underwater with his own party.

But this is about more than just Schumer.

Democratic voters are furious at the leadership and skeptical of the Democratic establishment. In primary after primary this cycle, there is a penalty for being the choice of Democratic leadership.

While the numbers have improved since last year, Democrats are not happy with their party leadership. In a June 2025 Reuters/Ipsos poll, 62% of Democrats said the party’s leadership should be replaced.

In 2026, you would much rather be an outsider than an insider. People hate politics and politicians and are yearning for something different.

Platner is different. He’s not a politician, and nothing about him suggests establishment or politics as usual. That contrast with an incumbent governor worked very much to his advantage.

Janet Mills’ entire message was electability. Even if you like Platner more, or want a progressive or an outsider candidate, you should swallow your concerns and nominate her because she was the candidate most likely to beat Susan Collins. Once again, on paper, this seemed like a good idea. We need to win Maine. Susan Collins is very hard to beat. In 2020, she won by nearly 10 points while Biden won Maine by 9 — meaning she outran Trump by roughly 18 points. That level of crossover voting is stunning in this era. It’s why she is the last Republican senator representing a solidly blue state.

Platner made Mills’ electability case much easier because of the dump truck’s worth of potential attacks in his online posts and the Nazi-symbolism tattoo.

The problem for Mills is that she didn’t seem more electable to most Maine voters. Part of that is the hangover from Biden in 2024, but poll after poll showed Platner doing well — and often better than Mills — against Collins.

Mills might have been more electable in a pre-Trump, pre-Biden political world. Voters now look at older establishment candidates and see losers, not winners. Even with all of his flaws, they think a candidate like Platner is more likely to win.

Here’s hoping they are right.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: The Supreme Court Just Gave the GOP a Massive Gift
item.description: On Tuesday, I wrote that the newly proposed Congressional maps in Florida were the last shoe to drop in the redistricting wars. I was very wrong. On Wednesday morning, the Supreme Court dropped its decision in Louisiana v. Callais . Here’s how Politico described the 6-3 ruling: The Supreme Court…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
On Tuesday, I wrote that the newly proposed Congressional maps in Florida were the last shoe to drop in the redistricting wars.

I was very wrong.

On Wednesday morning, the Supreme Court dropped its decision in Louisiana v. Callais. Here’s how Politico described the 6-3 ruling:

The Supreme Court significantly narrowed a key provision of the Voting Rights Act in a 6-3 ruling Wednesday, further eroding the impact of the landmark civil rights-era law.

For decades, Section 2 — a provision that broadly outlawed discrimination in voting on the basis of race — has been interpreted to allow, and sometimes demand, the use of race-conscious data in redistricting, to protect the voting power of minorities.

But the court’s new opinion, which split the justices along ideological lines, throws into question exactly how states can utilize race in their mapmaking process. The case involves a challenge to two majority-Black districts in Louisiana.

The Court did not formally gut Section 2, as many feared it would. But this ruling is bad for democracy, voting rights, civil rights, minority representation, and the Democratic Party.

Here’s what it means for the midterms and beyond.

Honestly, no one should be surprised by this ruling. John Roberts’ legacy is as the Chief Justice who engineered the destruction of the Voting Rights Act. This is the third decision in the last 15 years in which Roberts has struck down or gutted key parts of the VRA. Thanks to John Roberts, one of the most important civil rights laws in American history is now a shell of its former self.

The ruling in Callais is the final blow.

Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act bans voting practices that discriminate based on race, including drawing districts to dilute the voting power of minority populations. In practice, this has led to the creation of majority-minority districts, particularly in the South.

While the Court didn’t strike down Section 2, it created a test that renders it basically inoperable. In the majority opinion, Alito reinterpreted Section 2 to require proof of intentional discrimination — a standard Congress explicitly rejected in 1982 because it made the law unenforceable. He’s quietly put back the test Congress threw out 44 years ago.

What this means in practical terms is that Republicans will now be free to redraw districts to eliminate majority-minority seats. States where as many as three in ten residents are Black will end up with zero Black representatives in Congress. This won’t be an accident. It will be the product of an intentional — and apparently constitutional — effort to entrench Republican power by erasing the political voice of voters of color.

This ruling has injected even more chaos into the already chaotic battle for the House. The mid-decade redistricting push kicked off by Trump and the Republicans has meant the midterms are taking place on a constantly shifting battlefield.

The exact short-term impact of the ruling is not yet known. What is known is that it will help the Republicans.

Because of the ruling, the GOP will certainly redraw the map in Louisiana and eliminate the state’s 6th Congressional District — currently held by Democratic Rep. Cleo Fields. The ruling will also give legal cover to Florida’s clearly illegal maps, which could result in four new GOP seats.

Because the ruling came so late in the cycle, there is a limit to its short-term impact. Most state filing deadlines have passed. Some primaries have already happened, and in other states, early voting has already begun. Redrawing maps at this late stage would be incredibly challenging.

However, South Carolina, Tennessee, and Missouri have not held their primaries, nor has early voting begun. They could theoretically make a last-ditch effort to preserve the GOP majority by gerrymandering their majority-minority districts out of existence.

This morning, Tennessee Senator Marsha Blackburn, who is running for Governor, called on the state legislature to reconvene to create another GOP seat. And even though Georgia began early voting this week, several Republican state representatives are calling for a special session to redraw the state's map.

I wouldn’t be surprised to see the Trump White House latch onto these efforts to push for maximum political advantage.

The Supreme Court’s latest assault on the Voting Rights Act means the Republican advantage in the House of Representatives will grow significantly in the coming years. Black, Latino, and other people of color will have less representation.

More specifically, Republicans could completely eliminate the Democratic delegations in Alabama, Mississippi, South Carolina, and Tennessee, and shrink Democratic seats in Louisiana, Georgia, North Carolina, Texas, Florida, and Arkansas. Analyses by Fair Fight Action and Black Voters Matter project as many as 19 House seats could flip. Up to 30 percent of the Congressional Black Caucus could disappear.

The damage doesn’t stop at Congress. Section 2 has been the primary tool for challenging discriminatory at-large elections in city councils, county commissions, school boards, and judicial races. Nearly half of all Section 2 cases since 1982 have been local. That entire body of law just got harder to use, and in many places, impossible.

The era of Democratic unilateral disarmament on redistricting is over. It has to be. Republicans wrote the new rules — gerrymander whenever, however, for whatever reason. Pretending we're still playing the old game is how we lose the House for a generation. Every governor's race, every state legislative chamber, every secretary of state contest in 2026 is now a redistricting fight. Act accordingly.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!
