item.title: DeSantis Is Betting the House on Latino Voters Who've Already Left
item.description: One week ago, Democrats were celebrating a huge victory in the battle for the House. Virginia voters approved a measure that would allow Democrats to redraw the Congressional maps, shifting the state’s delegation from six Democrats and five Republicans to one that would likely be 10 Democrats and…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
One week ago, Democrats were celebrating a huge victory in the battle for the House. Virginia voters approved a measure that would allow Democrats to redraw the Congressional maps, shifting the state’s delegation from six Democrats and five Republicans to one that would likely be 10 Democrats and one Republican. This net gain of four seats was a huge win and led to a lot of back-patting from Democrats. And rightfully so — Republicans had kicked off an extraordinary effort to gerrymander their way into a semi-permanent majority, and Democrats had basically fought it to a draw. Something that seemed impossible at the outset.

There was one more shoe to drop in the redistricting wars, and that shoe dropped yesterday.

Governor DeSantis released a proposed map for Florida that could potentially lead to Republicans picking up four seats this fall. DeSantis is making the districts of four Democrats significantly more Republican.

I should mention that this map is almost certainly illegal. Florida’s constitution prohibits redrawing congressional or state legislative maps for partisan gain. But DeSantis seems confident he can get the Florida Supreme Court — six of whose seven justices he appointed — to look the other way.

This proposed Florida map is more aggressive than most observers expected and might be so aggressive as to blow up in Republicans’ faces.

The math of redistricting is relatively simple. There are only so many Republicans in Florida. To create four new Republican districts, DeSantis needs to take Republicans out of safe Republican districts and Democrats out of safe Democratic districts and redistribute them. In drawing the map so aggressively, DeSantis risks spreading the state’s Republican voters too thinly.

Dave Wasserman, the redistricting guru at the Cook Political Report, raised this concern on Twitter:

New FL map proposed by Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) would target four Dem seats, aiming to convert a 20R-8D delegation to 24R-4D. But in a year like 2026, not all of the 24 seats would be safe for the GOP.

The new Republican districts are unlikely to be safe Republican districts. Given Democratic overperformance in elections over the last year, several of the newly drawn districts could be winnable for Democrats. Reps. Anna Paulina Luna and Laurel Lee in the Tampa area are the clearest example. To dismantle Kathy Castor’s blue Tampa seat, DeSantis has to spread her Democratic voters into the surrounding Republican districts, making Luna and Lee’s seats bluer in a year when both were already top DCCC targets. Meanwhile, in South Florida, DeSantis left Maria Elvira Salazar and Carlos Giménez’s districts largely untouched. He didn’t have to dilute them, but he couldn’t help them either

Most analysts believed that creating two new Republican districts would have been the wisest move. Anything more than that could blow up in their face. DeSantis took the riskier path because he is trying to rehabilitate his reputation among the MAGA tastemakers after his disastrous 2024 presidential campaign.

His need for approbation could cost his party.

It’s not just that DeSantis is diluting the Republican vote. It’s that he’s basing his map on the 2024 presidential election.

And that could be a fatal error.

Trump won Florida by 13 points in 2024. A huge margin in what used to be a battleground state. That victory was fueled by a historic level of support among Latino voters. According to the exit polls, Trump won Florida Latinos 56-43 — a 13-point margin, compared to losing them by 27 points to Hillary Clinton in 2016. That’s a 40-point swing in eight years.

2024 was the high-water mark for Republican support among Latinos. Every piece of evidence since then has shown a dramatic drop.

An April Third Way / UnidosUS poll found Trump’s net favorability among Latinos at minus 32, with Democrats holding a 30-point advantage on the generic ballot. An Equis Research poll fielded in March found Trump’s net approval among Latinos at minus 25 and Democrats leading the generic congressional ballot by 19 points. Equis also found that 28 percent of Latino Trump voters now express disappointment or regret about their 2024 vote — and that share is climbing month over month.

It’s not just the polls. Latino voters delivered double-digit margins for Democrats in the New Jersey and Virginia governor’s races in 2025. In December, Eileen Higgins became the first Democrat elected mayor of Miami in nearly 30 years, defeating a Trump-endorsed Republican by 19 points in a campaign that leaned hard into Trump’s immigration crackdown. In special election after special election, Latino-heavy precincts have shifted to Democrats in huge ways.

For DeSantis’s map to work, he is betting that Latinos will stick with the GOP at 2024 levels. There is not much evidence to support that bet.

The worst-case scenario for a gerrymandered map is that it produces a worse result than the old map. That’s called a dummymander.

Could it happen in Florida? It would take a lot to go right for Democrats. But it’s not out of the question.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Is J.D. Vance Already Losing the 2028 Primary?
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. I was in Washington, DC, for some counter-programming to the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, where Trump made his first appearance as a sitting president. Let’s get into it. A quick note: these mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. I was in Washington, DC, for some counter-programming to the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, where Trump made his first appearance as a sitting president.

Let’s get into it.

A quick note: these mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

And don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments.

Obviously the next R POTUS candidates are Vance or Rubio. Is there anyone else you feel may step into the race and is there anyone who stands a chance of winning a primary? Personally I think the best thing Vance can do now for his chances is to resign...

There are certainly more pressing matters in the world than gaming out the 2028 GOP primary, but it’s a fascinating intellectual exercise that offers a window into the state of today’s Republican Party — and what the subscribers want, the subscribers get.

History would suggest that Vance will almost certainly be the nominee. Sitting vice presidents who run for the nomination tend to win, and win with relative ease. George H.W. Bush and Al Gore are the two most recent examples. Both faced primary challenges but locked up their nominations early.

That said, my confidence that Vance will be the nominee has waned considerably.

He has repeatedly shown himself to be politically maladroit — so painfully awkward on the public stage that it’s hard to imagine him surviving the klieg lights of a full presidential campaign. He’s more DeSantis than Trump, and he might actually be worse than DeSantis.

Rubio is one of those Republicans who always seems better on paper than in real life. After his 2010 Senate win, he was seen as the next big thing. Time Magazine even ran this cover story on him.

But he’s never lived up to the hype. His 2016 presidential campaign was mediocre at best, ending when he got shellacked in his home state. He looked more this photo:

On the day he dropped out, Rubio was nearly in tears talking about how worried he was about Trump getting the nuclear codes. Now he helps Trump bomb countries for sport.

Is he a better candidate than Vance? Maybe, but not by much, and he carries real vulnerabilities in a GOP primary — most notably his role in passing a comprehensive immigration bill that Barack Obama supported. Among Republican base voters, that could be a mortal sin.

Based on the small sample of previous elections featuring a sitting vice president, these races tend to bifurcate: the VP runs as the continuity candidate, while a change candidate argues the incumbent administration has lost its way. It would be strange for the race to come down to two largely pro-Trump candidates carrying all of his baggage.

So who plays the change candidate? It’s unlikely to be a more traditional Republican — a Nikki Haley, Spencer Cox, or Mitt Romney type — because there’s simply no appetite for that in the hardcore base. The change candidate will come from the right of Vance and Rubio. Think Tucker Carlson, arguing that Trump’s heirs lost touch with true MAGA principles. The odds would be long, but someone like Carlson, who can generate massive amounts of earned media, might find an opening.

Do you have an opinion about taking away presidential pardon power? Seems to me it’s more often abused than used wisely, and taking it away could be seen as one of many reforms designed to rein in executive power. Thoughts?

The recent report that Trump promised his aides pardons before leaving the White House was yet another reminder of how badly this power can be abused. It’s essentially de facto immunity for presidential aides, friends, and cronies to commit crimes with impunity. I’d take it away in a second.

Unfortunately, it’s written into the Constitution and widely understood by the courts to be close to absolute — meaning Congress can’t meaningfully constrain it through ordinary legislation.

That leaves two options, and neither is particularly satisfying. The first is a constitutional amendment — something that hasn’t happened in over three decades. The second is to shine a bright spotlight on the abuse. A Democratic congressional majority could investigate how Trump has used the pardon power and expose the sketchy, likely illegal cottage industry that has emerged around it — where money flows to Trump allies in hopes of securing clemency.

Neither path is easy, but sunlight is at least a start.

Assuming that Trump pardons everyone within 200 feet of the White House, can they still be subject to a civil suit, e.g, a class action, etc.?

Yes. Presidential pardons only eliminate federal criminal liability. Pardoned individuals can still be prosecuted at the state and local level, and they remain fully exposed to civil suits — including class actions.

There’s also an underappreciated wrinkle: accepting a pardon has historically been treated as an admission of guilt, which can actually weaken a defendant’s position in civil proceedings. I expect many of Trump’s top aides to be drowning in legal bills even if they never set foot in a federal prison.

Hi Dan! Love the Message Box. Long time reader, first time questioner, there a stories popping up about the Democrats in the Senate race in Michigan cannibalizing each other and leaving an opening for Republicans. I know this is a highly competitive race, but how worried should we be about this? Thank you and keep on

Thank you — and welcome to the mailbag!

I’m not unconcerned about Michigan. There’s a three-way primary with real potential to get divisive. (Disclosure: I’m supporting Mallory McMorrow and hosted a fundraiser for her.) Most of the fighting has been online between supporters, but it could get nastier as we approach the August primary.

A lot of Senate map analysis treats Michigan as a near-automatic Democratic hold, and that’s a mistake. Mike Rogers is running unopposed and ran a surprisingly strong race in 2024, losing to Elissa Slotkin by fewer than 20,000 votes. If Democrats don’t unify after the primary and put their best foot forward, Rogers can win. There is no path to a Senate majority without holding Michigan.

It’s too early to panic — but I’m watching it closely.

Maybe a too paranoid question for next week, but will ask anyway. Everything I read about the “ballroom” makes it sound like it is actually a bunker. To protect them from what? We can imagine many scenarios. If it is really a bunker, shouldn’t we be calling everyone’s attention to it, instead of treating it as just another whim of the guy’s? Even on the pod seems like just another jokey confusion. What if it building this is far more strategic, another threat to democracy? Should we be calling this out?

In the Trump era, it’s generally better to be paranoid than caught off guard — so I respect the instinct. But I wouldn’t lose too much sleep over this one. I’ll acknowledge that there’s only so much I can say here, which I know is an unsatisfying answer. What I can say is that some kind of secure emergency facility beneath the White House wouldn’t be unusual or new — presidents have had bunker-style protections for decades. I don’t read this as something Trump is building specifically to cling to power.

Okay, that’s it for this week. Leave your questions for next week in the comments below.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Why Trump's Poll Numbers Won't Get Better
item.description: There is something deeply unsettling about having a dangerously erratic president at a time of war. The past week has been a perfect encapsulation of Trump-induced whiplash. Last Thursday, we were at war and blockading the Strait of Hormuz. On Friday, Trump declared the Strait was open, Iran had…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
There is something deeply unsettling about having a dangerously erratic president at a time of war.

The past week has been a perfect encapsulation of Trump-induced whiplash.

Last Thursday, we were at war and blockading the Strait of Hormuz. On Friday, Trump declared the Strait was open, Iran had agreed to all our conditions, and we were on the cusp of a peace deal. By Saturday morning, the Strait was closed again, and Iran had fired on at least two ships. On Sunday morning, U.S. officials were about to head to Pakistan to hammer out a deal, and a few hours later, the U.S. had seized an Iranian ship and Trump was once again threatening to commit war crimes. By Monday, peace talks were back on — and then, moments before J.D. Vance was scheduled to leave for Islamabad, they were called off, though the ceasefire was extended.

It’s impossible to know if, how, or when this moronic and illegal war will come to an end. Anyone dumb enough to start it is too dumb to finish it.

In that brief moment of “peace” on Friday, the stock market rallied, oil prices dropped, and political pundits began to wonder whether all the damage from the war would fade by November.

I don’t think so. Even if the war ended tomorrow and the Strait reopened, Trump will have suffered lasting political damage.

Of course, if the U.S. is still embroiled in a raging war and gas is well north of $4 a gallon come November, that’s a worst-case scenario — one Trump is doing a great job of ensuring with his unhinged tweets and schizophrenic policy decisions.

But no matter what happens, the damage is done, and this war is not one of those Trump outrages that will be memory-holed in a matter of weeks. Republicans will pay a price for Trump’s ill-fated, ill-considered decision to go to war.

Here’s why.

New wars tend to start popular and then lose altitude over time as people begin to reckon with the costs in blood and treasure. The Gulf War, the War in Afghanistan, and the Iraq War all launched with broad bipartisan support. The latter two dragged on and became particularly unpopular — but crucially, voters had initially supported the decision to go to war, and only soured over time on how those wars were conducted.

Not so with Trump’s war in Iran.

It started with a majority of Americans already disapproving of the decision to go to war. There are a number of reasons for this. Trump was too busy working on his ballroom and other taxpayer-funded home renovation projects to make the case for why the war was necessary. The State of the Union was three days before the first bombs fell, and Trump spent only a few minutes on Iran.

People are also quite unhappy with how Trump has handled the war since. His approval rating is down three or four points, and there’s been strong bipartisan majority opposition to statements like his threat to wipe Iran off the map. But even through the rising gas prices and ensuing chaos, approval of the war itself has remained relatively static — some people have moved from undecided to opposed, but support is within a point of where it was at the start of March.

You see a similar dynamic on questions about Trump’s handling of the war: if you like Trump, you tell pollsters you approve of how it’s going. It’s not really about the specifics of the war.

Folks on the Right may look at that and see good news — Trump has blundered throughout, and support has barely dropped.

But that’s the wrong read. If people don’t move when things are bad, they won’t move if things get better. The floor and the ceiling are the same.

NBC News released a new poll this week. Like every recent poll, it’s terrible for Trump: 37% approval, only 19% strongly approving of the war, 54% strongly disapproving. His economic approval is in the figurative toilet.

NBC’s write-up included this line that stuck with me:

Americans’ views of how Trump has navigated the war did not significantly shift after he announced a temporary ceasefire on April 7 — with roughly one-third approving of his handling of the war in the days following that announcement.

People’s minds are made up. And that’s bad news for Trump.

It’s also worth noting that four polls released this week all showed Trump under 40, with his lowest approval ratings to date.

None of this should be surprising. Trump ran on three promises: secure the border, lower prices, and no more wars. Then he went and started a war that raised prices. It’s an obvious and arrogant thumb in the eye of the people who put him in office. The true die-hards can twist themselves into a pretzel and be okay with it. Everyone else feels betrayed. That may not be enough to push most former Trump supporters to vote Democratic — but they probably won’t vote Republican either, giving Democrats a real turnout advantage in the midterms.

Beyond the specific promise-breaking, the war confirmed people’s worst fears about Trump. Even some of his voters knew he was erratic, overly emotional, driven by rage, and possibly insane. For a long time, those traits produced embarrassing moments rather than real-world consequences. Before the pandemic, Trump acted like an ass, but most people didn’t feel it in their lives or their bank accounts. People were willing to excuse a lot because they thought the economy was humming. Stumbling ass-backward into a catastrophe was the worst-case scenario Trump had managed to avoid for the first four years of his presidency.

Now that it’s happened, people are unlikely to forget it.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: What Does the Virginia Win Tell Us About November?
item.description: Last night, Democrats continued their streak of winning elections in the Trump era. In Virginia, Democrats passed a measure to allow the state to redraw its congressional districts.. This is a huge victory in the battle for control of the House this year. The Virginia congressional delegation…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Last night, Democrats continued their streak of winning elections in the Trump era. In Virginia, Democrats passed a measure to allow the state to redraw its congressional districts..

This is a huge victory in the battle for control of the House this year. The Virginia congressional delegation currently comprises six Democrats and five Republicans. The new maps will likely result in a delegation with 10 Democrats and 1 Republican — a net gain of four seats for Democrats.

While Florida is still trying to redraw their maps, Democrats have largely nullified the GOP’s effort to rig the 2026 elections through mid-decade redistricting. Florida Republicans may still gain a few seats, but far fewer than anyone thought possible last year.

This sort of election is different from the congressional special elections and local races — like the recent Supreme Court race in Wisconsin — that we’ve been using as a barometer of the political environment. A singular ballot initiative in the spring, six months after a high-profile governor’s race and seven months before the midterms, is not your typical election. So we don’t want to over-interpret the results, but there are some important takeaways.

A year ago, Democrats were in a panic about Republicans gerrymandering themselves into a semi-permanent majority. But Democrats didn’t just whine, cry, or get in the fetal position. Unlike in the past, they didn’t complain about broken norms and talk about how little power they had.

They got off their asses and got to work.

Democrats across the country are desperate for someone in their party to stand up to Trump and fight back. Sure, some of their expectations aren’t in line with the realities of being a minority party. But where we have power, we must use it — even if that means editorial boards and the centrist scolds get upset.

As we saw with the shutdown last year and the ICE fight this year, Democratic voters want their leaders to use the tools they have, as limited as they may be. Congressional Democrats can’t undo what Trump does. They can’t stop his unfit appointees. At best, they can slow him down. But in Virginia and California, Democrats recognized that they had real power to push back against GOP gerrymandering in Texas and elsewhere. They just had to be willing to use it.

In the recent past, Democrats would not have taken the extraordinary step of mid-decade redistricting — and even if they had, there’s no chance they would have drawn the maps so aggressively. Democrats believe that gerrymandering is bad, and our mindset in the past would have been that two wrongs don’t make a right. And honestly, too many Democrats would have been worried about what the elite opinion makers would say about the use of brute political force.

Hakeem Jeffries, Governor Spanberger, and Virginia Democrats deserve a lot of credit for putting some skin in the game and fighting fire with fire.

It took way too long, but enough Democrats now realize the stakes of this fight. The only way to win is to use every weapon in the arsenal. The best way to protect democracy is to punch fascism in the mouth.

Last night’s results validated the idea that voters want a fighter.

Back in November, California voters passed a similar measure by 30 points. Last night, the Virginia initiative passed by only XX points — far fewer than Spanberger’s 15-point margin of victory last fall, and likely even closer than Kamala Harris’s 6-point win in 2024 when all of the votes are counted.

Democrats have been overperforming by about 12 points in special elections in recent months, and this is the first election not to show a substantially worse political environment for Trump and Republicans.

So what gives?

A few thoughts.

First, this certainly doesn’t indicate a fundamental shift in the political environment. Four national polls came out yesterday showing Trump at his lowest numbers ever. In the AP/NORC poll, his approval was 33%.

Second, unlike in California, the campaign against the measure actually spent real money and delivered a message. Their most effective ad used Barack Obama’s previous comments against gerrymandering to push people to vote no.

Third, the difference between the 2025 Virginia results and last night comes down to independent voters. In 2025, independents favored Spanberger by 20 points. In the George Mason/Washington Post poll released in early April, they only supported YES by 10 points. But independents opposing a process-oriented ballot measure isn’t the same thing as them coming home to Republicans. In a Fox News poll taken around the same time, independents favored Democrats by 22 points on the generic ballot.

Finally, Republicans successfully nationalized this election, which helped juice their base. This will be their strategy for the fall midterms — trying to get some of those disenchanted Trump 2024 voters to show up and own the libs one more time. There are limits to that strategy, but it’s why the final 2026 popular margin is unlikely to be as high as the margins Democrats have been winning special elections by this year.

Ultimately, Republicans shouldn’t take too much solace from this slightly less bad than expected defeat. What matters more than the margin is the four House seats Democrats just picked up.

If there’s one lesson of the Trump era, it’s that moral victories aren’t really victories at all.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Introducing Message Box Pro
item.description: Today, I’m excited to announce a project that has been years in the making: Message Box Pro — a members-only consulting service designed for people running for office, working on campaigns, and leading grassroots organizations. The traditional model of political consulting is broken. The economics…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Today, I’m excited to announce a project that has been years in the making: Message Box Pro — a members-only consulting service designed for people running for office, working on campaigns, and leading grassroots organizations.

The traditional model of political consulting is broken. The economics are such that only well-funded congressional and statewide candidates can afford strategic advice. The vast majority of candidates running for local office — and the people running grassroots and community organizations — are left without guidance at exactly the moment they need it most.

Over the years, I’ve consistently heard from people running for local office — state legislature, school board, city council — who tell me they rely on Message Box and Pod Save America to help guide their campaigns. These are candidates whose campaigns can’t afford to run a poll or hire a political consultant. I also regularly hear from folks working in communications, messaging, and digital strategy at every level of government and campaigns who use Message Box to sharpen their approach.

It’s frankly an indictment of the Democratic Party’s infrastructure that downballot candidates are left to fend for themselves.

The need has never been greater. As politics becomes increasingly nationalized, even local races are heavily influenced by national events and trends. A school board candidate in Wisconsin needs to know how to respond when Trump attacks teachers’ unions. A city council candidate in Arizona needs messaging guidance when immigration dominates the news cycle.

I want to help fill that gap.

Message Box Pro is a subscription product that provides data-driven insights, strategic guidance, and public opinion analysis. Subscribers receive:

Weekly Strategy Memos — Clear, timely, actionable guidance tailored to the political environment, delivered directly to your inbox. Think of it as your campaign strategist breaking down what’s happening and what to do about it. Unlike my Substack posts, which are primarily news analysis, these are the kinds of memos I’ve written for politicians like Barack Obama throughout my career.

Weekly Office Hours — Post your questions and challenges throughout the week. Get answers from me — and learn from other community members who’ve faced similar situations.

Message Testing & Polling Insights — Access to data-driven messaging and public opinion analysis designed to help you understand the shifting political landscape and identify the most effective responses to the issues dominating the news cycle.

A Community of Operatives, Organizers, and Candidates — Connect with other candidates, campaign staff, and organizers. Share what’s working, troubleshoot challenges, and build a network that extends beyond your race.

Local and municipal candidates and elected officials (school board, city council, state legislature, county commission)

Grassroots and advocacy organizations

Campaign staff, communications professionals, and political operatives

Anyone who wants to sharpen their understanding of political strategy, messaging, and how to break through in today's chaotic media environment

The first 250 members lock in this founding rate forever. After that, the price goes up to $65 — so if this is for you, now is the time.

This is a fraction of what a single strategy call with a consultant costs (more than a $1,000) or a monthly retainer with a firm ($5,000–$15,000).

This is a separate product from the Message Box newsletter. Message Box will continue exactly as it is — same content, same price ($7/month or $70/year for paid subscribers).

Message Box Pro is designed specifically for people actively working in politics or running campaigns. If that’s not you, you probably don’t need it. But if you’re a candidate, staffer, or organizer who needs strategic guidance you can’t otherwise afford, this is built for you.

I’m limiting founding memberships to 250 people to ensure I can deliver real value. Once we hit that number, the price goes up.

Questions? Reply to this email or comment below.

Thanks for your support over the years. I’m genuinely excited about this — and about the chance to help campaigns win races they might otherwise lose.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post.

item.title: The Hidden Story: Trump Is Losing the MAGA Base
item.description: Now that Donald Trump has hit his all-time low in job approval, there is a general consensus that he is up shit’s creek without a paddle. Much of the conversation has been about how the Latinos, working-class voters of color, and young men who powered his 2024 victory have turned on him. That’s all…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Now that Donald Trump has hit his all-time low in job approval, there is a general consensus that he is up shit’s creek without a paddle. Much of the conversation has been about how the Latinos, working-class voters of color, and young men who powered his 2024 victory have turned on him.

That’s all very true.

The last ones onto the Trump train are the first ones out.

The fact that the coalition Republicans (and a lot of pundits) thought would produce a semi-permanent MAGA majority crumbled within a year is notable (and reassuring).

But this focus on the presidential-year coalition actually understates Trump’s political problem. Right below the surface, an existential threat looms for Republicans in 2026, 2028, and the long term.

White working-class voters have soured on Trump.

It’s not getting nearly enough attention, but Trump’s most reliable voters — the ones that powered his two presidential wins and kept him afloat in turbulent times — are abandoning him in droves.

To fully understand why white voters who didn’t go to college are so important to Trump and the Republicans, you have to take yourself back to that ever-so-painful time of 2016. The reason so many people (like me) were so surprised by Trump’s victory is that we drastically underestimated his strength among these voters. The assumption was that Barack Obama’s performance among the white working class would be the nadir. Surely, a white candidate would do better.

Nope. Not even close.

Trump blew the doors off with white voters who didn’t go to college. Somewhere between 6 and 9 million people who voted for Obama in 2012 voted for Trump in 2016. More impressively, Trump generated massive turnout in counties with a disproportionately large population of white voters who didn’t attend college.

In the end, Trump improved on Mitt Romney’s performance with these voters by 14 points.

In 2024, Trump won white voters without a college degree by a whopping 34 points.

Those days are over.

Several polls in the last several weeks have shown pluralities — and in some cases outright majorities — of white non-college voters disapproving of Trump.

CNN/SSRS (March 26–30): 49 / 50 → net −1

Fox News (January 23–26): 49 / 51 → net −2

Marist (March 2–4): 46 / 48 → net −2

CBS (April 8–10): 48 / 52 → net −4

To make matters even worse, some polls have found Trump underwater with white men who didn’t go to college — the most MAGA group of voters in America. In the Marist poll, 49% of white non-college men disapprove of Trump, while 46% approve. A Fox News poll from March found the same thing, with this group at 48 approve / 52 disapprove.

This is a dramatic shift among Trump’s most loyal cohort of voters. A year ago, his approval with this group was roughly +20 to +25 across major pollsters. That’s a swing of 20 to 30 net points in twelve months.

This is a massive political problem for the Republicans. The Latinos and younger voters who supported Trump in 2024 are how he reached his low ceiling, but it’s the white working-class voters who have given Trump his high floor over the last decade.

When they start to leave, the bottom falls out for the GOP.

While their share of the electorate shrinks every year in a rapidly diversifying country, white people who didn’t go to college are still the biggest group in American politics. Several post-election studies found that about 4 in 10 voters in the 2024 electorate were white voters without a college degree. The number is much higher in battleground states like Pennsylvania, Michigan, and Wisconsin.

The movement of white working-class voters into the Republican Party in the Trump era is the simplest explanation for why Barack Obama was able to win Ohio and Iowa twice, and Democrats haven’t come close since. It also explains why Obama was able to win the Blue Wall states of Michigan, Wisconsin, and Pennsylvania with relative ease, and Trump has won them in two of the last three presidential elections.

White voters without a college degree also make up a disproportionate share of the population in several of the states that will decide control of the Senate in 2026. The white non-college share of the electorate is higher than the national average in Maine, Iowa, Ohio, Alaska, and North Carolina. In Iowa, roughly two-thirds of voters are white people without a college degree; in Maine and Ohio, it’s somewhere in the high 50s to low 60s.

If Trump had maintained his support with this group, Democratic control of the Senate would be off the table. Because these voters are leaving him, it’s very much on the table.

Getting voters to become disenchanted with Trump and the Republicans is step one. The second step is getting them to vote for Democrats. To that end, Democrats still have a lot of work to do.

In the January Fox News poll, Democrats have a 6-point lead on the generic ballot, but among white non-college voters, Republicans have a 10-point advantage. (The March Fox poll didn’t ask the generic ballot question, or at least didn’t release it.) Other polling shows that white working-class voters hold the Democratic Party in particularly low esteem. It’s possible that some of our excellent candidates with their own independent identities — like Roy Cooper, Sherrod Brown, and Mary Peltola — can significantly outperform the national party brand. But rebuilding a durable electoral coalition that can compete everywhere and stop MAGA in its tracks depends on the Democratic Party becoming a worthy vessel for the voters disenchanted with Trump.

We aren’t there yet.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: The Politics of Israel Have Shifted. Many Democrats Haven't Caught Up
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. Let’s get right into it. But first a some housekeeping. These mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags. Don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. Let’s get right into it.

But first a some housekeeping. These mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

Don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments section.

It seems like the majority of Democrats and Independents are shifting to a negative view of AIPAC/Israeli lobby. How does the Democratic Party thread the needle of calling out anti-semitism but also acknowledging that Bibi’s regime in Israel has too much control and influence over our politicians / foreign policy and that has led to general negative feelings towards Israel?

For decades, support for Israel was a bipartisan staple. Aid to Israel passed with near-unanimous support every year, and criticism of Israel was enough to make someone persona non grata in Washington.

That has changed dramatically in just a few years. I have never seen an issue shift this fast in my lifetime. And it’s moving faster than the Democratic establishment has realized.

This Gallup chart shows how quickly the politics around Israel have changed.

According to an NBC poll, positive views of Israel have dropped 15 points since 2023, in the immediate aftermath of the October 7th terrorist attack.

The shift is driven primarily by Democrats, but it goes much further than that. Support among independents is down 19 points, and even among Republicans, it’s dropped 9 points.

Pew found that strong majorities of Americans have negative views of Israel and Netanyahu. This is not just a Democratic phenomenon — a majority of adults under 50 now rate Israel and Netanyahu negatively.

Bibi Netanyahu and the genocide in Gaza have done untold damage to Israel’s standing in the United States. Only a few years ago, Israel was seen as one of our closest allies. Now, many Americans view it as a pariah state.

That has real implications for American politics. While many Democrats have spoken out, the leadership — particularly Senator Schumer — has been reluctant to, clinging to an outdated view of the U.S.-Israel relationship. Politicians who don’t grasp this seismic shift risk being out of touch with the electorate in both parties. Israel is yet another issue where there’s a yawning gap between the base and the establishment.

Democrats must speak out against anti-semitism in all of its forms. But they cannot accept the proposition that criticism of the Netanyahu government or AIPAC is the same as anti-semitism.

There are limits, though. On Thursday, a special election was held in New Jersey. Progressive Analilia Mejia easily won the seat previously held by Governor Mikie Sherrill. Mejia overperformed in the Hispanic parts of the state and persuaded a significant number of independents and Republicans. In that sense, her win looked like every other Democratic special election — with one big exception. Mejia massively underperformed in the heavily Jewish precincts in towns like Livingston. In one precinct, she underperformed Kamala Harris by 50 points.

Politico described her positions on Israel as follows:

Mejia has said she believes Israel committed genocide in Gaza and did not raise her hand when asked at a candidate forum if she believed Jews “have the right to self-determination in their ancestral homeland, what is commonly referred to as Zionism.”

The Republican spent most of his time and money communicating to the Jewish community and painting Mejia in the worst light possible. This is just one race, so I wouldn’t read too much into it, but it’s an early indicator of the Republican strategy for this fall.

Having said all of that: the public is unhappy with the Netanyahu government, and they are unhappy with our government’s relationship with Netanyahu. They don’t want their tax dollars paying to kill kids in Gaza, Lebanon, or Iran. And Democrats can’t be afraid to speak to that anger.

As we sit here today, it’s hard to imagine a candidate unwilling to do so with clarity and force winning the Democratic nomination in 2028.

Dan, my message box question is: how worried are you about AI election interference? Especially when chairman Powell and Kevin Hassett called an emergency meeting to discuss the new anthropic AI, Mythos, that apparently has the capability to hack our entire financial infrastructure. If that’s the case, I can’t see why or how another LLM would not be able to hack our election infrastructure? Is there anything anyone can do about this? Is anyone in the party leadership thinking about it?

There are a lot of ways AI can disrupt our elections.

There’s the use of deepfakes to spread disinformation. There’s the capacity to use AI to mass post on social media through bot armies in an attempt to surface certain messages in the algorithm. And more and more Americans are turning to AI chatbots for research about political issues — how those language models are trained can shape the information people get. Just look at how Grok, Elon Musk’s AI chatbot, spits out MAGA-coded answers.

It’s also possible AI could make it easier to hack our election infrastructure. Unfortunately, the federal government has primary responsibility for protecting our elections from cyber attacks. States have some responsibility too, and that should give us some hope.

Lots of people in the party are thinking about this. We just don’t have a lot of power to stop it. (Sorry, I know that’s not reassuring.)

I have a semantics question. I feel like throughout my life, terms like “liberal,” “progressive,” or “the left” have all shifted meaning and it seems like every few years a new one becomes the preferred term. I’m noticing we’re starting to shift away from “progressive” and back toward “the left.” Similarly, I feel like a lot of people are using “moderate” and “centrist” as interchangeable terms. How do you define all of these at this point in time?

I’ll try to give you a semantic answer.

There was a movement in the 90s and early 2000s to transition from “liberal” to “progressive,” because “liberal” was seen as carrying too many negative connotations. Then basically 80% of the party started calling themselves progressive, so people to the left of the party mainstream started using “leftist” to distinguish themselves.

Moderate and centrist are often used interchangeably, but that’s a mistake. Moderate is an ideology. Centrism is an identity. Moderates hold a series of positions that are more conservative than the average Democrat. Centrists will take any position that is equidistant between Democrats and Republicans. That’s a political strategy — and a bad one — not a consistent ideology.

Question for mailbag: I’m in TN and we have a young, flashy progressive running against Tim Burchett for TN-02. It’s a 20+ point district for Republicans, and we have many other local or state races that are within a closer margin that could actually flip. Should I encourage my friends to support other campaigns strategically instead of pouring resources into TN-02?

I don’t want to discourage anyone from investing their time, energy, hopes, and dreams in an inspiring candidate. Well-funded campaigns that engage grassroots supporters pay dividends over the long term, even when they fall short.

But people should go in with eyes wide open. A district Trump won by 20 points is tough under even the most optimistic scenarios. If your friends are looking for the highest return on their dollar, there are races closer to home where those resources will go further.

Looks like Primary season is picking back up in May. Any state races to watch?

You’re right. May is filled with interesting primaries that will tell us a lot about Trump’s hold on the Republican Party.

On May 16, Louisiana holds its Senate primary. Incumbent Bill Cassidy is being challenged by two Republicans — Julia Letlow, who has Trump’s endorsement, and John Fleming. Trump hates Cassidy because he voted to impeach Trump after January 6th. This one probably goes to a runoff, because it’s unlikely any candidate gets to 50%.

May 19th is the second stop on Trump’s vengeance tour. Congressman Thomas Massie is being challenged by Ed Gallrein, who has Trump’s backing. The Trump political operation is going full bore to defeat Massie. If they fail, it will be a big blow to Trump.

Finally, on May 26th, Texas holds a runoff between John Cornyn and Ken Paxton. After the primary, everyone assumed Trump would endorse Cornyn, but he never did. Paxton is a slight favorite to win the runoff — and he’s the preferred candidate of Democrats.

If/when democrats take the house how can those in power under dementia don be held accountable with no power in the DOJ?

Democrats won’t be able to prosecute or remove Trump officials. They can refer people to the DOJ for prosecution, but we know this DOJ isn’t going to do anything.

What they will have is the power to conduct oversight of the Trump administration. That matters for two reasons. First, public scrutiny of the corruption and incompetence. Second, they can collect evidence of criminality that becomes a roadmap for the Department of Justice under the next president.

Any thoughts on Kamala Harris doing a write-in campaign for Governor of California now that Swalwell has imploded? Saw a piece on that in the WSJ and am curious.

Having looked at the rules for write-in candidacies in California, Kamala Harris could mount a campaign fairly easily if she wanted to. I'm not sure she does, and Democrats already have plenty of choices (too many, honestly), but the option is on the table if she wanted to pursue it. It would be high-risk for everyone involved, though. Successfully executing a write-in campaign in a state as big as California is a heavy lift.

Okay, that’s it for this week. Leave your questions for next week in the comment section below.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Why the MAGA Media Revolt Matters Even More Than You Think
item.description: Everyone is used to Democrats criticizing Trump. We have even become accustomed to a certain set of proudly non-MAGA Republicans periodically casting aspersions on Trump. But MAGA types attacking “Dear Leader”? Never. Until now. Trump is getting savaged by some of his closest former allies. Tucker…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Everyone is used to Democrats criticizing Trump. We have even become accustomed to a certain set of proudly non-MAGA Republicans periodically casting aspersions on Trump.

But MAGA types attacking “Dear Leader”?

Never.

Until now.

Trump is getting savaged by some of his closest former allies. Tucker Carlson has been going on hour-long tirades. Alex Jones is calling for the 25th Amendment. Megyn Kelly is telling Trump to “shut the fuck up.” Candace Owens called Trump a “genocidal lunatic.” Fellow travelers like podcasters Theo Von, Shawn Ryan, Tim Pool. and Joe Rogan, who all backed Trump in 2024, have been harshly critical of Trump on several issues, including the Epstein files and the war in Iran.

The clips of Trump getting ripped by his former allies have gone viral on social media and become a source of schadenfreude for Democrats and others disgusted by Trump.

It finally feels like the walls may be collapsing around him.

But the press and pundits have been quick to rain on our parade, pointing to polls showing that MAGA voters are sticking with Trump, with nearly 9 in 10 supporting the war in Iran.

I think this analysis misses the point, misunderstands how the modern media ecosystem works, and understates the short- and long-term damage to Trump.

What About the MAGA Voters?

Sure, Dan, but does dissent from the MAGA media really matter when MAGA voters aren’t following them off the Trump train?

Trump is losing Republican support — just not in the way the press expects.

As this Navigator Research poll shows, 85% of MAGA Republicans support the Iran war, but only 54% of non-MAGA Republicans do.

The assumption is that MAGA Republicans are America First isolationists in the mold of Steve Bannon and Tucker Carlson, while non-MAGA Republicans are traditional establishment types who love Mitt Romney, Paul Ryan, and anyone with the last name Bush.

It’s more complicated than that. MAGA is not a philosophy or a worldview — there is no specific ideological agenda. MAGA is now a proxy for loyalty to Trump. It means you wear the red hat, figuratively or literally. This explains why these voters stick with Trump even when he violates the most essential principles of his own agenda. They were against forever wars when Trump was against them, and they’re for forever wars now that Trump has started one.

Some of the non-MAGA Republicans are surely dyed-in-the-wool establishment types who may have voted for Nikki Haley or DeSantis in the primary. But a lot of them are younger, less engaged, or first-time Republicans who got on board with Trump because of his promise to lower costs and end wars. These voters aren’t moderates per se — many have very conservative views on immigration, and their economic views may be more Bernie than Romney.

But even if you dismiss the polling evidence, the intra-party criticism has longer-term consequences for Trump and the Republicans.

Trump loses his Armor

Democrats always wonder how Trump gets away with saying and doing things that would fell any other politician. It’s not as complicated as it seems. Trump doesn’t have magical powers or sell his soul to the devil (he never had a soul to sell).

Trump’s political protection racket is powered by a massive media apparatus that amplifies his message, attacks his critics, and never finds fault with anything he does. It rushes to his defense, hammers anyone who steps out of line, and helps him communicate not just with his base, but with the less overtly political voters who powered his 2024 victory.

This media apparatus lets Trump tell his story to his people on his terms and dictate the terms of the political debate. That aggressive media loyalty has been the signature feature of his political success over the last decade.

That is gone now. Trump is taking heat from some of the loudest, most influential voices on his side. It means he has to watch his flank. It means other critics will be emboldened, and negative views of Trump will reach large swaths of Americans who have tuned out traditional media.

This level of intra-party dissent also makes it harder for Trump to get the troops in line to pass difficult legislation — like the hundreds of billions in funding for the war — and will almost certainly dampen GOP turnout this fall.

This seismic shift in the MAGA media will have political consequences far beyond a short-term effect on Trump’s polling. His greatest strength has become a weakness.

Oh, and by the way — the polling impact is bigger than the pundits suggest.

Trump’s Trusted Messenger Problem

Another problem with the analysis of the MAGA media revolt is that it comes from an outdated understanding of how the media works. The assumption is that the audience for these shows is a static group of people who tune in for every episode. That’s not how the modern media ecosystem works. Even though Megyn Kelly and Tucker Carlson have relatively large audiences, their biggest impact comes through the distribution of clips on social media. Exponentially more people will see a viral moment than will ever actually tune in.

And those people aren’t just hardcore Republicans likely to dismiss the criticism. They’re independents, less engaged voters, and softer Republicans who would never download a Megyn Kelly podcast — but who are now receiving the information anyway.

For these voters, the fact that the criticism is coming from Trump’s former allies matters a lot. Many of the voters we need to move away from Trump are deeply skeptical of traditional media and immediately dismissive of anything said by a Democrat. People are looking for trusted messengers.

When the criticism comes from someone with whom they share ideological affinity, it’s far more likely to land. One clip of Tucker Carlson attacking Trump is more effective than a thousand clips of Gavin Newsom, Hakeem Jeffries, or Pod Save America making the same point.

The MAGA media revolt is more than just satisfying viewing for the anti-Trump coalition. It’s a real political problem for Trump and the Republicans.

item.title: Swalwell's Campaign Has Imploded; Here's What Comes Next
item.description: The California Governor’s race was rocked on Friday evening when the San Francisco Chronicle reportedthat an ex-staffer of Congressman Eric Swalwell had accused him of sexual assault. The claim was very credible, and the story was assiduously reported, including contemporaneous text messages.…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
The California Governor’s race was rocked on Friday evening when the San Francisco Chronicle reportedthat an ex-staffer of Congressman Eric Swalwell had accused him of sexual assault. The claim was very credible, and the story was assiduously reported, including contemporaneous text messages. Everyone should read the story itself.

This report opened up a floodgate of women talking about Swalwell’s inappropriate behavior and sexual misconduct. The allegations are horrifying, and the courage of the women who came forward should be applauded.

Swalwell has denied the allegations of sexual assault, although in a video posted on Friday night, he seems to admit to some level of infidelity.

Until this point, Swalwell was the frontrunner to be the next governor of California. He entered the race with a ton of momentum and had the support of powerful organizations like the California Teachers’ Association and the SEIU. He had also been endorsed by influential politicians like Senator Adam Schiff and former Speaker Nancy Pelosi.

Swalwell has pledged to fight on and continue his campaign.

Here’s what comes next in the race.

Swalwell’s Campaign and Career are Over

Since the allegations surfaced, most of Swalwell’s prominent supporters have rescinded their endorsement. Several Democratic leaders, including Schiff, Pelosi, and Hakeem Jeffries, have called for him to drop out of the race. Most of his campaign staff have resigned. The Manhattan District Attorney is now investigating Swalwell for sexual assault.

But whether he knows it or not, his campaign and his career is over. It is impossible for him to continue under these circumstances.

To do these things and then run for governor knowing you did these things is truly sociopathic behavior.

It’s also clear that while average Californians were shocked by the report, most political insiders were not. This is not just one allegation. It fits with a broader pattern that has been whispered about for years. The rumors have spilled into the public domain as several online influencers with close ties to Capitol Hill began intimating that Swalwell had a very bad past, including sexually propositioning young staffers. The speed with which Swalwell’s supporters moved away from him suggests they had been preparing for this shoe to drop for a long time.

Since the rumors of inappropriate behavior, sexual misconduct and infidelity seem to have been an open secret for years, it does make you wonder why so many prominent Democrats and powerful organizations backed Swalwell?

Dems Unlikely to Get Locked Out of the Top Two

California uses a jungle primary system in which the top two vote-getters, regardless of party, advance to the general election. For months now, Democrats have been concerned that they would get locked out of the top two and the general election would be a contest between two Republicans. I know that seems impossible to imagine, but California is a 60-40 Democratic state, and there are something like 9 Democrats splitting the 60 and two Republicans splitting the 40. In the vast majority of the polling to date, Republicans Steve Hilton and Chad Bianco have been leading in the polls with Swalwell, Katie Porter, and Tom Steyer coming in third to fifth in some order.

With Swalwell’s campaign over, the lockout scenario seems highly unlikely. Swalwell’s 12-15 percent will mostly allocate to the other Democratic front-runners almost certainly pushing one of them above Bianco.

Even without Swalwell’s implosion, Trump helped Democrats by randomly endorsing Steve Hilton against the wishes of every Republican in the state, including Hilton.

Who’s the Dem Frontrunner Now?

The inevitable exit of Swalwell has created a vacuum in the race. There is now a decent slice of the Democratic electorate up for grabs. To date, only Porter and Steyer have gained any real traction. Former Biden HHS Secretary Xavier Becerra has made a little noise but consistently and significantly trailed. The rest of the field has no real success to date and it’s hard to see their path forward.

Most people assume that Swalwell’s exit helps Tom Steyer the most. The prediction markets immediately had Steyer as the strong favorite to be the next governor of California. Now, I don’t put a ton of stock in prediction markets, but the idea of Steyer is consistent with what I have heard from people.

On one hand, it makes sense; he has unlimited money to spend, and I wouldn’t be surprised if some of these unions that backed Swalwell throw their support behind Steyer.

However, I wouldn’t dismiss Porter. Like Swalwell, she came to the forefront through her opposition to Trump and is a well-known figure among the Resistance/pro-democracy set.

Voting starts in a couple of weeks, and we have a whole new race for Governor. One that is better without Eric Swalwell.
